title.rendered: Episode 580: Keeping the VCP Process ALIVE

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Dawn Lyons from BNI San Francisco Bay joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about how to keep the VCP process ALIVE. ALIVE is an acronym to help people keep the actions going and moving relationships forward.

Let’s review the definition of VCP. Visibility means you know the person’s first name, last name, and company name. Credibility means you believe the person is an expert in their profession and will do what they say. Profitability results from being consistent, proactive, and reciprocal in your referrals.

These are simple definitions, but it’s often difficult for BNI members to know how their referral relationships are going. Here are steps you can follow to keep them on track.

  1. Acknowledge that you’ve received a referral from a new source.
  2. List your referral sources in a spreadsheet by name, profession, the person referred to you, the time it took to close the sale, and whether the referral was reactive or proactive.
  3. Identify where you are in the VCP process (and add it to your spreadsheet).
  4. Verify that this is in fact where you think you are in your relationship with that person.
  5. Elevate your relationship to the next level by asking your referral partner what s/he needs to move further in the VCP process.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 580 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I just got back from one of my many visits to BNI Germany. They are on fire there. They have a fantastic group of regions in Germany, and they really had a fantastic event. It is good to be back home, though.

Priscilla:
Oh good. We are glad to have you.

Ivan:
Today, I have a guest who has been on BNI Podcast at least three times. I think this is her fourth visit to BNI Podcast. Her name is Dawn Lyons. Dawn is the owner of three franchises of BNI in the San Francisco Bay Area. Her regions have 62 chapters. She has 62 chapters under her management. She has 40 Director Consultants on her team and 1,800 members as part of her organization. Dawn is in the Hall of Fame for BNI for opening 20 chapters in one calendar year. She is the only woman to ever do it, and I am really proud of her for that. There are only like four people who have ever done that and Dawn is one of them. In 2017, her members generated $132 million in thank you for closed business for each other. She is the contributing author to the New York Times Bestseller Masters of Sales and co-author of the Amazon.com bestseller A Roomful of Referrals. I heard one of your coauthors was a real pain on that one.

Dawn:
(Laughing) Well, I won’t mention any names, Ivan.

Ivan:
Okay, so I am one of her co-authors on a Roomful of Referrals. Dawn is going to talk today about the VCP process and how to keep it ALIVE. So, Dawn, the VCP process is in fact the process, but tell me what you mean by keeping it ALIVE, which is an acronym.

Dawn:
Yeah. So the one thing that I have been discovering lately, Ivan, is that people understand the VCP process. It has taken a while, there are legitimate definitions to VC and P, very simple definitions, but the catch is how are we actually moving the relationship? If something is moving, it has to be alive. So what I wanted to come up with was an acronym to help people keep the action going and moving the relationship.

Real quickly, and I know you know these but for our audience today, V stands for visibility and the definition is literally that I know the person’s first name, last name and their company name. Right? We have to make it super simple. That is the definition of visibility.

You have credibility. Once you are in visibility, then the credibility part is I believe the person to be a good expert in their profession. I believe they will do what they say. So there is this level of belief, conviction and confidence that you are going to have when you are in the credibility phase of a referral relationship.

Then P stands for profitability. We have a little acronym that we use: CPR for consistency, proactive and reciprocity. If you are consistent in your referrals, you are proactive in your referrals and it is reciprocal. Then that is what leads to a profitable relationship.

So really simple definitions, right?

Ivan:
Yes.

Dawn:
So when I ask people how are your referral relationships going, the answer is, “Well, um, they are going. Some of them are really good and some of them are really slow.” So I guess how do we move that? How do we keep it ALIVE? The acronym is literally this: the A is going to stand for acknowledge, and anytime a brand new person gives us a referral, we want to acknowledge the fact that we have received a referral from a new source. I think that is a really powerful position to be in. I think that too many times, we kind of let those first ones kind of slide through the cracks. Like, oh I wonder if it was an accident. So with acknowledgement, you are acknowledging that referral source.

The L is going to stand for list and here is where you want to actually start collecting better data. We are all in business. We know that data really can be changed. When we analyze the data, we know what is happening in our business. So I recommend to people to create some kind of spreadsheet. This depends on how technical you are. I am not all that technical, but you know, if you have the person’s name, their profession, who they referred to you, how much money generated – I also like people to have a sales cycle in there. Ivan, what I mean by that is let’s say that Susie gave you a referral and it closed in three weeks but Bobby gave you a referral and it took seven months. Now, neither of them are bad referrals. It is just wow- one took longer than the other and then understanding what that information tells you.

Now, if Susie keeps giving you referrals that are closing in three weeks, you know, you might want to take a look at how much time you are spending with her versus how much you are spending with Bobby. So it is just an idea around understanding the concept of who is bringing referrals more often and how quickly are they closing?

And then there is a column that I feel most people really, really miss. That is what I call the reactive or proactive column, and the column title is “How Was the Referral Generated?” When we look at generating referrals for somebody, if they are reactive, then the prospect brought up some idea, concern, issue or need and we reacted to that information and offered a solution. Those are great. A proactive referral is one where I may have started the conversation in order to be proactive about generating that referral for somebody that I really want to help in their business. The funny part is I ask people all the time what would you rather have. Would you rather have reactive referrals or proactive referrals? And guess what the number one answer is?

Ivan:
Proactive.

Dawn:
Yeah. Absolutely. And my answer is why not both? Right? If we can have both in our relationships, while one could be better than the other, we actually want a team of people around us that are doing both.

Ivan:
Good point. Can I just add one thing? You are talking about the Excel spreadsheet, which I think is brilliant. If you are really good at doing that kind of stuff, you should do that. For those of you listening who aren’t good at doing that kind of thing, an old common technique to use is in your existing database to put this information, whether it was in the olden days on a Rolodex or contact sheet. Today, maybe it is your Outlook contacts. If you are using a system like Salesforce, putting this information in there is so valuable because you have it right in front of you. So great suggestion.

We have a few more minutes. Tell me about the I, V and the E.

Dawn:
Alight. The I is going to stand for identify. This is where you are going to start to identify where you are in the VCP process with those relationships. That could just be an added column on your spreadsheet. The key here is that the credibility phase can take a while. You can have kind of a low credibility, medium credibility, high credibility relationship as you are moving in the process.

Then the V and the E, I think, are the action items. This is where the conversations are going to be really important. The V is going to stand for verify, so if I have identified what relationship I feel we are in, I actually need to go to the other person and verify with them. That actually means having a conversation. I don’t know if we are on our phones too much or what is happening in the world today, but that means going to the other person and having a very quick [conversation] – it is literally 90 seconds or less. When I mention this to BNI members, oh, do I have to have a full 1-2-1 to figure this out? It’s like no, this could take literally 90 seconds, so I want to give people an example what it could sound like.

I believe that if I went to someone and I said something like this: “Hey, you know how we are always talking about the VCP process in BNI and moving our relationships. I did a little verifying of my relationships and I kind of identified where I thought we were at, and Susie, we have only had one 1-2-1. I have only been a member for four months. I kind of feel like we are still in visibility. How do you feel?”

Ivan:
The truth is it doesn’t matter what you think. It matters what she thinks because it is sort of the lowest common denominator, right?

Dawn:
Yeah, definitely. So if she thinks, yeah, I totally think we are in visibility, too, then the E is going to stand for elevate, and this is the action item: “So, Susie, how do you think we could elevate it to the next level? What would you like to do next?”

She might say, “I think I need to come see your office,” or, “I think I need to understand the type of client you are really looking for,” or, “I don’t understand your packaging,” or, “Gosh, you are in IT and I am in health and wellness. I don’t understand your lingo. I need you to teach me a little more.” Whatever it is that the other person needs is going to be the movement that the relationship needs.

So literally, we do an accelerated business theories for our chapters and one of the interactive pieces of the program is to have people stand up and in 90 seconds or less, go to their fellow members and say, “I think we are in this level of relationship. What do you think and what do we do next to keep the relationship moving forward?” It is probably one of the most eye opening experiences for people because they are just not having that conversation.

Ivan:
Would you say that is where most people falter in the process, not having that conversation or is there some other area?

Dawn:
I think where most people falter is V, verifying it with the other person, and the E, the next step in the relationship. What does that look like? What information do we need to transfer between each other? Or is it just belief? Is it just getting to know you so well that I am inspired and I believe in you in order to want to help you?

Ivan:
We are about out of time, Dawn. Is there any closing comment you have before I wrap up?

Dawn:
I would just say if you know the VCP process, look at are you really keeping it ALIVE? Are you really acknowledging, getting people on that list, identifying where you are, verifying it with the other person and then asking them how to elevate the relationship? That really has to be done together. It is a two-person process.

Ivan:
Great content, Dawn. Thank you so much for being on today. I feel like I am little bit outnumbered today because I have two people from the Bay Area. Priscilla is from the Bay Area and you are from the Bay Area. It was great to have you on today and I look forward to having you back again on BNI Podcast. So over to our other Bay Area person, Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you. Just a comment that I am a chapter that Dawn manages, and it is very fun to be a part of that, so thank you, Dawn.

Dawn:
Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Well, I think that is it for this week. Thank you both so much for that great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dawn Lyons has created a process to make sure you progress from Visibility through Credibility and on to Profitability. Learn how to keep your referral relationships ALIVE.

title.rendered: Episode 579: Sub Abuse, or Is My Chapter Smaller Than It Looks?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tom Fleming from BNI West Central Florida joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about substitutes and what he calls “Effective Attendance.”

If you have a 50-member chapter and 2 people are absent, the chapter is at 96% attendance. But if there are 2 absences and 8 substitutes, there are only 40 members present, so your effective attendance is only 80%. When effective attendance drops below 90%, chapters pass 10 fewer referrals per week.

How to increase your effective attendance

Here are some suggestions for making the best use of your substitutes.

  • Remember that your chapter accepted you as a member, not your substitute
  • Remember that your BNI chapter is your best customer
  • Choose a substitute who can represent you effectively (a client is a great choice)
  • Prepare your substitute with your weekly presentation, your referral slips, your TYCB and a review of what other chapter members do in case the substitute wants to pass referrals
  • Make sure the substitute can show up on time, stay for the whole meeting, and participate

Do you have suggestions about how to tighten up the substitute program in BNI? Let us know in the comments.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete TRanscript of Episode 579 –
Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am in Poland this week. It is my second visit to Poland, and what an amazing region this is. There are a ton of members. They have a big conference going on and they invited me to come speak. So I have a translator and I am talking about BNI.

Priscilla:
That’s great.

Ivan:
So today I have a guest on the show who has been on, I counted four times. Tom Fleming has been on the BNI Podcast, so this will make his fifth appearance on BNI Pocast. That tells you something about my opinion of his content. Tom is a really strong BNI Director. He has been a BNI member since 1996. He served as the Executive Director of BNI West Border Region since 2003 and his commentary on this topic today is really a result of his attending thousand of BNI meetings through the years and analyzing the statistics of BNI chapters over the last two decades.

Tom, first of all, welcome to BNI Podcast again, Tom. It is great having you on.

Tom:
Yank you so much. Wow. Podcast number 579. Kudos to you, Dr. Misner.

Ivan:
It’s all the gray hair, you know, the longer you do this, the more gray hair you get, I think. You have a great topic. It is a topic that we have talked about a few times on the podcast, and my frustration with what I really felt was a good idea – and that is to allow people to have substitutes. But it is not working out quite as I had hoped because it has been abused a bit. That is really what you are talking about in this and I love the way you are framing this: Is the chapter smaller than it looks?
In reading your notes, you were talking about effective attendance. What is the concept of effective attendance?

Tom:
We have been keeping our eye on this the past few years, especially during the summertime. By the way, I think the substitute concept is a good concept. It is just when it is abused. Effective attendance, just for easy numbers, let’s say we are a member of a 50-member chapter. If we have two people absent that day, we are at 96% attendance. But if you have two people absent and eight substitutes at the meeting that day, now we have 40 truly contributing members of 50 and our attendance rate is down at 80%.

So effective attendance, as we define it, takes and treats your substitutes as absences.

Ivan:
Okay. So you are saying what your effective attendance is, so you have some chapters that are really looking at effective attendance? Is that what you are doing or are you just talking about this conceptually?

Tom:
Our entire organization looks at effective attendance because what we have noticed is members so a good job of training their substitutes. However, many members don’t when they line up their substitutes. They line their substitute up to do their weekly presentation and that is about it. Often, you will hear a sub say, “Oh, Sally will catch up with your guys in terms of her referrals or thank you for closed business next week.”

Ivan:
Yeah, that is not the same as being there, is it?

Tom:
No, not at all, to the point where, you know last chapter back here quite a few years ago and discovered that the effective absenteeism was less than 90% on a weekly basis on the weeks that effective attendance was less than 90% versus when effective attendance was above 90%, on average the chapter passed ten less referrals per week. That’s 520 referrals for the year. The average revenue per referral here in our region is about $1300. That’s a lot of thank you for closed business that these people possibly miss out on. Is a substitute bad? No. Is substitute abuse bad? Yes.

Ivan:
I agree complete and you have probably seen it where there are in some chapters what they call a professional substitute. He or she is just available to sub for anyone and they are not prepped and they really don’t know the person. They are basically a bookmark in the chapter. That to me is the poster child for abusing what I felt was a good idea but has turned into something that has worked out in a way that I hadn’t hoped. I hoped it wouldn’t work out this way. So what are your suggestions?

Tom:
I think chapters’ membership committees want to realize the implications of people who are maybe consistently sending subs or sending subs a little too often and explaining those implications to the members and the fact that, hey, we are all losing out due to this, and of course, people are allowed to send a substitute for vacations or business conferences and so on and do forth. But the point of the matter is that we accepted you as the member not your substitute to be here twice a month or three times a month.

Ivan:
That’s crazy. That is completely unacceptable.

Tom:
It happens. You know, take the accountants during tax season. All of a sudden, you know, he or she is not there for a month and they have substitutes. I think that is a great opportunity and time of year for an accountant to be present at the meeting every week.

Ivan:
Right. Right. You know, I have seen subs over the years that were done right. Oftentimes, it is a friend of the member or what I really love is when a client of the member comes in, and they represent the member and just knock it out of the park. I have seen it, and it works well, where somebody represents that member well. They talk about what a great service provider they are and how much they like working with them. Then they bring referrals. I have even been to chapter meetings where, “I have a referral for the person I am subbing for but I also have a referral for myself.” I have seen that. I watch that and I go, okay, this is what the substitute program is supposed to be.

Tom:
Absolutely and I think it is just a matter of educating members of the value of proper substitutes, which is exactly why we are doing this podcast here and how the substitute program was designed to be effective and maximize the member experience and people’s productive versus hurt the situation.

Ivan:
I know it sounds like there is more to prepping a substitute if you are not getting them the weekly presentation. What suggestions do you have?

Tom:
I would advise sort of substitute on the roster and have them start to think about what products and services they may need in their own personal life to the friends and acquaintances they need, I certainly want to wind a substitute up with my weekly presentation. As suggested, have the substitute do a testimonial for me if they are an existing client. Then, let’s make sure that substitute is ready and they contribute at the end of the meeting with the referrals that I have generated and the Thank you for Closed Business that I have generated rather than viewing- I do believe that when some members view, when they know that they are going to have a substitute it’s a vacation week for them from BNI. I am not going to be there next week,so I do not have to produce anything. Ugh. I just don’t know what word to choose to say that we are just missing opportunity there.

Ivan:
So, for the members that are listening, let’s give them some actionable suggestions on not abusing the substitute program but using it effectively. What are tow or three suggestions that you might have? I certainly have one or two. What suggestions do you have?

Tom:
First and foremost, realize that the BNI chapter accepted you as the member in the chapter and not your substitute.

Ivan:
And not of the things that I think you said was preparing the substitutes. I think prepping the substitute includes what are you going to talk about and if they are a client, hey, would you just mind giving a testimonial for how it is working with me? Or if they are not prepared to do something like that, say here’s the topic I would like you to discuss on my behalf, if you would, please, and give them some information. What else would you recommend to pre p the substitute to come on?

Tom:
Certainly, being there on time and staying the entire meeting. We have had substitutes who were kind of like, I am just substituting, so it doesn’t matter if I pop in a little late or if I leave after giving the member’s weekly presentation. You know, your sub is a reflection of you and you want to position your substitute to maximize your credibility as a member of the chapter.

Ivan:
Right. Another thing is to actually physically give them the referral slips if you have done some or at the very least, give them the details of the referrals that you may have. You know, I passed two referrals this week. Would you share this information during the referral part of the meeting so everybody knows? Now, we do that kind of thing and that makes it work.

My concern is that people, like water, tend to seek the path of least resistance. Then the chapter accepts that reasonably poor performance because they are just trying to do the letter of the law as opposed to the spirit of the law, which is to have somebody truly represent them. Anything you would add, tom, before we wrap up?

Tom:
I just want to make note of the fact that BNI allows three absences in a rolling six month period. The substitute guideline reads that members should be wary of sending substitutes more than three times during that six month period. So that is six times in the 26 week period that people are available to not be at the meeting. That is a 75% effective attendance rate. That means a member is not present at the meetings for one out of four meetings a month, and I don’t think that does the members visibility and credibility too much good because unfortunately, we are all in the world of out of sight is out of mind.

Ivan:
Right. And that means your chapter is smaller than it looks.

Tom:
Yeah, yeah.

Ivan:
Well, listen, let me just add one thing and then I am going to turn it back over to Priscilla. The International Board of Advisors is looking at the substitute program now and it is very likely that sometime in the not too distant future, we are going to have a revision to this substitute program to help address some of these abuse issues.

I love your suggestions. If you are listening to this and you have some suggestions on how to tighten up the sub program in BNI, this is a place to do it. Write down your comments here on BNI Podcast. Tom Fleming, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast yet again. I appreciate you, Tom, thanks.

Tom:
My pleasure.

Ivan:
Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Great. I think that is it for this week. Thank you so much for the great infromation. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.at really is.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Low effective attendance (too many substitutes at a BNI meeting) results in many fewer referrals. If you have to miss a meeting, make sure your substitute is prepared with your referrals and TYCB as well as your weekly presentation.

title.rendered: Episode 578: The Referral That Keeps on Giving (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 506.

Synopsis

Tiffanie Kellog joins Dr. Misner on the podcast today to talk about a different kind of referral: referrals to referral sources in your contact sphere.

People in your contact sphere serve the same clients you do, but are not competitors. These are the people most likely to be able to pass you quality referrals. A referral to a contact sphere relationship can be more valuable than a referral to a single prospect over time. Once you go through the VCP process with this new person, s/he will introduce you to many qualified prospects.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 506 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, featuring Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Austria. Vienna, Austria this week at a BNI German-speaking conference. I have been here in Germany and Austria many times. They have some amazing members and Directors here, so I am really excited to be back.

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
So, today Priscilla, we have a guest someone who, I think we can say it’s official. She has been a guest on the BNI podcast more than any guest ever. That is Tiffanie Kellog. Tiffanie is a trainer with Ascentive in Tampla, Florida. She is a speaker and an author. She has done two books: The Four and a Half Networking Mistakes. I absolutely love that title. And Tiffanie has been on my podcast talking about that book, so if you are intrigued by that title, type “four and a half” in the BNI Podcast search engine and you can see my interview.

She is also the author of a book called Knock the Socks Off Your Audience. That is s title that is really meaningful because Tiffanie has more crazy colored socks than anybody I know. That is sort of your thing, isn’t it, Tiffanie? Colored socks and – ?

Tiffanie:
Yeah. The crazier and more colorful the better.

Ivan:
So I don’t know if we talked about this last time. I think we did off-air. I don’t know what got into me but I had seen all these socks that she always has and I took a photograph of my sock drawer, which you have to understand is like all black socks with just one white sock. I sent it to Tiffanie and then after I sent it, I thought, this is actually kind of creepy of me to be sending her a picture of my black socks.

But Tiffanie, thank you, you didn’t take it wrong.

Tiffanie:
No, I love pictures of socks. Anytime people see socks, sending me photos is totally fine.

Ivan:
Well good. Listen, it is great having you on again and we are going to talk about a topic that I don’t think we have ever talked about. I don’t think I have ever talked about it on any of my- we have never talked about it on the podcast and I have never talked about it in my presentation. That is really an interesting perspective on referrals.

First question: are there other things you can have as a referral other than a referral to a specific prospect? I know the answer is yes. Explain it.

Tiffanie:
Yeah, so we are so used to in BNI “Here is the client I am looking for. Here is the prospect that I am looking for.” I think that is powerful, though we are missing an opportunity because for most people, we have gaps in our network for professionals that if we can meet more of a certain profession, they would be able to refer us on a consistent basis.

So I think that referrals to a referral source specifically a contact sphere relationship can be very powerful. So I definitely count it as a referral to the referral source.

Ivan:
Just so everyone knows, a contact sphere – I have talked about it in a number of my books. It is a group of business professionals that have a symbiotic relationship. They are compatible, non-competitive professions. It is that group of people that becomes a power team. So a contact sphere is all the potential people that are in non-competitive professions, your power team, the people that are actually in those professions you have a relationship with.

So what we are talking about in effect is a referral to someone who is likely to be in your contact sphere and is a referral source. Is that right?

Tiffanie:
That is correct.

Ivan:
I am going to ask you for an example for people to hear but let me lay it up with my next question, which is what is the value of a contact sphere relationship like what I am talking about? And maybe you can give an example.

Tiffanie:
I think the value of a contact sphere relationship – you know, contact sphere relationships with people who have the same target market as you and they are not competitive. So your ideal client is also the ideal client for the contact sphere relationship and you are able to pass referrals back and forth all day everyday.

This flow of referrals can be so powerful because what I see a lot of time in BNI is you are getting one referral from this member, two referrals from that member. You get a couple here and there, but the value of the contact sphere relationship is how do you create that flow of referrals?

A really great example of a contact sphere relationship that paired up – we have a woman who sells and does video marketing. She is actually partners with a website design company because one of the things that the website search engines love is that video. She thought that somebody who is getting a new website should probably have some new video to go along with that.

Now, they have taken it to the next level. The website designer actually bundles those packages that he offered to include the videos for her. So when he gets paid for the website design, it includes the videos that she is going to do. So he essentially when he has a referral for her, it is “here is the referral and here is the payment already collected on your behalf.”

Ivan:
I like it. So we don’t think about that too often. I was thinking about that because you were telling me about this before we went on the podcast, about some that I have run across. Just a really big one I ran across a number of years ago was a member who needed building managers for new buildings that were going up because they could refer him. He did telecommunications work and they could refer him for him to do the phone equipment.

Who would have thought? He said, “Look, I don’t want to sell anything to them. They are not my prospects. They are a referral source for me.” So he got all kind of referrals for people that were a referral source for him. They managed the building and when someone new would move in, they would refer him. He got a ton of business from them and I thought it was a brilliant and under utilized idea.

Tiffanie:
I also use the analogy – we have all heard if you give a man a fish, he eats today but if you teach a man to fish, he can eat for life. I think it fits. If you give me a referral to a prospect, great, you know, I have business today. If you give me a referral to a contact sphere relationship, we are creating referrals for life.

Ivan:
Right. One last question. I think we kind of already know the answer to this but I would like you to expand on it. That is what will it take to make that referral to the referral source profitable for a BNI member?

Tiffanie:
So that is kind of a thing BNI members look at. “Oh, I just got a referral slip or I got the referral for BNI Connect and it is an introduction to a contact sphere. While you will typically close the prospect hopefully within a couple weeks or a couple months and than you for closed business, it typically does take longer to begin to se ether referrals come in from the referral source.

That’s because you have to go through the VCP process. You know, just because I am able to refer you to someone who can pas you referrals, it doesn’t mean that the referrals are gong to happen today. Just like you do with any members in your chapter, you will have to get to know the people, to like them so you can create that referral relationship- that profitability to get those referrals in.

It oftentimes may take a little bit longer to begin to see the money so you can do the thank you for closed business but the hope is once you develop that possible relationship, you should see a flow of referrals from that person and you get to thank your fellow member who introduced you to that contact sphere relationship.

We have a general contractor here who is in the process of bidding on a NFL, a professional football player’s project. This could be a very lucrative half a million dollar deal. It came from that process. The original referral was actually and introduction to a contact sphere relationship- and interior designer- that then led to this.

So it is another step or two, but there is huge opportunity and potential for us to meet those contact sphere relationships outside of our chapter.

Ivan:
I think it is really cute that you kind of hesitated as to whether the NFL was professional football.

Tiffanie:
Yeah. I am like NHL, NFL. Sports are- I am good with basketball, but anything easel is not my-

Ivan:
Listen, this is great advice, Tiffanie, and I appreciate you spending time sharing it with everyone.

What you have learned here today on this podcast if you are listening is sort of networking 401. This isn’t the basic stuff. This is a little more advanced. So think about referrals that will help you as a referral source and then ask your fellow members and then ask your fellow members about potential referrals for them that would be a referral source for them as opposed to a prospect.

So we have had on our podcast here today Tiffanie Kellog. She is a trainer with Ascentive in Tampa, Florida. She helps people create an amazing business and spectacular life. I mean this. There are very few people that I would refer others to, to be trained in networking. You are one of them. You are really good at this and I appreciate you being on my podcast.

Tiffanie:
Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan:
Thanks. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay well, that is great. Thank you so much for all that great information. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Referrals to prospects are great–but referrals to referral sources can be even more valuable in the long run. Tiffanie Kellog joins Dr. Misner to talk about contact spheres in BNI.

title.rendered: Episode 577: I Wasn’t Referable!

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Meaghan Chitwood, Director of Global Training at BNI,  joins Dr. Misner to talk about being referable.

When Meaghan first joined a BNI chapter in Alabama, she’d just come out of the construction industry. Although she was now working as a business coach, she was still dressing to fit in on a construction site. She was focused on establishing credibility by knowing what she was talking about.

One day, two of her fellow BNI members came to her and said “We want to help you, because right now you’re just not referable.”

Despite her dismay, Meaghan responded “Okay, so what are we going to do about it?”

They explained that they believed she knew what she was doing, but the first impression people had of her was terrible. She didn’t look the part of a successful business coach. They connected her with other BNI members who helped her with her hair, makeup, and wardrobe.

Four months later, a fellow member invested the money to help Meaghan buy the BNI franchise and become his business parter.

If there’s someone in your chapter who isn’t referable and you want to help, you can try saying “I really want to help you, and I have some suggestions on how you can be more referable.”

If you get feedback from your chapter members tell you that you’re not referable, take a breath, ask yourself what part of it is true and what part of it you want to work on. That puts you in growth mode.

If you’re open to feedback, you can achieve your dream referral. Always give feedback in a kind and compassionate way.

Complete Transcript of Episode 577 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am fantastic, Pricailla, and I am at home this week. I am in Austin, TX, looking at the beautiful lake and having the chance to talk to ou you and my guest today is Meaghan Chitwood. I love Meaghan. She is a fantastic BNI Director. She joined BNI in 2003 in the San Francisco Bay Area. Soon after she joined, her business really took off and allowed her to move back here she in now in Northern Alabama to be closer to her family. Within seven months of being back in Alabama, Meaghan became the Executive Director of Alabama North. She has been an Executive Director now for 14 years. Two years ago, she joined the Global Support Team at BNI Headquarters. She in involved with BNI Headquarters, although she is still working out of Alabama. She is the Driector of Global Training. She wouldn’t be where she is if she wasn’t open to feedback and willing to take action on the feedback, which is the topic that you are going to talk about today. THis is your first visit to BNI Podcast. Welcome, Meaghan.

Meaghan:
It is! Thank you so much I appreciate you having me.

Ivan:
I love this story that you are going to share. I think it is so powerful and every member needs to hear this, from both perspectives. Your perspective at the time that it happened as well as the perspective of the people that you were talking to. I think it is powerful perspective. You have a very specific story to share about being referable. The floor is yours. The podcast is yours.

Meaghan:
Thank you so much. It was funny. I told this story a couple of years after it happened, and as I was telling the story, it felt like fiction, so it’s very interesting to me how this whole thing happened. I came back to Alabama and I was a member of a chapter in Northern Alabama, and I had just come out of the construction project management industry. I was a young woman in the construction project management industry, so I didn’t dress very well because I specifically did not want to stand out at the constructions sites. My entire wardrobe was collared shirts and khaki pants and I at that point had become a business coach.

Again, I was young and I thought to myself- I always thought credibility came from do you know what you are talking about, are you effective, and things like that. That’s what I was focused on, was making sure I was a really good business coach and I could effectively communicate that.

One day, two ladies, Kerry and Sheery, came to me. They were in my chapter, and they came to me and sat me down and thinking back on it, I felt a little bit cornered now that hindsight is 20/20. They said, “Meaghan, we want to help you because right now, you are just not referable.” At the moment that I heard it, it kind of took my breath away a little bit. It was a little bit shocking because I was like, “What do you mean? I know what I am talking about. I know what I am doing. I worked really hard to be here. I am working really hard everyday.”

Luckily in that moment, I had a moment of wisdom and clarity. I looked at them, leaned forward and said, “Okay, so what are we going to do about it?” Because I wasn’t going to let them off the hook. This was “we”. I was like you are going to drop this bomb on me and you can’t like turn for the hills. You have to help me out here.

Ivan:
Did you ask them why? Did they share with you why?

Meaghan:
I did. That’s what happened. When I said what are we going to do about it, I don’t really know what you are talking about. That is when they said, “We do believe that you are really good at what you do. We have seen you do it. We have experienced you doing it. We believe you are really good at what you do. The problem is you are young, you’re a woman and when you show up, your first impression is pretty terrible.”

I said “Okay.” I come from a household where my mother washed her face with water. She just splashed water on her face. There were no cosmetics in that house, no hairdressing on a regular basis. it was 100% natural. So I literally had no idea what to do. So they took me under their wing and they got me hooked up with a Mary Kay consultant in our chapter. They got me a hair appointment with Terrie’s hair dresser. Terrie let me go shopping in her closet. I had a chance to actually go shopping.

Ivan:
You looked the part of a successful business coach.

Meaghan:
Totally. 100%. That was the only thing that I needed to do, was look the part.

Ivan:
A lot of people have that issue in one way or another. When I started BNI, I didn’t have the gender concer, but I had the young concern. I was a management consultant. I was 27 or 28 and I had to get past that. One way I got past it by the way was being the president of a chapter, being on the leadership team, where people could see me behave. They could see how I managed. And that gave them more confidence.

I think the point here is you have to find what works for you and work on that. These two ladies helped you

Meaghan:
100%

Ivan:
It’s important. They did it from a position of caring. They weren’t just trying to be mean. They weren’t trying to put you down, and they did in in, as you said, a loving, caring way.

So if you are in that position, you want to make to sure to do it like these ladies did it and show that they care about this person and not slam them.

Meaghan:
Yeah, I wish they would have kind of said, “Hey Meaghan, we noticed a couple of things” and kind of eased into it. I think they did jump into it quite quickly.

Ivan:
Too directly?

Meaghan:
No, it wasn’t too directly, looking back. I just remember being stunned. But I knew they cared. I think that was the most important part, is that the person has to know you care before you can give them any constructive feedback. I knew they a cared.

Ivan:
You knew they cared. That is good. People don’t care how much you know until they know how much you care. It is so important. You think it would be better to say, “Are you open to a suggestion?” I like saying that and getting persmission because people always say, “Yeah.”

Meaghan:
The other ones I love are , ” I really want to be able to help you and I am having a challenge.”

Ivan:
That’s great.

Meaghan:
Yeah, because then okay, I am all ears if you really want to help me.

Ivan:
“I have some suggestions on how you can be more referable.”

Meaghan.
Yeah, yeah.

Ivan:
That may be a better way to do it that what these ladies did, but they did it from a position of caring, obviously.

Meaghan:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
So how do you recommend people receive feedback?

Meaghan:
I always recommend that – sometimes I get feedback and I am like You are so wrong and I walk around and I take a walk. And then I ask myself the question ,”What part of this is true?” We hear it a lot. Perception is reality. If somebody has the perception of that, what part of it is true? How could I be showing up that way? It’s only helpful, and I would say if you are not in growth, you are in decline.

So you are getting feedback from people all over the place, and even if it is no feedback, sometimes you are getting feedback or you are getting something- it is still feedback. Taking that feedback and really implementing it and thinking through, what part of this is true? Do I want to work on that or don’t I want to work on that? And then, moving forward. You are always in growth mode.

Ivan:
Yeah, and sometimes the feedback is not necessarily about you. It is something that you are working on. And I can tell you that you are passionate about developing a culture of feedback. I see that. You are even every open to frantic feedback from a Founder who calls you up and says, “Hey, we have this problem.” Does that reign any bells for you at all?

Meaghan:
I think that happened this morning, didn’t it?

Ivan:
It did. It happened today. You got a frantic phone call from me saying, “Oh my goodness. How could we be doing this?” And what I love about you, Meaghan, is that you were very calm and you were like, “Okay, here is the situation and here is where we are really going. How do you feel about that?” I was like, ” I feel much better now.”

Meaghan:
Also, it was one of those things that I had identified- this is a paint point. This is causing a small pain point, so we will get it fixed and we’ll get it handled. If I can do something and make small adjustments and make people happy, I love it. Even if it is small adjustments with me. I am happy to do it. It’s just let me know what it is because sometimesI have no idea I am driving you nuts and you just have to tell me and I go, “Oh, I can change that. That’s easy.” Or, “I had no idea.”

Ivan:
Right. It doesn’t relate to the issue this morning, but you were incredibly receptive to my concerns and you walked the talk, is what I am trying to say.

So for members, we are almost out of time and this is such an important question for you: What if all chapters, regions and countries were open to feedback? What if everyone was willing to get feedback? What would be possible?

Meaghan:
Anything is possible. Your dream is possible. Your next contact that you are looking for, the golden goose, the dream referral. even if you are not currently prepared for that referral that will change your life, if you are open to feedback, you will be because kind of the rest of the story with Terry and Terrie was four months later, Book Crook, who became my business partner, invested the money for me to buy the BNI franchise. I would not have been able to do it had it not been for Bob seeing that I could take feedback, I could radically implement it quickly and he wanted me as a partner.

So imagine that something like that can be just behind something as simple as adjust the way you look, adjust a little bit your first impression, is amazing. I just think everyone could reach their dreams.

Ivan:
So we are over time, but I am going to take it a little further because I want to get just a little bit more on what are your suggestions on receiving and giving feedback? Both sides of it, something short.

Meaghan: Come from a place of loving. It is giving. Give feedback in a kind and compassionate way. Be open to taking the feedback and processing it, whether you like it or not. Process it and I assure you – there is nothing special about me. I just happen to take feedback and implement it as much as possible. If you can do that, you can do anything that you want in your life.

Ivan:
Meaghan, this is great content and great information for the BNI chapters. We want them to give each other feedback in a living and a caring way, but that feedback helps members be better members. This six great first podcast for you, Megan, and I invite you back on any topic anytime to the podcast. I appreciate you being on.

Meaghan:
Thank you so much. I appreciate the opportunity, and I hope it helps someone.

Ivan:
Yep, I think it will. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Thank you both for some great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to Ivan Misner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Meaghan Chitwood joins Dr. Misner to talk about how being open to feedback makes you more referable, and how to give feedback to help your fellow BNI members become more referable.

title.rendered: Episode 576: The Hardest Year of Membership

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Vickie Wacek, opera singer and executive director of BNI Vermont, joins Dr. Misner on the show this week to talk about why the first year in BNI is the hardest year of membership. The key to getting through that year successfully is setting expectations.

Why the First Year Is the Hardest Year of BNI Membership

  1. New members don’t know how to train referral partners. It takes repetition, feedback, and focus to perfect 1-minute and 10-mintue presentations.
  2. It takes time to build trust with other members. Long-time members need to see that you’re going to stick around and show up before they refer business to you. The Member Success Program teaches you how to do that.
  3. It takes a minimum of one year to start getting results from BNI. New members are often disappointed when they don’t see results after putting in so much time, but relationships take repetition to get results. You don’t expect oranges the first year you plant a tree.

Share this podcast with the new members in your BNI chapter and encourage them to stick around for a second year.

Complete Transcript of Episode 576 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic and I have a guest on the podcast, Vickie Wacek. She has been involved in BNI since she was 24 years old. She started as a chapter member of one of the Burlington, Vermont chapters, The Champlain Connections, I think. Since 2012, she has been the Executive Director of BNI Vermont and I love this – she has taken the average membership of under $8000 per member each seat to over $36,000 per seat a year. When not leading and loving BNI, Vickie is a professional opera singer. I am going to come back to that. A teacher as well as a competitive ultimate frisbee player – that would be interesting- and an accordion trumpet player and an avid reader. That’s a lot, there, Vickie, but I want to talk about the opera singer thing.

Absolutely true story. Vickie came out at the last BNI conference. She stood up, and oh my goodness. From this little person came out this amazing opera voice. Now, I was sitting way back and I heard you start to belt this song. I didn’t hear the introduction. I turned to my wife and David, “That’s not really Vickie Wacek is it? Yeah, that’s Vickie.” You have an amazing, amazing voice. Powerhouse voice. From such a small person, I am really impressed.

Welcome to my podcast:

Vickie:
Good use of being an opera singer- you can make a lot of noise without a microphone.

Ivan:
Yes, you can. And you said something before we went on air about a little person with a big voice and how that applies to BNI. You said something about BNI to be sure.

Vickie:
Ivan, you are not the first person to say, “You are so tiny. How do you make so much sound?” I have been teaching people to sing opera for almond 17 years now, and I have several degrees in opera singing. Size does not matter when it comes to vocal chords. The chords do not care how big the body is. You could be 4’5″ and weigh 70 pounds and make a huge sound. Size or weight doesn’t matter, so yeah, I like to brag about that. You can be tiny and loud.

Ivan:
Alright. We spent a lot of time on your bio, and you have such an important topic. I want to make sure we have time to talk about this. It’s really about the first year of membership in BNI. You call that the hardest year. Talk about that if you will.

Vickie:
I absolutely would. If you think about really setting expectations and there is hardened sphere of membership, not – every individual has their own experiences. Life may throw things at them, but when it comes to the timeline of experience and results at BNI, anyone who has made it into their second, fifth or seventeenth year of BNI membership is going to look back and say it was that first year that was the most difficult.

I have some pretty impressive reasons why. I like to really drill these with our new members. I like to set the expectations, and here is what I have got:

The number one reason why the first year of membership is the most difficult is new members don’t know how to train and especially even if they do have experience in training others, they don’t know how to do it in one minute. Or they don’t know how to do it in ten minutes. Or they don’t know how to do it through one-to-one human interaction. So that is just one of the pieces that makes the hardest year the first year- is the fact that it takes repetition, feedback, focus. A new member needs to spend that first year kind of saying, “Hey, did that land? Does that make sense? Did my intention translate correctly?” So just presentation skills. The first year is making mistake after mistake after mistake for 50 weeks and the second year really piecing together what got results in terms of presentation.

Ivan:
When you say train, you are talking about the new member teaching existing members how to refer them and how to refer them and how to talk about business. I couldn’t agree more. I am a professional speaker and I do the things that you speak about. I try out new content. It always starts at my wife. I run it by her first. She is my first audience. Then I have to try it with other people and test it out and fix parts of my presentation. It’s really what happens in the first year of membership, talking about your business. You have to tinker with your message until you get a message that is working really well. I think that is what you are talking about.

Vickie:
Absolutely. New members really need to be patient, not only with themselves and their ability to train but the members’ ability to understand them. That is that first year.

Ivan:
So your second topic is about building trust. Talk about that.

Vickie:
Yeah, you know, I live in Vermont where decades ago, there were more cows than humans but now there are more humans than cows. I make the joke that we, in BNI, are not cattle. We don’t just lie behind the milk room and wait for referrals. We are human beings- trust is how you get us to produce for you.

Think about the referrals process here. Once I trust you, I will then produce referrals for you. Because we are not cattle that just stamp up, you treat us the right way and we produce for you, trust is an ever-endeavor in experience. Each individual member- you are going to have to do different things with each person to get trust. One person, if you just show up on time, do weekly presentations and be an advocate for your profession, they will refer you. Another person, if you are just nice to them and you connect with people, they’ll refer you. Some people, it doesn’t matter how nice or professional you are, they need to see your reputation for a year and sometimes more before they are going to put their reputation on the line because they need to see you show up for a year plus worth of meetings on time and your 1-2-1 and featured presentations.

So trust is the second piece. I am not necessarily going to trust you in one year, and if I have been a member of my chapter for a while, chances are I have seen a number of you new folks come and go. You come here one year and you leave the next year. Why am I going to put any effort into in your first year if I have been in my group for 10 years and most of you just leave after one year. It’s not worth my time until I notice that you are going to stay with me and my business for a long time.

Ivan:
One of the things that hat we have discovered really taking a look at the data is that the new members is that first year member who does not go to members success program training – now, some of you are thinking what, that is optional? No you really need to go. It is required but some people still don’t go. The non-reward rate of people so do not go to MSP is substantially higher than those who do go to member success program training because when you go to the training, you learn how to do the things that Vickie is talking about better , particularly teaching people how to refer you and you are much more likely to be successful so the turnover rate is much higher for people who don’t bother to do that member success program. We talk about that time confidence curve in the training.

Vickie:
I hope you don’t mind, but in Vermont. we don’t have that problem. We have problems with people not attending in their first two months, but we do have every member through the member success program. I think that is one of the benefits of my smaller region.

“I know your name because you haven’t been:” They might hear me say that.

Ivan:
Well, listen, those regions that are tough on that topic in those chapters are more successful. There is no question about it. That is one of the reasons that your region has gone from $8,000 to 36, 000 per seat. So the next talking point you have is about time and effort during the first year. Do you want to talk about that a little?

Vickie:
Yeah. I tell our new members all the time that is it very normal after the first year of membership just to reflect on all of the time that you spent in the coup and all the effort you put in, then to reflect on all the income generated that first year and then typically new members experience disappointment. You know, I spent a 150, 200, 300 hours in my last year of membership and maybe I generated whatever the number is in the first year of membership. They say this doesn’t work and that is where I reflect on the first thing I said about learning to train us in understanding you and the second point of trust takes time. It is to be expected that at the end of 365 days, you have not gotten what you expected to get out of your BNI membership because we are not cattle to be led and you need to learn how to get in our heads.

New members need to know how to wait – it is a minimum one year to start getting results. I know, Ivan, you have examples. I have so many examples of members who called me at their one year renewal and I said, “Give me that second year.” If I have that relationship, I will say give me that second year, because every single time, I am so glad that I listened to you.

Priscilla:
I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Vickie Wacek, opera singer and executive director of BNI Vermont, joins Dr. Misner on the show this week to talk about why the first year is the hardest year of BNI membership.

title.rendered: Episode 575: How to Become a Key Person of Influence

content.rendered:
Note 2021-03-07: The Zigsecrets.com offer is no longer available, but you can buy the updated version of Secrets of Closing the Sale on Amazon.

Synopsis

Kevin Harrington, one of the original “sharks” on Shark Tank, joins Dr. Misner on the show today to talk about how to become the go-to person in an industry.

When Dr. Misner started BNI, he wanted to be the go-to person–the person everyone thought of first. To become a key person of influence, you have to put yourself out there and build your personal brand. People need to see your name everywhere and know what you’ve accomplished. Publishing a book is a key part of this process, as are speaking engagements, press releases, social media–and your personal network.

If you want to take your business to the next level, you need a dream team of experts. Your BNI chapter is part of that dream team.

Kevin has partnered with the Zig Ziglar’s family to produce a free video series called Zig Secrets. If you go to zigsecrets.com, you can download a free cheat sheet with the secret 3-part process that Kevin has used in every successful sale to generate more than $5 billion.

Complete Transcript of Episode 575

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you??

Ivan:
I am in Charlotte at their global support this week, doing some presentations here at BNI. I love doing it.

Priscilla:
That’s great.

Ivan:
So, we have a special guest on the podcast today. He is an original on Shark Tank, the hit TV show in the United States. They have similar shows around the world. He is the creator of the infomercial. He is a pioneer of the As Seen on TV Brand and he is the co-founding Board Member of The Entrepreneurs Organization. That is Kevin Harrington. Kevin has pushed past all of the questions and excuses to enjoy immense success. His legendary work behind the scenes of business ventures has produced well over 5 billion dollars in global sales. He has launched more than 500 products, making a lot of people millionaires. 20 of his companies have topped 100 million dollars in revenue for their business. He is my guest today on BNI Podcast, Kevin Harrington. Kevin, welcome to the show.

Kevin:
Hey, thanks for than introduction. It is great to be here. It’s great to hang out and talk a little bit of business and entrepreneurship today. It’s exciting. Thanks for having me.

Ivan:
It’s fantastic to have you on. I am a fan of Shark Tank, I enjoyed watching you on the show. It’s a show that has done some amazing things. One of the things that you wanted to talk about today, which I think is fantastic, is how to become the go-to person in an industry. I think this is so important and it is what I wanted to do when I started BNI. I wanted to be the go-to person. It’s one of the reasons that I started BNI. So talk to my members about that a little.

Kevin:
I’ll go back. Pre-Shark Tank. I was an entrepreneur for 40 years, and I would build a business in a market and always try to become the number one in the business if I could. If I was in the heating business, I wanted people to think of me when they needed a furnace or air conditioning system. That was way back when I was in college.

But then, as I got into the infomercial As Seen on TV space, when somebody cam out with a new gadget, I wanted to get that phone call so that they would come to me before anybody else. So what I had to start doing is I started this system of becoming a KPI, a key person of influence.

It is sort of a step by step system and it starts by building your personal brand, building your business brand. That means you need to get yourself out. You need to start going to trade shows. You need to start speaking. You need to start putting out press releases about closing the deals and the people that you are doing business with and raising your profile.

I started doing all of that, and this was the breakthrough- one of the things that I did was I published a book. My first book was Act Now: How to Turn Ideas into Million Dollar Products. As a promotion for this book, I was doing radio talk shows, newspaper interviews, and TV interviews. So this was all part of a prearranged plan that I had, which was building a brand, raising. A profile, writing a book, publishing, getting myself out there, speaking, and creating content- this system of Beijing a KPI. Kind of productizing myself almost. I would be giving away free chapters of the book when I went on radio. So I was promoting the book, giving away a free chapter- this is productizing myself and my systems that I am doing.

Little did I know, but this was all done the year that Mark Burnett was actually looking for sharks for his show. So when I first met with Mark, he called me and said, “Kevin, I need to talk to you about this new show that I am doing.”

I said, “How did you get me, Mark? I am curious.”

He said, “Well, I saw you. I heard your radio spot. I saw your content, the videos you were putting out. You were on TV. You were in press releases. You were everywhere I turned on the internet or somewhere, I was running into you doing business as a product guy, helping people build million dollar plus businesses. So I had to meet you to see if this could be right for me.”

So I believe that my plan to become a KPI put me in the motion that got me the phone call to be a shark on the show and took me to the next level across the board.

Ivan:
Right. I think the lesson for the BNI members out there, the business out there where the rubber meets the road and they are trying to generate their businesses, is to get out there. Be visible. Get connected. Build yourself as a brand as you are describing, which is what I was trying to do when I started BNI. But the one things I added to that was be a connector. When somebody needs something, you want to be the guy that people go to to say, “Hey, where can I get this? Do you know a good lawyer in the area? Do you know a travel agent?” If you are that person that people are going to, then you become a connector and that is kind of what led to the building of BNI.

I couldn’t agree more and I think this is great advice for my BNI members. I have a question for you about Shark Tank. For those of you. Who are listening – because we have, you know, members in 73 countries- if you are not familiar with Shark Tank, it is a show where businesses come in and they pitch their business to investors. Kevin was one of the investors. He was one of the original sharks on Shark Tank. They may or may not get a deal on the show. In some countries, I think it is called Dragon’s Den. Is that right, Kevin?

Kevin:
Yeah. England, Canada, Sweden. There are two formats, Dragon’s Den and Shark Tank.

Ivan:
Yeah, so that is the show and Kevin was on the original shark tank. What’s the best or worst deals that you experienced when you were on the show?

Kevin:
I loved picking personal things that had a product tied into them. One of the best products that I did- I looked for things that had a little zaniness to them or craziness or vitality possibly. This woman came on with the pet product. It was a device for your cat to teach the cat – how you could get rid of the litter box and teach the cat to use the human toilet to go to the bathroom. It was a cat toilet training system and it was called Sitty Kitty. Now, people asked why would you want to do that? I said, “I saw the ability to take viral videos and put them out on the internet.” I am in the Shark Tank, and this took off like crazy. Millions of dollars made. We had a huge success in profits. It got into the retail shelves, etc. So that one was a great one.

And then, I have always had more than one that weren’t so good. I had one that a woman had a line of products that revolved around jellies, jam and things like that and 20 SKUs. I actually invested a half a million dollars into this company for a large percentage of the equity but one of the challenges was that we were missing sort of that hero product. We had a lot of great products but not any couple of heroes. So when we went on Home Shopping Network, we sold some product, but we didn’t have something that was repeatable and is usually successful. So at the end of the day, that turned out not to be a big success, and was actually a half a million dollar investment that to this day has not paid back.

So the bottom line is you take the good with the bad. Take Sitty Kitty and who would have known that would be so successful? If you remember the movie, Meet the Faulkers, they had a cat that jumped up on the toilet and drove everyone crazy on the movie, right? That was the kind of thing that Sitty Kitty taught cats how to do.

Ivan:
So you know, I think one of the lessons here is you have to keep plugging away and there might be a specific product that you are offering or a service that you are offering, but you have to keep- don’t give up.

Kevin:
Yeah, yeah, I definitely. Agree. I say it like this: I don’t get discouraged by failures. Winston Churchill said it this way: Success is being able to go from failure to failure without the loss of enthusiasm. It’s because you know you are going to hit a success after you lose some, right?

Ivan:
Yeah.

Kevin:
I want to fail fast and fail cheap. And then, the success will come right? I will fail two or three times and know that is out of my system and now when I hit that home run, hundreds of millions of dollars comes across.

Ivan:
We are running out of time but I have two other questions for you. Here is one of them: What is the one thing, and I know there are many things, but what is the one thing that an entrepreneur can do to supercharge their business?

Kevin:
I believe in all my years as an entrepreneur, in my earlier days, I tried to do everything myself. I was very headstrong. I tried to do it all and didn’t anybody. Once I brought in experts – I brought in a banking guru that helped me raise a lot of money. That took our company from 60 million to 500 million. I couldn’t have done that without capital. So I say that most entrepreneurs, if they build a dream team, here is the plan that I say is important at: get experts, get consultants, get advisors, get mentors. Whatever it takes, get some smart people around you because no matter how smart you are, you are not going to know everything. There is a lot of stuff happening in digital marketing. I am an old school tv marketer, newspaper marketer. I have gurus in digital. Who have done amazing things that we use now very successful. So I had to bring in experts for things I did not have the skill sets in. I call it building the dream team and getting the best that you can get.

By the way, I am not saying they all have to be employees. Some of these are advisors, advisory board members, you know how that goes, Ivan. It’s power.

Ivan:
I do. This is great advice for everyone who is listening to this. Your BNI chapter is part of that dream team. You need to have those good employees, as Kevin is talking about. You need to have outside advisors. But you should consider BNI as part of that dream team because these are people who give you advice on many, many different topics. So I absolutely love that one.

I have one more question for you, Kevin, and we need to wrap up. I understand that you have just partnered with the Zig Ziglar family to help them with Zig’s content. There is nobody int he world who has the sales content like Zig Ziglar. I was honored to be a guest on a radio show with him one time. Amazing gentleman. You partnered with the family. There is a website now [This website is no longer valid] ZigSecrets.com, and you are partnered with them now. So I urge my BNI members to go on the website. You have something there that you want to recommend that would be of value to them.

Kevin:
Yes, I do and thank you so much. Zig passed away six years ago, and I got with the family and said there are so many people that we need to connect Zig with.

I have one more question for you, Kevin, and we need to wrap up. I understand that you have just partnered with the Zig Ziglar family to help them with Zig’s content. There is nobody int he world who has the sales content like Zig Ziglar. I was honored to be a guest on a radio show with him one time. Amazing gentleman. You partnered with the family. There is a website now [This website is no longer valid] ZigSecrets.com, and you are partnered with them now. So I urge my BNI members to go on the website. You have something there that you want to recommend that would be of value to them.

[This website is no longer valid] ZigSecrets.com will give you a cheat sheet. It is absolutely free. You download. You put in an email. We will send you in an email all kinds of goodies from Zig. He touched a lot of people, hundreds of millions of people while he was here on the earth and when he passed away, millions of people went to Facebook to leave him a tribute. He was a mentor to me, and amazing man. And now anybody listening can get a slice of Zig. We digitally remastered a lot of content from Zig and I shot some stuff on how he came to be in my life. That is at [This website is no longer valid] ZigSecrets.com. I partnered with the family. It is great stuff. Thank you. Thanks for putting it out there. Anyone who is listening can go there and check it out.

Ivan:
[This website is no longer valid] ZigSecrets.com is the website, and you get the cheat sheet there by just filling out some information. I urge all BNI members to do it. If you want to find out more about Kevin, you can go to KevinHarrington.tv and we will have the URL hotlink right here on the podcast in the transcript so you can just click on it and go straight there.

Kevin, thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast. It has been a real pleasure speaking to you and having you on the show.

Kevin:
My pleasure, too. Thank you. I hope everybody is having a great day and got a couple tips today. Thanks for having me.

Ivan:
I am sure they did. Thanks, Kevin. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. I think that is it for this week, Dr. Misner. Thank you so much for the great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Kevin Harrington joins Dr. Misner to share his secrets for becoming the go-to person in his industry and building billion-dollar businesses.

title.rendered: Episode 574: Knock the Socks off Your Audience (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 489.

Synopsis

Tiffanie Kellog joins Dr. Misner this week to share some tips from her new book, Knock the Socks Off Your Audience. One of her favorite presentation formats for referral generation is called “Three, Three, and Three”.

First Third of Presentation

Tell people why you do what you do (your emotionally charged connection).

Second Third of Presentation

Share success stories about how you’ve helped your clients.

Final Third of Presentation

Conclude with your call to action. You can include more than one, for those not yet ready to refer you.

Remember, you don’t want to sell to your fellow members: you want to sell through them. Be as specific as possible about the referrals you want. Always respect the time limit: your audience will stop listening when the bell sounds.

Brought to you by Networking for Success YouTube Channel.

Complete Transcript of Episode 489 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in the San Diego area today, Priscilla. Thank you for asking. I understand Tiffanie, that you are up in northern California for the Ascentive Conference. Am I correct?

Tiffanie:
I am.

Ivan:
You have been a coach with Ascentive since 2006. It is great to have you back on mypodcast. We did a podcast last month, and we did it about inviting visitors to your featured presentation.

You have a new book that has just come out this week, and I have invited you back to the podcast. I should also say that you are the author of Four and a Half Networking Mistakes, and you are on my Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which Priscilla just mentioned is the sponsor for this podcast.

So you are joining me on the Networking for Success Channel. You have done this book. You have been on my podcast several times, and today you are going to talk about knocking the socks off your audience.

You use that phrase. You use that phrase often. But for you, that phrase has a special meaning, doesn’t it?

Tiffanie:
It does. I didn’t start off trying to start a sock collection. In the fall of 2015, my husband and I thought it would be kind of fun and a good marketing thing to see how long it would take me to wear every pair of socks I had in my closet. The rules were simple. They had to be fun or colorful. They couldn’t be just plain socks. And I could only wear them once and then I would have to move on.

This started in October of 2015 and I am still going with wearing brand new socks. I actually have over 250 pairs of different socks that I wear and I have become known as kind of the gal with the crazy socks.

Ivan:
Crazy socks is an understatement. They are insanely crazy. Just go to Tiffanie’s website: TiffanieKellog.com. And you can see. Certainly, in the “About” section, there is a great picture of one of the pairs of socks.
