Tiffanie, we have known each other a long time, and I am going to share something with my audience. It could be creepy. I asked you and said, “This isn’t creepy, is it?” And you said, “No, no, no, it isn’t creepy.”

So I have seen all of these socks that you have and I always laughed because they are so colorful. I actually took a photograph of my sock drawer – and what color were all my socks, Tiffanie?

Tiffanie:
They were all black.

Ivan:
They were all black. So I just wanted to compare my sock drawer to her sock drawer. I can tell you that her sock drawer has got to be a lot more entertaining than mine.

What I didn’t tell Tiffanie is that I have another sock drawer as well that is all white.

All white. All black. You have these crazy, crazy socks. But you did this book along this theme in a sense about doing things that really get people’s attention. Let’s talk about that.

Tiffanie:
So the book, Knock Your Socks Off Your Audience, is all about how to deliver presentations with power and passion to really compel the audience to like you.

I thought it would be a good follow up from our podcast last month because we were talking about how to invite people to see your presentation. In the book, we talk a lot about how you really capture the audience or knock their socks off because you are just that amazing when you do that presentation. We really have a powerful opportunity with the time that we have at the meeting. So we capture not only the visitors but the chapter members’ attention as well.

Ivan:
You even have an entire chapter in the book about BNI member presentations. Is that correct?

Tiffanie:
That is correct. The book is for all speakers at all levels. It is interesting because if you would have told me 11 years ago that I would be classifying myself as a professional speaker- that is what I consider myself today. I am a member of the National Speakers Association. That’s what I do. I go around and speak.

If you would have told me that 11 years ago before I met my first BNI member, I would have been in disbelief because if the person who had invited me to BNI had said, “Hey Tiffanie, you are going to have to speak for 30 seconds to this group of 50 people,” I would not have gone. I was petrified to speak in public.

Now, through my membership in BNI, I have taken on roles such as leadership and being a trainer for the organization, I kind of got over that fear.

Ivan:
Yeah. So you have some presentation formats that I want you to share here. Here are the nuggets for those of you listening to this podcast. These are the nuggets that you can take away and use for your feature presentations or your presentations at a BNI meeting.

Your format- you call it the 3, 3, 3 format. Is that correct? Do you want to talk about that so that members can walk away with some real tangible ideas?

Tiffanie:
Yeah. So one of my favorite formats when it comes to referral generation as a BNI member is the one that we call 3,3,3. Essentially, we take your presentation and break it down into thirds.

You want to spend the first third of your presentation sharing with us why you do what you do. Sometimes people call this your story. At Ascentive, we call this your emotionally charged connection. This is what drives you. I think that you want to spend the first third of your presentation sharing this. It will actually help compel the audience to like you.

Then in the second third, you will want to share a couple success stories. I am a big believe that you can give us all the details and all the facts and people will or may not remember. But if you can take your business and wrap it up into a story, a success story, of how you helped your clients, it is so much more impactful and so much more memorable.

It’s really not about giving us a whole bunch of ideals. It’s really about giving us two, maybe three max, successes about how you helped your clients, that can really help your chapter members to refer you by sharing what you created for people.

Ivan:
So the first third is your emotionally charged connection. I know I have talked about that here on BNI Podcast. You can do a search on that. If you want to see more on emotionally charged connection, pick up my book, Avoiding the Networking Disconnect. Brendan Scanlon and I go at length to talk about the emotionally charged connection.

The second third, Tiffanie, is success stories. Give some success stories, a story or more, on some of the things that you have done for people.

What is the third?

Tiffanie:
The last third of your presentation is where you get to give us a call to action. I have seen several BNI presentations where people will tell us all about their business but not tell us what to do. This is very important not to skip.

With a call to action, this could be something as simple and obvious as, “Hey, get me a referral,” and give us the specific referral request of people that you are looking to work with. It can also include an introduction to a contact sphere relationship, with noncompeting industries with the same target martket. It could be something as simple as “like me on social media.” “Please give me a review.” “Book me a speaking engagement.” “Invite me to go out and networking with you.”

I love giving a couple of options for the call to action. For BNI members, there may be a couple of people in the room that are at visibility with you, some at credibility with you and some at profitability with you. So if you give us a couple of options on the call to action, it will be more likely to engage the entire audience.

Ivan:
I love the idea of options rather than just one because you are right. You are at a different level with different people in the chapter, so great suggestion on options.What else? Anything else?

Tiffanie:
So those are to me- that is one of my favorite presentations, the 3, 3, and 3. A third on why you do what you do, your ECC. A third on success stories and then a third of the call to action. It is a simple format, very powerful, meant to create referrals.

Ivan:
Alright. So Tiffanie, you have a book that came out this week, Knock the Socks Off Your Audience. How can people get it?

Tiffanie:
If you go to Amazon, if you put into the search bar “Tiffanie Kellog” it will actually pull up my author page and all my books are there.

Ivan:
And this will help people improve their presentations, not only in BNI but outside of the BNI context, yeah?

Tiffanie:
Yes. Definitely.

Ivan:
Good. Anything you want to add before I wrap up here?

Tiffanie:
Do I have time for a quick “do” and a quick “don’t” because we do get into the do’s and don’t of feature presentations for BNI members.

Ivan:
Absolutely. Go ahead.

Tiffanie:
Okay. So a couple “don’ts” that I can share with BNI members. You don’t want to sell to the members in your feature presentation. You want to sell through the members. It’s very important-

Ivan:
They are your referral team. You are not trying to sell to them. You are trying to train them.

Tiffanie:
Correct. Also, one of the things in BNI- it’s interesting. We have the bell in some chapters to make sure we don’t go over our time in our feature and our weekly presentations. What I have learned is the bell doesn’t tell the speaker to stop talking. It tells the audience to stop listening. So it is crucial at the end of our 30 seconds or our feature presentation one minute,or however long you have for those two pieces, that you honor that time and not go over because the audience isn’t listening anymore.

Ivan:
Right. Good suggestion.

Tiffanie:
When it comes to the “do’s” make sure you use your success stories to inspire your chapter members to help refer you.

Ivan:
Great. Any other “do”?

Tiffanie:
One other “do” is be specific to be terrific. Tell us exactly who it is that you are looking for.

Ivan:
Yeah, yeah. Laser specific. Look, Tiffanie, those are great suggestions. I recommend that members and people who just listen to my podcast, go pick up the book, Knock the Socks Off Your Audience by Tiffanie Kellog. You can find it at Amazon and go check out Tiffanie’s videos along with my videos at Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. We will have a link in this presentation as well as on the website.

Tiffanie, thanks again for being a guest on BNI Podcast. I appreciate it.

Tiffanie:
Thank you for having me.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. Thank you both very much. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by The Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Divide your presentation into thirds for better referral generation. Remember, you don’t want to sell to your fellow members: you want to sell through them.

title.rendered: Episode 573: Dude, Where Are My Wheels?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week, Dr. Misner shares a story of an early lesson he learned on the value of networking. He was working at a hardware store in South El Monte while getting his bachelor’s degree. One night after closing up, they found one of the employee’s cars on concrete blocks–someone had stolen all the wheels.

One of the other employees, who lived locally, said “Calm down. Go back inside. Let me make some phone calls, and I’ll tell you when you can come back out.”

When Dr. Misner and the owner of the car came back out of the store, all four wheels were back on the car, shiny rims and all. The local employee had friends in the gang that was known for stealing wheels. All it took to have the wheels returned was one phone call to a person this employee knew well.

This experience brought home the lesson that it’s not what you know or who you know, but how well you know them.

If you have a story about a real-life networking lesson you learned, share it in the comments.

For more of Dr. Misner’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 573

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic today, Priscilla, and I have a topic that has a very unusual title. Would you not agree?

Priscilla:
I definitely agree and I am totally curious.

Ivan:
So I recently visited Los Angeles where I grew up and lived for many, many years. I was driving through an area where I grew up around and I was regaling my wife with a story about a job I had in a pretty tough neighborhood when I was in college. At the end of the story, Beth said to me, “You have got to tell this story in a podcast.” So here it is. It is sort of one of my early lessons on the value of networking.

I grew up in a really working class environment early in my life. It was roughly 1975 and I was working on my bachelors degree while I was employed at a hardware store in South El Monte, California. You have to understand that South El Monte was a pretty tough neighborhood with a fair number of gangs that were active in the area. We closed the hardware store one evening at 7 pm and it took about 30 minutes to close all the registers and leave the store. In that 30 minute period, a lot could happen, particularly in that neighborhood. So around 7:30, we walked out of the store and we found one of the employee’s cars sitting in the parking lot literally propped up on blocks. Someone had stolen all four awesome wheels and left the car on four concrete blocks where it sat in the parking lot. Basically, waiting for us to get off work and walk out there. Clearly, my friend, the person whose car it was, was apoplectic when he walked out. He went absolutely crazy.

What’s amazing to me was that one of the other employees who lived locally said to the guy with the car with the missing wheels. He said, “Calm down. Relax. Let me make a call. Give me just a few minutes and let me see what I can do. You guys go back in the store and wait and I will let you know when you can come back out.”

I’m not kidding. Within an hour, he came into the store and said, “Okay. It’s alright. You can come back out.” We went back into the parking lot and lo and behold, there was this guy’s car with all of his wheels, bright shiny rims and all, reinstalled back on his car, good as new.

It turned out that that the local employee had friends in a gang that was known for heisting awesome wheels off of cars and he simply made a call to one of the members that he knew well. Now, you have to understand, he wasn’t in the gang, but he knew people in the gang. All it took to have the wheels returned was one phone call to that one person who he knew really well. And I was just, I was amazed. I was 18 years old and I think it was really one of the first really powerful lessons that I experienced about the value of really knowing the right people, which is of course, a tenet of networking. It helped me to learn it really is not what you know or who you know. It’s how well you know each other that counts. No matter what the situation. I had this story, obviously, in my head for a long time, and when I told my wife this story as we were driving by El Monte, she said, “That’s a networking story.”

I said, “How is that a networking story? I thought it was an Ivan-in-the-hood story, not a networking story.”

She said, “No, it’s about who you know and how well you know them.” The fact that he could make this call and have our fellow friend’s wheels not just dropped off but put back on to the car was hysterical and true. So I think it is a great example of why networking is an important skill set to have no matter where you are talking about. What do you think, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Well, just curious, did he have to pass a few dollar bills in their direction?

Ivan:
No. He didn’t have to pay a penny. This old friend, a co-worker said he called a buddy of his who he knew was part of the group and said, “Hey, dude. This guy is a friend of mine. Bring his wheels back, will you?” First he said, “ I am guessing you guys took them.” “Oh yeah, we took them.” “Well, bring them back. He is a really good friend. I work with him.” And they just brought them back, no problem. No money exchanged hands, which was absolutely amazing.

Priscilla:
Yeah, it really does pay to know people in high or low places.

Ivan:
That’s a good point. In high or low places. There is no doubt about that. You know what I think is good about this story too is that I think people assume that because I run a global company and because I have several degrees that I maybe come from a background – there are just assumptions about who I am and where I come from. You know, this is really not true. I come from a very working class sort of community and the edges of that community were, in fact, very edgy.

As sort of a side note, I think it is a great example about how education is a great equalizer in the world that people can come from a community like that and with the right education and the right support, certainly from family members, you can lift yourself from situations like I experienced on more than one occasion.

That is my story today. I would love to hear, if you are listening to this podcast of an example, of how this might apply to you in your life, you know, something that happened in your experience that you can share here on BNI Podcast to show the power of your network. So that is everything I have today, Priscilla. Back over to you.

Priscilla:
Well, great, Ivan. I am sure that there is a lot of inspiration in that story for people that are facing the kind of situations that you may have faced when you were young. Also, there is a lot of humor in that story, so thank you so much for sharing it.

Ivan:
For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thanks so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares a story from his youth about how important networking is–even (or maybe especially) in rough neighborhoods. If you have a story about a real-life networking lesson you learned, share it in the comments.

title.rendered: Episode 572: Focused Invite Days

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Charlie Teixera from BNI Hawaii joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about focused invite days. By using this approach, Charlie’s chapter had a 25% increase in membership and a 35% increase in Thank You for Closed Business.

A focused invite day is a day each month where the whole chapter invites visitors who are in the featured presenter’s contact sphere. That means every member gets a day where the entire chapter is focused on bringing the most likely referral partners for that member to the meeting.

Leading up to these focused invite days, the leadership team provides education to the chapter members about what contact spheres are and ways in which members should invite visitors.

There are five key ingredients to the success of focused invite days:

  1. Support from area and regional directors and your chapter’s leadership team with the education process and the invitations.
  2. Asking all members to write down and share their reasons why growth is important and inviting visitors is advantageous.
  3. Consistent and kind reminders to the members in advance of the focused invite days, including recognition for those who have invited visitors already.
  4. Holding members accountable for following through with their invitations. It inspires everyone to see who has accepted invitations. Seeing your work pay off and how it helps your fellow members is a great motivator.
  5. Focused invite days help with retention by re-focusing on contact spheres. The chapter helps existing members recognize who can help them grow their business and then invites those people as visitors.

It takes a little time to build up momentum with focused invite days, but if you follow the process consistently every month, it will pay off.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 572 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am at Global Support in Charolette, North Carolina this week, Priscilla. It’s always great to visit headquarters. I have a special guest today on BNI Podcast. Her name is Charlie Teixeira. She is a BNI member in Hawaii and she is the owner of North Shore Embroidery which runs the Route 99 Hawaii line. She’s been a member of BNI for 7 years and a Director Consultant for 4 years. Charlie, welcome to BNI Podcast!

Charlie:
Hello, Ivan. Good to be here!

Ivan:

It’s great to have you on. We are going to talk today about a concept that you have been applying to you chapter called “Focused Invite Days”. Your director [Joan Shiree] loved it so much. She said that I had to bring you on to the podcast, and you and I had met so I was happy to do that. In the last year- in the last roughly around June to May 2017 to 2018, you had a 25% net growth in your membership by using this approach- which is awesome! And which is incredibly impressive to me, is that you had a 35% increase in your Thank you for closed business. So, well done! And well done to you chapter! You went from the grey to the green in 1 year by using the things that you’re talking about with the “Focused Invite Days.”

So, your 5 key takeaways, let’s see if we can hit on as many of these as we can. First is “Support from your leadership.” Do you want to talk about that a little bit, Charlie?

Charlie:
Sure! Thanks for having me on, I really appreciate it! So, I kind of boiled down what I thought were the 5 key takeaways for having really successful “Focused Invite Days.” The first one is, having strong support from your leadership. And this comes from the Executive Director, as you mentioned [ Joanne Shuree] who held these numerous training webinars for all of our chapters in Hawaii, and they were recorded for incoming growth coordinators. So for anyone who in on the podcast and doesn’t know about “Focused Invite Days” what they are is you set up a time of the month- so for us at the North Shore chapter, its the third Thursday of each month where members are inviting specific people to listen to this Feature Presenter who are in the speakers “Contact Sphere”- we’ll come back to this. And leading up to these “Focused Invite Days” your providing education to the chapter members on what “Contact Spheres” are, the ways in which members should invite visitors – which includes using BNI Connect Global and just general stuff on how to invite.

So, we had Joanne running these webinars, and then we also had really good support from our leadership team in the chapter such as our President { names stated but didn’t look up } and our Director in Market theory all kind of walked the the walk and they were okay with holding members accountable and checking with members and saying or asking them, “How can we help you invite visitors?” So, that was key to having that support.

Ivan:
And that is helpful, but if you’re in a region where the Director is not doing that, how do you suggest they address that? Do maybe after chapter meetings discussions about this- just in case you don’t have a Director who is doing that.

Charlie:
I think that is a great question! Maybe just have an open discussion with the Director and say, “This is something that has worked for another region.”

Ivan:
Good point! So come listen to this podcast and do it in your region. Then of course you need to have the Leadership Team actively on board.

So, next thing I think you have in the key takeaways is, do chapter members really need to start this discussion with their “why”? You thought this was very important. Talk about that.

Charlie:
Yeah, I think this is actually one of the most important things is having member really consider for themselves why growth is important. Or why inviting visitors would be advantageous. So, I had members write this on a piece of paper and handed it in, I compiled it and then emailed it back to the group. I was actually really surprised by the variety and in-depth answers. As a former hippy teacher, one of the pieces of advice I got was, “To find a great discussion is asking questions to which you do not know all the answers.” I got other interesting surprising answers there.

Ivan:
Excellent! What else? Because I agree with you about asking your “why’s” being it is one fo the most important thing.

Charlie:
It builds a foundation that they can come back to later. So, the third key which I wanted to hone in on just a little bit is consistent “Be Kind” reminders to the members. So leading up to our “Focused Invite Day”, we were asking members to bring to the meeting 4-6 names of people they will be writing. Our meeting is on a Thursdays, typically right after the meeting I’ll send an email out to all of our members recognizing members who have invited visitors- which recognition being 1 of the “Be Nice” core values. And then the following Monday I’ll send out another email reminder letting them know who they should be inviting, and I think that tone is really important because as everyone knows, we are really busy and sometimes the reminders I would send out would be more gentle or funny. Just to kind of nudge members along to invite them. I think between being gentle and being bossy, I would be on the side of gentleness. Because hopefully the motivation for them inviting people would intrinsically come back to the “why’s” which they’ve already thought about.

Ivan, one of the biggest “why’s” for me, which happened maybe 4 months into this “Focused Invite Day” series, is that we had invited an interior decorator from one of our members to hear her speak, and we had 7 visitors that day. This member shared with her husband, who then shared with [ Joanne Shuree], that it really meant a lot to her and was really encouraging that everyone took the time to invite these people. And that just kind of hit home. This is a feeling you that you can’t fake of “Givers Gain.” I don’t know, I was kind of surprised at how emotionally engaged- even more engaged I became at that point. So, I just wanted to share that one story.

Ivan:
And that where- what you said to me that, “Tone is important.” Doing this as a way of helping one another and supporting one another to have a better team is all about that tone.

Charlie:
Correct. Definitely.

On the flip side of being kind and gentle is it also enforces accountability. We need to do more than just ask our members to invite visitors. IT’s asking them in the beginning, “Is it okay if you give me permission to hold you accountable?” So, it suggested in the BNI Coordinator manual to use a whiteboard or flip chart, and what we do is we have a white board and two weeks before “Focused Invite Day” we are saying to our members, “You will be asked to write 6 names of the people you are inviting.” Write them literally on the whiteboard, and the next week circle the names that RSVP-ed. You are actually aiming for 100% participation, so like a day before I’ll send out a text saying, “I’m filling out the board right now, just a (gentle) reminder to do that-“ You don’t want to embarrass anyone or call anyone out. But it really does work where everyone sees it on the board. It’s really exciting to have everyone in a chapter to write down all these names on the board.

Ivan:
And that process not only helps maintain accountability, but it also helps get engagement. That engagement is, what I believe leads to more effective accountability.

Charlie:
Yes, exactly! One of the long term lessons that I’ve learned was that it had this snowball/lava flowing technique where it a gated people more and –

Ivan:
You’re from Hawaii and so that “lava flowing” thing is really important!

Charlie:
It is! It probably is right now.

Ivan:
I’m sorry, I cut you off. Go ahead and finish your statement.

Charlie:
It’s just that flow where they are seeing this pay off and they are seeing how it’s helping their fellow members. It really does create this snowball/lava-ball effect.

Ivan:
Right. So, the last key takeaway is “Retention.” Talk a little bit about that and how that has anything to do with “Focused Invite Days.”

Charlie:
Well, this really ties in with a great presentation at the BNI National Convention. The talk- that I’m sure you remember was, “The most important thing is not growth; it’s retention.” And really refocusing back on context here. So you can grow your chapter by 10 members, but if you lose 20, you obviously have a net loss. So, it’s a really good education topic to talk about Contact Spheres. Specifically- this came from a chapter that I oversee that had a “Focused Invite Day” that had a credit card processing person in the chapter and we asked him, “Who do you want the members in the chapter to invite?” His immediate response was, “Anyone who uses credit card processing.” Of course that is not someone in his Contact Sphere. So, we are talking to him and saying, “Well maybe you can invite people from an alarm company, who already go into restaurants who have a similar target market.” Those are the people in that person’s Contact Sphere to invite. So, very similarly, this happened in out North Shore chapter where we had an [inaudible] representative and her immediate response was, “I want to have people come to my presentation who just want to change their health.” Which of course is broad. We had her narrow down and say we wanted to invite nutritionists because they share the same target market. Nutritionists would be talking to clients about their health. It was really helping build these Contact Spheres for members and as we’ve read that 60-70% of your business in BNI comes from your Contact Sphere.Which we see retention leads to sustained growth.

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree more!

Let me just clarify for those of you who are listening to the podcast what the difference between a Contact Sphere and a Power Team is. Contact Sphere is a group of business professionals that. Have a symbiotic relationship. A “Power Team” is the professional that you have a relationship with. So, the Contact Sphere are the people that could potentially fit within that symbiotic relationship. A Power Team are the people that you have a relationship with that fit within the Contact Sphere. So ithe idea is to look within your Contact Sphere to bring them to BNI so that they become a Power Team for you because they are a symbiotic profession. If you are focusing your inviting based on people who are in your Contact Sphere, you are going to increase your referrals at a great percentage. Hence that example of 25% net growth in membership, but a 35% increase in thank you for closed business.Does that sound right to you, Charlie?

Charlie:
It does.

Ivan:
Well, thank you so much for being on the podcast! Any last comments of thoughts you have about your “Focused Invite Days’?

Charlie:
Just that it takes a little bit of time and momentum to build. And just like BNI, it goes back to structure and doing it on a monthly basis.

Ivan:
Yes, I agree with that last thing a lot. It’s about having a plan and working the plan. So, if you talk this concept that Charlie is talking about here and you do it consistently, and get engagement from your local Executive Director or Director Consultant to help you, you too can have a substantial growth in you membership and your referrals.

Charlie, thanks so much for being on the podcast today. I really appreciate you and I appreciate you taking the time to be on the show!

Charlie:
I appreciate you so much, too. Thanks, Ivan and Priscilla. Aloha!

Ivan:
Aloha!

Over to you, Priscilla!

Priscilla:
Okay, great! Aloha to you both. Thank you so much! That was a wonderful podcast.

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and I look forward to you joining us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: A focused invite day is a day each month where the whole chapter invites visitors who are in the featured presenter’s contact sphere. That means every member gets a day where the entire chapter is focused on bringing the most likely referral partners for that member to the meeting.

title.rendered: Episode 571: The BNI Foundation–What’s in It for Me?

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Kevin Barber joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about why the BNI Foundation is relevant to the average BNI member.

  • Social consciousness is extremely important to younger generations of business professionals who may be considering BNI membership.
  • You can’t have an organization with the philosophy “Givers Gain” without wanting to support social causes.
  • Getting involved in your community makes your chapter and your business more visible and introduces you and BNI to people you would never have met otherwise.

As a BNI member, you can contribute financially to the BNI Foundation, but you and your chapter can also get involved in your local community by offering your time and your talents through the Business Voices program. One of the most important things you can do is teach basic business skills and networking to students.

Many chapters and BNI members are already doing work in their communities. Let your area director and the BNI Foundation know what your chapter is doing. Contact the BNI Foundation and tell them you heard about Business Voices on this podcast.

For more of Dr. Misner’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.

Complete Transcript of Episode 571

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:
I am fantastic, Priscilla, and I have a special guest on today’s podcast, Kevin Barber. Kevin has been a BNI Director for 13 years. He manages 41 chapters. He has over 1260 members in his region. Here is the really important thing. Last year, he generated $100,000,000 in thank you for closed business, and since he started 13 years ago, more than a half a billion US dollars worth of business. Kevin is a BNI Director in Germany, although you won’t recognize it from his accent. He is the person who started the BNI Connect Five, so if you have ever seen any of the incredible videos called BNI Connect Five, he is that man, and you may recognize his voice from that. He was a really important contributor to helping support BNI Connect when it first came out, and Connect Five was a major part of that. Kevin is today the Chairman of the BNI Foundation. I count him as one of my personal friends. Kevin, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Kevin:
Thank you, Ivan. It is a pleasure to be here.

Ivan:
So our topic today is the BNI Foundation. Obviously, you are a BNI Director. You have a lot of members. Referrals are extremely important to the organization. You have also been incredibly supporting of the organization through the foundation. And you are currently the chairman of the BNI Foundation globally. Why is the BNI Foundation relevant to the average BNI member or director?

Kevin:
Well, when I started BNI in early 2005, fairly quickly, we go to the point there other people were asking me, “What can I do for you?” I really wanted to tell them to do something for charities close to my heart. That was what I was interested in as a person. I just didn’t feel comfortable doing that because BNI is not about charity. BNI for me, was you joined BNI to get more business, not to ask people to give money to an organization. So for very many years, i was extremely cautious and never even mentioned a world about it becauseI didn’t think it was important for BNI. BNI should be there to generate referrals and nothing else.

When I did start getting involved is I realized this was a huge benefit for members, I realized this was a huge benefit for members and Directors because we have lots of things going on in our community. I don’t know what it’s like in your part of the world, but certainly in Germany and other countries in Europe, there is a very high level of business activity. People are not complaining that they don’t have enough work, but getting involved in the community gives the business, the chapter, the member a very good station for themselves which is not only shown in the form of referrals, but we are talking about the long term reputation of your own business, of your chapter of BNI as an organization. What we do find is that chapters and members are getting involved in their local communities and enjoy a much higher reputation. People come back to them for more business.

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree more. There is this great book I read a number of years ago, Conscious Capitalism, which really impacted me. We had started the BNI Foundation after that, but it really supported in my mind the importance of BNI and local regions supporting charitable causes. One of the things I have learned and you have probably learned this as well is that millennial, more than ever – if we want to attract millennials into BNI as we do, the younger business professionals, social consciousness is extremely important to the younger generation of business professionals.

And I think there are many reasons why supporting charitable causes – of course the biggest one, you can’t possibly have an organization’s philosophy of givers gain without wanting to support social causes. Would you agree?

Kevin:
I absolutely agree. I think we are the organization in the world which is predestined to be involved in this sort of activity, without any doubt at all. When we started getting involved in these charitable events – I hate the word charity, but I cant find a better one at the moment, but you realize it was the doing good which felt good. You have to feel good in your heart. But what was starting to happen is that members and chapters were becoming more visible in their community. When they were doing that work and were engaged in their local community, they were making new contacts. They were getting people to their chapters that. Would never, even have come to BNI before. Certainly a lot of people knew who we were and what we were doing. And we were very, very highly thought of as a result.

Ivan:
Yeah, and I think that this idea of a social consciousness is not a new mission for BNI. Our mission is still to tell people how to increase business through referrals. But it is, I think, a great support of that mission because it builds a name recognition and exposure for the organization worldwide. One of the questions I get a lot and as the chairman of the foundation, maybe you can talk about it, is what is the difference between the BNI foundation and Business Voices?

Kevin:
Whenever I listen to Dr. Ivan, yourself, or Beth answer this question; Everything is perfect and the answer is complete and clear. As soon as anyone else starts to answer it, it becomes a little more difficult but I have my own definition of Olasioso!! And have come to the conclusion that the best way of looking at it from a members point of view is, “Yes we could have a BNI foundation.” A BNI foundation involves donations and grants. It needs a very standard and charitable sort of process. But being a Business Voices initiative- which is an initiative of BNI, enables BNI members and chapters to get involved in their local communities without a foundation, without money being involved, using their time and their talents to support schools and educational organizations in order to make young peoples lives easier in the future. I know there’s issues in certain countries with school supplies and materials, other countries don’t have those issues. But what most countries do have, is the situation that children are leaving school without access and knowledge of basic business skills- such as finance, insurance, and networking.

One great example of benefits of Business Voices here was the first initiative we did in the east of Germany, two people within a BNI chapter went into a local school and were teaching them for several weeks. Everything went very, very well. The kids loved it. We had a good impact on the local community. And a few weeks later, somebody turned up to the BNI meeting and the Executive Director said, “How did you get to know about us?” And he said, “Oh that’s easy, you taught my daughter.”

Ivan:
It’s exciting to see people come in from the work of the foundation! Just to clarify, Business Voices is a initiative of the BNI Foundation which is part of BNI, but its mission!! Of the BNI Foundation.

I think you described it well, it’s basically a way for people to contribute their time and talent locally- which chapters are doing anyway but what we want to do is put it underneath the umbrella of Business Voices. I think business can be noble and that business can make a difference in local communities, and this is a way for local businesses to have a voice in the local communities for children and education.

So how can people get involved in Business Voices- which is part of BNI Foundation?

Kevin:
I think the best way to go about it would be to visit the BNI Foundation website. On the BNI foundation website ( BNIFoundation.org ) there is a point “Contact Us” which is on the left-hand side, fill in the inquiry form put your name, email address and someone from BNI will get back to you.

Ivan:
“Contact Us” is across the top towards the right, but then when you click on that it’s all on the left. And mention that you heard about us on the podcast, that would be helpful too.

So if you’re interested in finding out more about the BNI Foundation, particularly Business Voices which is contributing back to the communities with your time and talent, we’d love to talk to you! Kevin is as I said the Chairman of the BNI Foundation which runs the Business Voices project.

Let me just ask you one last question before we wrap up, Kevin. What’s the vision of the BNI Foundation globally and is there any other way that people can get involved other than contacting us, that you recommend?

Kevin:
I would like BNI members to think how easy it is to visitors or difficult it is to get. How easy it is to get people to understand the benefits of BNI as an organization in the community. And just imagine there are now 8,000- well I think at the beginning of the year we were at 8,400 chapters. Just take the number 8,400 chapters and every chapter in the world is doing something for their local community. If only we can gather that information, and that is my vision that some point in the very near further, we’ll have chapters in 73 countries reporting what they are doing in their local community. If they’re only doing 100 dollars a month, we are looking at 10 million dollars a year and making a social impact in the communities and where we do business. I think that would be an enormous ticket to have. And what people can do to help support this would be to. Let us know what you are doing whether you’re an Area Director, Executive Director, or National Director we want to know what you’re doing. Find a way to accumulate all of this social impact we have as an organization. Because I would have never thought when I joined BNI, that BNI would be doing so much good in communities across the world.

Ivan:
You bring up a good point, because a lot of people are doing things but they’re not doing it under the umbrella of the BNI Foundation. If you’re doing anything in you chapters, we’d love for you to bring it under the umbrella of the BNI Foundation through BNI Foundation- Business Voices initiatives. So reach out to your Local Director, reach out to the BNI Foundation website.

I love your story about someone showing up, and I recently heard about a chapter who the foundation supported a local charity and that charity was so impressed by what was being done, they joined the BNI chapter and somebody who was at that event also joined the local BNI chapter because they said, “Any organization that supports local charities like this organization, I want to be apart of.” So they ended up getting two members as a result of doing a local, charitable event. I think that’s a great example of how the BNI Foundation- can not only do good, but help local local chapters as well.

Well, Kevin, thank you so much for being on my podcast. And thank you for all the of the amazing work you do for the BNI Foundation. You are a volunteer and you volunteer an immense amount of time, and I personally appreciate it! Thanks for being on our podcast today.

Kevin:
You’re very welcome! We just started to scratch the surface, there’s still a long ways to go.

Ivan:
Thank you.

Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
Ok, thank you both for the great information. I really appreciate it!

For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and I look forward to you joining us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Kevin Barber explains why the BNI Foundation is relevant to the average BNI member: “Givers Gain” and social causes go hand in hand.

title.rendered: Episode 570: More 1-2-1s = More Referrals (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 472.

Synopsis

Because you give away a little bit of your reputation when you make a referral, trust is very important to the referral process. BNI can accelerate the process of developing trust between BNI members through regular 1-2-1s.

To be effective, a 1-2-1 needs to be both structured and social. The more you can find overlapping areas of professional and personal interest, the more successful your 1-2-1 is going to be. The Gains Exchange form (see below) is an effective tool for discovering these common interests.

As you can see from the graph below, there is a direct linear correlation between the number of 1-2-1s conducted and the number of referrals given and received. Those who do three or more 1-2-1s per month give and receive twice as many referrals as those who do one or fewer 1-2-1s in a month.

[gview file=”https://www.bnipodcast.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/08/GAINS-Exchange-Form-BNI-fillable.pdf”]

Listen to Episode 191 for a great example of how this works.

Do you have a story about how 1-2-1s have helped you get referrals? Share your story in the comments.

Brought to you by Networking Now.
Complete Transcript of Episode 570 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Well, I am actually home this week. I am in Austin, Texas trying to catch up on some of my writing. Today I am going to talk about, I think, a really interesting topic. It is based on some research that Beatrice Sparacino did recently. It is about 1-2-1s.

When you give a referral to someone, we have talked about this in the podcast before- when you give a referral to someone, you give a little of your reputation away. If it is a good referral, it enhances your reputation. If you give a bad referral, it hurts your reputation.

The referral process works most efficiently if you know, like and trust someone. Trust is particularly important. Therefore, referrals and trust tend to go hand in hand. Trust is important to the referral process. How do you expedite that within the context of networking? How can you move that along?

BNI can be, I think, an accelerator of the proces, reducing the time required to build trust between individuals. One of the best ways to accelerate that trust is through regular 1-2-1s.

Research involving hundreds of BNI members in Europe was recently concluded by Beatrice as part of her thesis in Milan, Italy. She had a really interesting thesis, and over the next few months, I am going to be sharing some of the results of that. I have co-authored an article with her in SuccessNet. That is what I am talking about today, material from that article.

In here, there will be a graph. So if you are listening to this on iTunes, when you get a chance, go to bnipodcast.com so that you can see the graph that I am going to be talking about. I am going to post the graph up here.

What is key in conducting an effective 1-2-1 is that it needs to be both structured and social. It’s kind of interesting. You want it structured but you also want it to be social- a combination of a business discussion with enough personal information to allow both parties to get to know one another better.

If you continue the process, you will soon discover that this is the key to overlapping the areas of professional and personal interest. The more you can find overlapping areas of professional and personal interest, the more the 1-2-1 is going to me.

One of the BNI tools that we recommend you use to achieve this is the GAINS exchange. I talked about the GAINS exchange previously on the podcast. It stands for goals, accomplishments, interests, networks and skills. We will have a PDF of the GAINS exchange also on this podcast that people can download so when people are doing 1-2-1s- try to use the GAINS exchange, particularly with other BNI members.

This is a great tool to use to get to know people better, both socially and professionally.

We know that the quality of the 1-2-1s important. What is interesting is the result of the research that Beatrice did. The quantity of 1-2-1s, not just the quality but the quantity, is really important. You can see that in the graph that we are going to attach here. There is a direct linear correlation between the number of 1-2-1s conducted and the number of referrals given and received. So then the more 1-2-1s people did, the more referrals they gave and they received.

Those who do three or four 1-2-1s a month both give and receive double the number of referrals as people who do one or less. Let me repeat that. If you do three or more 1-2-1s per month, you are more likely to give twice as many referrals. More importantly, you are more likely to receive twice as many referrals as people who do one or less.

So by just- if you said to somebody, “I can tell you how to double the number of referrals that you get in BNI,” anyone would say, “Yeah, please, tell me.” Do three or four 1-2-1s every month. One a week. And you will double the number of referrals that you get.

You see, once you get to know someone and develop a level of trust, you are clearly more likely to refer this person. There is a lot of anecdotal evidence to support these finds.

For example, episode 191 of BNI Podcast includes a great story, Priscilla. I don’t know if you remember it was about a BNI member named Allen who was really concerned after the big recession of 2008. He was a commercial real estate agent whose industry was decimated by the recession. But after he joined BNI, he just did a ton of 1-2-1s and he actually had his best year ever after the recession. Really in the midst of the recession, he had his best year ever because of the number of 1-2-1s he did. Do you recall that podcast, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I don’t recall the specific podcast, but the way this works, I have seen this over and over again in the group and in my personal experience as well.

Ivan:
Yeah. So the Milan study that Beatrice did clearly shows hard evidence, hard evidence of what Allen’s story suggested, namely that there is a direct correlation between the number of 1-2-1s carried out and the number of referrals generated. The higher the number of 1-2-1s, the higher the number of referrals.

You’ve got to have quality 1-2-1s, not just sitting down with someone and saying, “Eh, tell me what you do again. What is it that you do? And here is what I do,” and that is it. That is not a quality 1-2-1. They have to be quality 1-2-1s and that is why we recommend the use of the GAINS exchange as a way to help make sure that you really go deep and do this properly.

It is okay to talk about some social things. I know I have told the story about two guys who found out they were both soccer coaches and all of a sudden really made a strong connection. Even though they had been in the chapter together for a year and never referred each other, they started referring each other after they found out they were soccer coaches.

I have developed relationships, professional relationships, with people after I found out that they loved to play chess. And I love to play chess. We started making a connection and stay in touch as a result of chess, for crying out loud. Go figure. But it is the social interaction that often makes a very big difference.

BNI provides members with methods and instruments to activate this process and as the results show from this study that Beatrice did, BNI is a little like a gym membership. Paying a subscription to get fit is just not enough. You need to actually show up and work out in order to get the results. BNI functions the same way. The more effort you put into the program, the better the results.

So start setting up your 1-2-1s with your fellow members today. That is the most important message. Three to four a month, and you are, over time, going to generate twice the number of referrals.

Priscilla:
That is some great advice. I am going to follow it myself.

Ivan:
And we would love to hear your story, as I have always said. If you are listening to this and you have had a great experience with 1-2-1s and they have helped you, please share that story here on BNIPodcast. Feel free to comment.

I know I have said this before. I read every comment that I get. Oftentimes, I share those stories that you share publicly at BNI events that I go to. So leave your message. Leave your story here on BNI podcat. That’s all I have today, Priscilla, unless you have anything else.

Priscilla:
No, I think that is it. So thank you so much, Dr. Misner. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

To be effective, a 1-2-1 needs to be both structured and […]

title.rendered: Episode 569: Body Language and Networking

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Body language can be a powerful attractant–or deterrent–in networking. Here are four questions that the media have asked Dr. Misner over the years about body language and networking.

What can you do to increase your confidence and come off as a warm, friendly, knowledgeable person?

People overthink this. It comes down to being more interested than interesting. Just practice being an interested interviewer and an active listener.

What’s the latest reputable science saying about hand gestures and how they affect the way we’re perceived by other people?

In 2015, analysis of hundreds of hours of TED talks found that the most popular speakers used twice as many hand gestures as the least popular speakers.

Is it true that the so-called “power pose” may not be as effective as research originally showed?

The “power pose” is great if you’re Wonder Woman or Superman, but for mere mortals, not so much. Just be your best self. Most importantly, maintain good eye contact.

How close should you stand to people when you’re talking to them?

“Personal space” varies a lot from culture to culture. In North American culture, you should stand roughly an arm’s length away from people. Stepping into someone’s cultural space can make them very uncomfortable–especially a member of the opposite sex.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 569 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. I am in Austin Texas where the heat is crazy this summer. It’s crazy.

Priscilla:
I bet.

Ivan:
Today, I want to talk about body language and how it can be a really powerful attractant or deterrent when it comes to building networking relationships with other people. People kind of assess you visually within the first few minutes of meeting you. Over the years I have been asked a lot of questions about body language by the media. Here are four questions I have been asked over the years and what the answers are.

The first one is what can you do to increase your confidence and come across as a more warm person in terms of body language? The answer to that is that I think people over think this one. The answer is pretty straightforward. If you are having a conversation, be more interested than interesting. I have talked about that in a podcast before.

If you are meeting people, practice being an interested interviewer and an active listener. Learn about them and during the process, make sure your expressions match that interest. Don’t look bored. Look engaged. Do that with a smile, an appropriate reaction to a comment, a few nods – not like a bobble head doll – and a few nods. Also, use your eyebrows to show your reaction to comments. Don’t do this unauthentically. If you show interest in other people, you will be amazed at some of the stories you hear and people that you meet. You know, be sure your eyebrows, if you are confused, that they scrunch down, and if you are interested or surprised by something, you raise your eyebrows. My friend Sam Horn calls this the “Eyebrow Test” to really show interest and confidence.

Here is the second question: What does reputable science say about hand gestures? I just had this question recently. What does the latest reputable science say about hand gestures and how they. Affect how you are perceived by other people? A study recently done by Holler and Beatty found that gesture increase the value of someone’s message by 60%. They analyzed thousands of hours of TED Talks and found one striking pattern. They found the most watched TED Talks were done by people who effectively used hand gestures. Specifically, they analyzed the top and the bottom TED Talks and found that the least popular TED Talks used an average of 272 hand gestures during the 18 minute talk. The most popular TED Talks used an average of 465 hand gestures. During their talk, almost double.

So remember that hand gestures are good when talking to someone, but don’t turn. It into jazz hands. You know, what I mean by jazz hands, Priscilla, where your hands are just kind of flopping back and forth?

Priscilla:
Right. Right.

Ivan:
Don’t turn it into jazz hands. By the way, I see people do that. I saw somebody on a TV show on an interview talking about body language. And you know, here is the body language expert and she was using her hands so much that it looked like jazz hands. Don’t do that.

Also, when doing certain hand gestures, make sure to do them from the listener’s perspective. Not yours. This. Is Hand Gesture 401 not 101. For example, if you are talking about the growth of the business, you might naturally do a hand gesture going from your lower left to you upper right. Oh yeah, my business has really grown.

That looks like growth from your perspective but it would be the opposite from the listener’ s perspective. So you reverse it. You would start from your lower right and go to your upper left if you are talking to somebody. It’s also particularly relevant if you are a speaker. Always use the opposite the way the audience is going to see it. The same goes regarding a timeline. For you, the start of a project can only start on the left and for them it starts on the right. So for the listener, the hand gesture should be flipped. So you just use your right hand. The project starts here and then over time, you go to your left. It’s really subtle techniques to help you in your discussions with people. They are sort of hand gestures that you have to get used to in order to do it well.

Here is another question that I have been asked lately by the media. I had someone ask me recently: I have been hearing about so-called power pose or Superman pose when your hands are on your hips may not be as effective as research initially showed. Is that true? The short answer to that is yeah, the power pose is great if you are Wonder Woman or Superman, but mere mortals not so much. I think it just looks theatrical. The power posing is a technique in conversation. It was actually a discredited theory of psychology that was based on.a 2010 study that has even been refuted by one of the original authors of that paper.

Instead of striking a pose, be yourself. Don’t hunch over. Don’t look like a wallflower. Don’t cross your arms. Above all, maintain good eye contact. That’s t really important. The critical part of body language is maintaining good body language. Don’t look around. The room as you are talking to people. It makes them feel as if you don’t care about them. Be interested and look interested if you are talking to someone.

Here is a last point of body language: Personal space is sometimes an issue. How close should you stand to people then you are talking to them? The study of proximics has an application of personal space in a conversation. Personal space varies by culture, however. Be aware of that. Generally speaking in North American cultures, where I am, personal space is generally about an arm’s length away. So don’t get in someone’s face unless you have a relationship with them that would justify that.

In other cultures, when I am in India, personal space is a lot closer. You have to recognize that in different countries and different cultures. Don’t get into someone’s space unless you have a relationship with them and make them feel uncomfortable by standing too close. In this day and age, that is particularly important with the opposite gender.

So just to wrap up, unless you have any questions, Priscilla, body language in networking environments, I believe, can be very important. If you keep these four points in mind and comprehend it while not really overthinking the issues, I think you will do well. The key is to practice, practice, and practice and observe people’s reactions over time.

Priscilla:
Well I have one question, Ivan. What do you do if somebody is moving in on you and the personal space is diminishing, and you are uncomfortable? How do you deal with that?

Ivan:
I tend to back up. You know, I have not historically said that you know, you are getting a little bit in my space. If backing up diligently doesn’t work and they keep in my space, I am usually carrying something, like a notepad or something. I put it in between us. So you know, I will hold that notepad in between the two of us. I may have an agenda or I may have a conference workbook. And I will just hold the conference workbook out in front of me so that it adds another foot in between us and they are not going to come up in front of it and start bending the conference workbook.

I have done that from time to time. Also, I am. Cognizant of the culture. In some cultures, it is just very natural to get. In your space. I am the outsider and I need to learn to deal with it, but it does make me uncomfortable. But backing up and holding something in between us are the two techniques that I use.

Priscilla:
Alright. Well, that is helpful. Thank you!

Ivan:
Sure. You bet. Here is one other thing. If they get really close with really bad breath, if you have mints, I would take one and offer them one. If none of that works, I tend to turn my face so their breath isn’t assaulting my nose directly. I turn my face slightly so it makes it look like I am listening, right? I am putting my ear to them? But the truth is they have a little breath so I turn off slightly to the left.

Priscilla:
Oh deal.

Ivan:
I hope that gives everybody good ideas on body language. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
I think it does. Thank you so much, Dr. Misner. For more of Ivan’s content, go to ivanmisner.com. Thank you so much for listening, This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Body language can be a powerful attractant–or deterrent–in networking. Here are Dr. Misners answers to four questions that the media have asked over the years about body language and networking.

title.rendered: Episode 568: Networking Up, Part 2

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In Episode 530, Dr. Misner talked to Mike Macedonio about “networking up.” That episode focused on hanging out where successful people are and how to connect with them. Today, Dr. Misner wants to share some additional suggestions about how to network above your weight class.

  1. Embrace discomfort. If you’re not uncomfortable connecting with someone, you’re not aiming high enough.
  2. Don’t sell to them. Nothing will shut down your chance for a real connection faster.
  3. Don’t complain to them. You don’t want to be remembered for complaining.
  4. Don’t be a sycophant. It’s fine to acknowledge their work, but don’t overdo the fandom.
  5. Work within the context of the meeting. Connect what’s happening at the moment
  6. Find out what they’re currently interested in. Do your homework before meeting them–or at least look them up on your phone at the event.
  7. Add value. Offer help with something they care about.
  8. Don’t assume they remember your name. They meet hundreds of people at every event. Remind them of your name and how they met you.
  9. Don’t sell to them! It’s the biggest mistake if you’re networking up, and it’s worth repeating.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 568

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Montreal, Canada this week attending a TLC event and also speaking at a BNI event.

Priscilla:
Great! That is a beautiful part of the world right there.

Ivan:
It really is. It’s absolutely beautiful! I’ve been here several times. I love it.

Today I’m talking about Networking Up part 2. So, we did a podcast- it’s podcast number 530, where I talked with Mike Macedonio about Networking Up. We’ve all heard the advice “you become the people who you hang out with,” which means that you not only need to surround yourself with successful people or however you define success, but you also need to continually network up and raise the bar for yourself over time. Now, Mike and I mostly talked about hanging out where successful people are and learning how to connect with those people. But what I want to do today is give you a number of points, about half a dozen or more, in addition to learning how to hang out where successful people are. I want to give you some other points that I think can really make a difference. You know, I’ve always believed that there’s generally plenty of room at the top, but it’s the bottom that’s really crowded. So, here are some suggestions on how you can network above your weight class and move up into networking with stronger people.

In addition to hanging out where successful people are, Podcast 530, the next thing is to embrace discomfort. If you’re not uncomfortable connecting with someone, then you’re not aiming high enough. I’ve been there and I understand this feeling. However, you need to get past that and go talk to them. So if you meet somebody or see someone at an event, it’s really important to go on up and talk to them. Your discomfort may be a sign that this is the exact person you really should be talking to.

Here’s another one: Don’t sell to them. I can’t tell you how many times I’ve met someone for the first time and they start selling to me. I’ve seen the same thing happen when I’ve been with other business people who are far more successful than me. Don’t do it! The old adage, “It never hurts to ask, right?” It’s completely wrong! When you’re networking up, especially if you’re networking up with someone for the first time, a lot of people do that. Don’t be one of the crowd. Don’t sell to them. Don’t say, “This is my one opportunity!” Because it’ll be your last opportunity with that person if you try to sell to them.

Here’s one that surprises people that other people even do this: Don’t complain to them. If you’re networking up with somebody more successful, don’t complain to them. I know that sounds obvious, but I have been both the victim of it and I’ve seen it done to other people. I was with an incredibly successful businessman some time ago, he was meeting people in a crowd and someone he just met went on a rant about some problem with the man’s company. This guy absolutely stood out and was quietly escorted out. You do not want to be remembered for that. If you’re talking to somebody really successful, don’t lead with a complaint. If it’s more important to complain than it is to make a good connection, then do whatever you want. But if your goal is to really network and build relationships with people, don’t do that.

Here’s another one: Acknowledge their work, but don’t be a sycophant. Plenty of people will flatter them, but don’t puppy-dog-lick somebody to death. When you meet somebody that you’re just so excited to see them, don’t puppy-dog-lick them to death. Successful people, you know they are still people, they appreciate knowing that their work makes a difference. Just don’t overdo that. Be cognizant.

A really important recommendation: Work within the context of the meeting. If at all possible, find a way to connect what’s happening at the moment, something interesting in your discussion or setting. For example, I met a well-known international thought leader for the first time at the book signing for speakers at a conference. The problem was the audience was still at dinner and nobody was at the signing. So, I shared a story with him about me at a book signing that I did where the ONLY person who showed up was my mother. Priscilla, you may have heard me tell this story before. She acted like she was a fan and made such a big deal of the signing that people started crowding around the table where I was signing. I had this guy laughing so hard that it helped him remember who I was and what I did, and then he invited me to join an organization of his where I had the chance to network up with many, many people. As a matter of fact, I’m doing that here in Montreal right now with those people.

Here’s another point: Find out what they are currently interested in. This is a critical item. If you know that they are going to an event, do some internet research. If you meet them at an event or you see them at an event, get on your mobile device and just look them up. See what they are working on so that you can have a conversation on that topic- which leads me to the next suggestion:

Add value. If you don’t sell to them, what can you do? Add value to them. Here’s a great real-life example, one of the times that I met Richard Branson on Necker Island, I really wanted to do a video with him- that I put up on IvanMisner.com. I wanted to put it up there. I wanted to say, “Hey, Richard, will you do a video with me?” Instead, I asked about something he was working on, which was The B Team- which was about how business can make a difference in local communities. And he just lit up, he was very excited! I said, “How can I help you with that? What can I do to help you with that?” He said, “Well, you’re in a network, can you get the word out?” I said, “Yeah! Would you like to do a video on it?” And he said yes. So there it was! I’m adding value to him, rather than asking for a favor. And by adding value, it makes that person really want to do that, and I was able to do that. You can go to IvanMisner.com and do a search on Richard Branson and you’ll see the video that I did with him on Necker Island.

Here’s the last thing and then I want to repeat one other: If you meet them a second time, you’re networking up and you’ve already met them once, don’t assume they remember your name. That’s so important. If you meet them and connect with them again, never ever assume they remember you. Always help them out by giving them context of how you know each other or met. Really successful people tend to meet hundreds of thousands and thousands of people, so giving them context can help them jog their memory. So if you meet them in person, give them a reminder of where they met. For example; when I’m networking on an email communication and I’m networking up, I’ll often times send a copy of a photo that may have been taken of the two of us at the event and say, “Hey, we met at this event. Not sure if that jogs your memory but here’s where we met and I just wanted to reconnect.” And that helps people. Don’t ever assume they remember you.

And the last thing I want to share: Don’t sell to them! I call it “sales Tourettes.” People can’t help themselves, they just burst out trying to sell. It’s the biggest mistake if you’re networking up.

So there are about eight different techniques in addition to what we talked about in the last Networking Up podcast. Feel free to share this in your chapters. And if you have something that you want to add through my networking up scenario, please add it here on BNIPodcast.com. I read all of the posts that go up. That’s it for me, Priscilla, unless you have a question or want to add anything.

Priscilla:
No, I think that’s fascinating. I like all of your points. Especially the uncomfortable barometer as evidence that you’re doing the very thing that you’ve promoted here.

Ivan:
Yeah, a lot of people may think, “Oh, that’s Ivan. He can just introduce himself to anyone.” Well, you know what, I suffer from the same issue. I’m networking up and I’m uncomfortable. You just have to work past that. You’ll never have a chance to meet them if you don’t step up and introduce yourself.

Priscilla:
Right! And I love that you suggest reminding them of your name because it’s just really difficult to keep those names at the top of your head- especially if you haven’t seen that person in a while or maybe you’ve only met them once, it’s very hard to remember their name.

Ivan:
Exactly. I had somebody get really mad at me a couple of years ago because I didn’t remember his name. And here’s the kicker, he said, “You don’t remember me?” And at that moment, I was at a PTA meeting. I’m thinking, “I’ve never met this guy at a PTA before. Is he in BNI?” So I ask him if we met at a BNI event and he said, “No. I went to school with you 20 years ago at Gladstone High School.” I’m like seriously? I’m at a PTA meeting, I run a large organization, and this is just completely out of context. And had he said to me, “Hey, we went to school together at Gladstone-” the brain matter would have gone to the right file and opened that. So, there’s just this assumption that people will remember them, even in a different context. Don’t make that assumption.

Priscilla:
Okay, cool. Well, thank you, Dr. Misner! I think that’s great information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner shares more tips for “networking up.” The most important thing to remember when making connections with people who are more successful than you are is “Don’t sell to them!”

title.rendered: Episode 567: Find the Wow in Referral Passing

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tony Kanak joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about an unintended consequence of the success of BNI Connect: BNI members are dialing back the “wow” in referrals passed during the BNI meeting.

When Tony first came into BNI, he was blown away not only by the fact that people were passing business to each other, but by their excitement about doing so. Now when he visits BNI meetings, he often sees people saying “Oh, I put one in the system.” A visitor to BNI isn’t even going to know what that means, much less get excited about it.

BNI Connect is a great thing, but it’s important to maintain the excitement of the referral part of the meeting every week. BNI is a referral marketing organization. Tell the group who the referrals are for and what the person you’re referring is looking for. “Specific is terrific” goes beyond asking for referrals: it also applies to passing them.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 567 –

Priscilla:
Hello and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great, Priscilla. Thank you you so much! We hav a guest on today’s podcast. His name is Tony CONIC?* Tony is a sales coach and trainer. He’s and area director for BNI Delaware Value regions and he’s got a really interesting topic. It’s about finding the WOW in referral passing. It’s sort of a good and the bad of the BNI Connect system- and I wont say bad. It’s the good and the challenges in the use of BNI Connect system. So, Tony tell us what you have in mind here because I completely agree with what you are going to say next.

Tony:
Well, I’ve traveled a lot this last year for work, Ivan. And I always make it a habit to try to fly in early for a meeting, conference, or anything like that so I can go to a couple of BNI chapters and the local market- just to see things from there because we work all around the country. I noticed the difference, between when I came in BNI 15 years ago, and what I see today in a lot of ways but in one specific point–which I think is the most important point of the BNI meeting; when people stand up and give a referral or tell a great testimonial about one of their partners. And the only thing I can equate it too is, the progress we’ve made with BNI connect system over the years, its so good and people are relying on it so heavily, that they’ve dialed back what I call the, “Wow in BNI.”

When I came into BNI, there was no BNI Connect system, as there wasn’t for many of our folks. I was a chamber expert. I was in 4 chambers before I came in, I was an ambassador of the chamber. I just remember running into a financial guy at a chamber function and saying to him, “Hey, we should add a piece to this chamber of commerce. Like a part at the end where we can either get each other into each other’s accounts or bring them in like we used to do when I was in Wilmington, Delaware.” It’s a really tight knit community. Everybody knows everybody. And everybody has everybody’s back and they try to get them in. Unfortunately for me, he was the financial advisor that had tried to get into BNI but couldn’t because all spots were filled. But he did sub for his partner and he knew what it was and he got me an introduction. When I came in, the thing that blew me away was, in a tiny little restaurant in a back room thats mostly dark- picture just lights shining just on tables themselves, I saw people stand up and openly give business to each other and I was just blown away. They did it with excitement! I remember a contractor next to me, Mike. He got two referrals for two kitchen jobs, one right after another in that meeting. I was just floored.
