I thought if they can find this guy Mike two kitchen jobs in one week, they can find me a referral for what I’m doing. I think so many people look past that.

What I see now, is a lot of people will stand up and just say something like, “Oh, I put one in the system.” I don’t think out visitors even know what that means. What is the system, right? So that to me really impressed me. That’s the fault now. I think people are saying that they put one in the system or they use terminology like that. They just put one in and don’t even call the referral! And I think we are missing that component of the BNI meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think you’re right. Not all chapters, but many chapters are doing that. BNI Connect is a great example of the unintended consequences of a good idea. BNI Connect is a great idea. We don’t want to go, “Oh, let’s not use BNI Connect anymore.” What we have to do is make sure to minimize the unintended consequences. So your experience when you came to BNI was, “Wow! Somebody stood up and gave two referrals to this contractor and they give referrals to other people.”  With BNI Connect now, it’s very easy to do what you’re saying which is, “Well, I had 3 referrals I put them in the system, it’s all good.” Oh no, you don’t want to do that because part of the excitement of the process is saying who those referrals were for. If you can give just a little touch of detail, “A friend of mine who’s looking for this.” Or, “A business associate who wants that.” That’s good information that gives it, I think what you’re calling it, the “Wow factor.” Would you agree?

Tony:
Yes, I absolutely agree! And when they don’t default to, “Hey, I put one in the system.” Just say something that you can own, which is, “I have three.” Or, “ I have two.” And that’s all they say! They don’t say, “I have one for Ivan. I have one for Tony. I have one for Priscilla.” They don’t entice any of that! We need to know the detail, and I think that is the “wow factor.” I think that’s what got me on board at BNI. And it’s what has gotten thousands of people into BNI, as well. 

Ivan:
Right. So, I truly believe this is a transitionary period. We are learning how to incorporate BNI Connect into the system. We certainly don’t want to go backwards. But at the same time, we want to make sure that we keep the “wow factor” that you’re talking about which is saying, “Hey, I have three referrals. One is for this person, this person, and this person.” Maybe just a little bit of detail. And whatever we do- Tony maybe you’ve heard any of my previous podcasts where I talk about this; we don’t want Feature Creep to come in, which is what’s happening. Because people will say, “I have three referrals, I put them in the system. But I did a 1-2-1 and I did that and that!” No!  That’s not this part of the meeting! If you really want to say you did a 1-2-1, do it during your weekly presentation. Take time out for that, but don’t take time out of the meeting. We only have one part in the entire meeting for referrals and BNI is a referral organization. 

Toni:
That’s the most powerful part of BNI.

Ivan:
It really is! So have you had this type of conversation with you chapter?

Toni:
Yeah, the chapter is all there, Ivan as you know. So you can be one town over from a chapter you visit and they have the “wow factor.” You can see it’s not related to to any particular territory or anything like that. You know, it’s just certain chapters have really adopted the system, I think. Some just stick to the old school way of making sure that they have excitement. They do it internally. They do a lot of education internally about what that part of the meeting should sound like and look like. You hit it exactly on being able to share a testimonial about somebody and where it came from. Those two components are the thing that spreads business like wildfire in BNI. We’ve got to keep it to to those two things and make that’s the most important thing because that’s where everybody-  when they see it going, Ivan, I think they want to become part of it. We want to get great people who are great business owners into BNI through that excitement.

Ivan:
That’s correct. You know, when you’re at a meeting you have to think, “There’s some visitors here, they have never done this before.” You want to make sure they understand what you are doing. And when you’re really specific, “Here is a referral for so-and-so. This is the contact that I have who I will be introducing you to.” That’s really specific when you say that. But when you say, “Yeah, I put it in the system.” That means nothing. Here’s the thing, this isn’t done on purpose. I don’t think anybody sat down and said, “Now that we have BNI Connect, we don’t have to be excited.” It’s just an easy fallback. It’s like just getting caught in a rut of doing something. What I urge everyone to do who listens to this podcast, is bring that back to your chapter. Talk about that message that Tony’s got. Put the “wow factor” part of the meeting back. 

I’m just really glad you came up with this topic, Tony. Any last closing thoughts before we wrap up?

Tony:
Yes, with closing, I think that one thing people can [do] is if somebody has multiple referrals and they stand up and they say, “Hey, I’ve got three referrals today.” And maybe the chapter makes some noise or maybe they don’t – I hope they do if they’re really excited. But just picture the person should rattle off that, “I’ve got one for Tony. One for Janet.“ That is the indication that they’ve got the “wow.” They are thinking about, “I’ve got to say the people’s names tonight that did the hard work and I opened up a piece of business for this person. This is really exciting!” I think that alone, if they stick to that, they’ll take a huge step back toward the “wow factor” by doing that.

Ivan:
Great advice, Tony. Keep the “wow factor” in the referral part of the meeting. I appreciate you bringing this up. Tony, where can people find out information on your sales coaching and training? What’s your website?

Tony:
It is SalesEvolution.com and we are on Twitter and LinkedIn, we’re all over the place. 

Ivan:
You’ve been a 15 year member and a Area Director for BNI, yes?

Tony:
Yes! For a pretty big region, too. Just wild success, and I really appreciate everything that you did to start this because it made a difference for me and two different businesses now. It’s just been phenomenal!

Ivan:
Well, thank you, Tony. I really appreciate that. And thanks for being on the show today!

Priscilla, back to you!

Priscilla:
Okay, well thank you both! That was really important information. For more of Ivan’s content, go to IvanMisner.com.Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Keep the “wow” in the referral part of your weekly BNI meeting. remember to tell the group the names of the people you’re passing business to and add details to help visitors get excited about how much business is passed in BNI.

title.rendered: Episode 566: Collaboration Using BNI Connect

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Lorrie Nicoles joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about using BNI Connect as a resource for collaboration. Lorrie is working on a book and was looking for other professional writers to interview. She searched BNI Connect and found 90 writers in the US–but only 40 of them had their email addresses listed.

BNI Connect is an incredible resource. Not completing your BNI Connect profile is like taking out a full-page ad in the newspaper…and leaving it blank. BNI Connect isn’t just about getting business. It’s about being available. You can network for knowledge as well as for referrals.

If you don’t complete your BNI Connect profile, you’ll miss out on all kinds of opportunities. If you haven’t done it yet, go to BNI Connect now and complete your profile. Make sure to include your photo and your email address.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 566 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi, Priscilla. I’m in Galveston right now. I have a condo in Galveston and we have an event this weekend in Houston, so I’ll be speaking in the eastern region this week. Part of my travels visiting BNI.

Priscilla:
Okay, well that sounds good! I hear that you have a special guest today.

Ivan:
I do, indeed: Lorrie Nicoles. Lorrie is the founder of Tora Writing Services. She’s the written word consultant and content editor. She calls herself a “word nerd.” I love that, Lorrie. It’s funny! But what I thought what was most interesting about your bio was that you had a career as a Mind Planning Engineer. You basically translated other engineers for the rest of the population through software documentation. That must have been mind numbing and if anything, what you do now has got to be more fun than that. Lorrie, welcome to the BNI podcast show.

Lorrie:
Thank you, Ivan! Yeah I was not in engineering for very long, and the mind planning engineer does not get to live in the urban centers- which is where I grew up. So I left that field. I don’t like to think back and beyond, but living there just was not going to work for me. And yes, translating engineers can be mind numbingly boring.

Ivan:
I can imagine! So we are going to talk a little bit about BNI Connect and how it can be a resource for collaboration. I think this is so important, I’ve talked about it in previous podcasts but you have an interesting story that I wanted you to share. You searched BNI Connect for writing services, talk about that a little bit. What was the journey that you went through?

Lorrie:
Well I am in the process of finishing up my book called, “How To Be A Writer Without Writing A Book.” and what it talks about are different types of ways to make money as a writer without actually writing a story or worrying about publishing. There are lots of different types of writers. I know what I’m good at and I know what I’m not good at. And I wanted to have insight into some of those other specialties that I knew were not my strength. I knew I could’ve researched it, but I also wanted to have quotes and comments from the people who are actually doing that kind of writing. So I got on BNI Connect and I would see all the other folks on BNI services, and because is writing is so cultural, I stuck to the west and that got me to about 90 of them. I went through everybody’s profiles and picked only the ones that had their email address listed. I knew I wanted to make a first contact through email, and that took 90 down to about 40. I could not believe how many people did not have their profiles filled out!

Ivan:
And maybe they’ll have part of their profile filled out, but not their email address. I’m not quite sure why they don’t put their email address in there.

Lorrie:
It was a bit shocking! So I sent out this email saying, “Hey, I’m another BNI member and I’m working on this project. Would you be willing to help me out?” And I got about 20 responses and out of those responses, I got about 10 contributors. I proved the rules of halves and got some really great insight into other types of writing, which isn’t what I specialize in. It was really a great experience! I got to make contact with other writers across the country. I even ended up talking with a writer also here in the San Francisco Bay Area- we still catch up every now and again. He’s a great guy and that was a lot of fun as well. I just had this great experience! And I’m sure I got lost in some spam boxes and so on and so forth, but it was so nice to be able to reach out to other people in BNI and ask for help. Because that’s sort of what BNI is all about. It was a great experience, but it was a little frustrating that I couldn’t ask more people for help be cause their profiles weren’t filled out. I don’t understand why people do that because BNI Connect is such a great resource.

Ivan:
It is! BNI Connect is an excellent resource and there’s no other networking organization like us that has the recourse. None. We now have almost a quarter of a million members. We can connect with our members globally through BNI Connect. The whole idea of being a local business but having a global network is really true. It is mind boggling to me that people don’t complete their profile. I tell people it’s a little bit like taking a one page ad out in the newspaper and leaving it blank. That’s just stupid! Members just don’t think to complete it! And there’s so many opportunities. Sometimes people will say, “Well, I’m not looking to do business in another country.” Well, it’s not just about doing business, it’s about getting exposure and having opportunities to do what you’re talking about. It wasn’t about a referral, but you were quoting these people and they are going to be in your book. That, trust me, I can guarantee anybody that’s great exposure to be written about in a book! It’s just a missed opportunity. And first of all, I’m proud of you for using Connect the way you did, and I’m proud of those people who responded. I would just like to see every member do that.

Lorrie:
Yeah, it’s interesting because currently I am one of those coordinators for my BNI chapter. We are all doing the BNI Passport Program and I do the BNI Tools Mentoring Session. We get together and I go over it in depth about filling out your profile and making sure that all those visible boxes are checked, all of the details because frequently I use this story as an example of it’s not just about getting business. It is about being available.

Ivan:
It is about being available!

Lorrie:
That’s my little success story but I also scratch my head all at once.

Ivan:
It is about being available and there are different ways to network. You can network from referrals, or you can also network using a thing called Knowledge Networking- it’s a network for sharing ideas and resources. That’s exactly the way you were using this. But it is also good for referrals. Sometimes, and I think I’ve mentioned it already, sometimes people say they don’t do business globally. One of my favorite stories is of a BNi member in I think it was Malta, whose mother is from London reached out to him and said, “Hey honey, you’re in this networking group, do you happen to have anybody in London who does landscape?” And here’s a member in Malta that contacted a member in London for landscaping work for local business. But it came from a global referral because of BNI Connect. That landscaping person would’ve never received that local referral had they not completed their profile. So I can’t guarantee that anybody is going to get business or exposure by putting their profile on BNI Connect, but I can guarantee they won’t if they don’t put that information on there.

Lorrie:
Yeah, my sister is out of state and she has learned that whenever she is looking for anything she’s like, “Okay, who in BNI do you know that can do this?”

Ivan:
See that’s the beauty of BNI Connect! You can now use BNI Connect to help make connections with people all around the world. It isn’t available in every country yet or every region, but you can count referrals in most regions now that you can give to other people in other regions. It’s just a win-win to me.

Any last thoughts, Lorrie, before we wrap up?

Lorrie:
Fill out your profile, include your picture, and answer your emails.

Ivan:
All good advice! And you said to include your picture. I’ll never forget the referral that came from somebody in Seattle that needed a photographer in New York, and I talked about this in previous podcasts several years ago, and half of the photographers didn’t include their own photograph. Again, I’m scratching my head, “How could you be a photographer and not include a photograph in your profile?” I think that’s a no brainier.

This is really good advice! I hope everyone who’s listening to this podcast, when they are done listening to this podcast, I want you to go to your computer and complete your profile. You can look up Lorrie and say hi to her! It is Lorrie Nicoles. She’s the founder of Tora Writing Services. Her website is Torawriting.com.

Lorrie, we are going to be looking for your book when it comes out! What is the name of it again?

Lorrie:
“How To Be A Writer Without Writing A Book.”

Ivan:
I’ll definitely be looking for it, Lorrie. Thank you so much for being on BNI Podcast today!

Lorrie:
Thank you!

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great! I just want to mention that Lorrie came to my chapter and talked about her book and how she used BNI Connect. I thought, “Oh my gosh, this is amazing!” Because we haven’t really heard of anybody who has done that before.

Ivan:
That’s right! So this is a referral from Priscilla to me and the BNI Podcast. Priscilla, I appreciate it very much!

Priscilla:
Great! I thought Lorrie would be an interesting guest and it turned out she was!

Ivan:
Yes, indeed!

Lorrie:
Thank you!

Priscilla:
Thank you, Lorrie! And thank you, Dr. Misner! I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: BNI Connect is an incredible resource. Not completing your BNI Connect profile is like taking out a full-page ad in the newspaper…and leaving it blank.

title.rendered: Episode 565: LCDs in Your Weekly Presentation (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 471.

Synopsis

The LCD in the title does not mean “liquid crystal display.” It stands for Least Common Denominator, a term from mathematics. If you can break your business down to its smallest components and focus on just one aspect of your business in your weekly presentations, it works much better than providing a laundry list of things you do, or a vague and meaningless term like “full-service.”

For instance, a real estate agent might do 60-second presentations about first-time home buyers, condos, single-family-homes, investment properties, house flipping, downsizing your home for empty-nesters, buying a larger home for a growing family, the communities you focus your business on, and so forth.

No matter what your business is, you know enough about it to break it down in the same way. If you do a whole series of LCDs over the course of a year, by the end of the year everyone in your chapter will know so much about your business that they can give you great referrals.

Make up a quick list of ideas for LCD presentations right now. Share your list–and your stories of how LCDs worked for you–here in the comments.

(Hear more about this topic in Episode 221: Your Business 101.)

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 471 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Charlotte, North Carolina today at BNI headquarters doing Director Orientation Training. BNI Directors from all over the world come to the global headquarters and get trained. So I am visiting. They go for three days of training, 24 hours. They get out there and hopefully help members worldwide grow their businesses.

Priscilla:
That’s great. So-

Ivan:
Today – oh go ahead.

Priscilla:
I was just going to ask you what this LCD episode is about.

Ivan:
You know, I just realized somebody was asking me about LCDs the other day because I talk about them in Givers Gain. I think it is in our orientation audio as well. In fact, I am sure it is.

So they asked me about it and I said to look up a podcast on it and get more details. Then I looked up a podcast just to make sure it was there and I have never done a podcast where I do a deep dive into LCDs. So that is what today is, to give a little bit more description to people.

LCD stands for least or lowest common denominator. It is actually part of algebra, where you take your least or lowest common denominator when you break a number down to its smallest increment. Believe it or not, this actually works in networking or doing presentations as well.

If you can break your business to their smallest piece, it works so much better in BNI. So you talk about your business and really chunk it down and talk about just one aspect of your business, we have found that it works much more effectively than trying to say everything that you do.

Now, I talk about this in a different variation in episode 221, so if you are listening to this one, go back and listen to 221, where I talk about Your Business 101, where you break it down. The usual term that I use with this is the least or lowest common denominator.

You see, people will come into a BNI meeting and they will do one of two things. The first thing that they will do is give you a laundry list of everything that they do. “I do this. I do that. I cover this. I make this part of my business. Whatever you need, I can do it.” Or they will come in and simply say, “I am full service.” My favorite one is the printers. “I am a full service printer. I can do any kind of printing. It doesn’t matter what you want. I can do it all.”

That doesn’t tell us anything. There is no educational aspect to that. What I urge members to do is take your business down to their least or lowest common denominator. I was having this conversation at a chatper. I had a real estate agent say to me, “I am a real estate agent. What in the world can I do to break my business down into really small chunks?”

I said, “I am not a real estate agent, but I bet I could break it down to two months’ worth of content in 60 seconds.”

She said, “Okay. How?”

I said, “Do you work with new homebuyers? Do you work specifically with people who are new to the market, have never owned a home, and it is their first time? First time home buyers.”

She said, “Oh yeah, absolutely.”

I said, “Okay, that is topic 1. What is it like working with a first time homebuyer? What do you focus on? How do you help that person in a way that is different than maybe other people who have had homes before? Do you sell condos?”

“Oh yeah, of course I sell condos.”

“What are the pros and cons of condos and how can you help somebody who is maybe looking for a condo as opposed to a single family home? And oh by the way, what about single family homes? There is the third one. I am guessing that you do a lot of work with single family homes, so talk about the pros and cons of a single family home versus a condo.”

Then I said, “Do you ever sell any investment properties when people want to buy a home but they want to buy it as an investment property?”

“Of course I do,” she said.

“Okay. There is your fourth one. One week, talk about investment properties. I bet you could talk about- you could probably do a month on investment properties one week at a time, just talking about different kinds of investment properties. You know- multi units, single family homes as investment properties, condos as investment properties. Each one of those could be a different week. What about fixer uppers to flip? Not where you are just buying an investment property to rent, but you are doing a fixer upper. You buy a place, you fix it up and you sell it at a profit. Have you ever done those?”

She said, “Not a lot but yes I have.”

“Okay. That’s a topic. What about people who-” Now, I am having a conversation with her, right? This is on the spot. “What about people whose family has grown up. Their kids are out of the house and they are downsizing? Do you ever work with people like that?”

She said, “Yeah, absolutely.”

I said, “Downsizing, that’s a great topic to talk about if someone actually wants to maybe move up to a better neighborhood but they want to downsize. Or what if your family is growing and you need a bigger house?”

“Of course I do that.”

“Well, that is a topic. What communities do you focus on?”

Now, those of you listening to this podcast may be thinking “this has now relevance to me. I am not a real estate agent.” It has all the relevance in the world to you because you know your business. You can break your business down, just as I did the real estate agent. I am not a real estate agent. I was just able to figure this out.

“What communities do you really focus the majority of your real estate business on?”

She said, “There are about six communities here, six cities, that I do most of my work in.”

I said, “Of the six cities, are there pros and cons of each of those cities?”

She said, “Of course.”

What about a case study? “So and so family came to me.” You don’t necessarily have to name them, but “a family of five came to me and they wanted to list this kind of house.” Maybe if you haven’t done it, start taking photographs of the houses that you have sold. Most real estate agents, of course, have done that. “They came in looking for this kind of house. This is what I could provide them, and this is what were looking for.” Then people can see the types of work that you are doing.

Now, in the last two minutes, we just gave what had to be easily three months worth of presentations. That is what an LCD is. You take your business and your break it down to the least or lowest common denominator. A new home. A condo. A single family home. An investment property. Different communities. Case studies. All of these are breaking their business down to little pieces.

For those of you listening to this podcast, think about your business. As a matter of fact, when you are done with this podcast, just make up a little list. Just do a quick list of your business and how you can break it down into bite-sized, where you are sharp shooting your presentation, not shotgunning you presentation.

Never, ever, ever stand up and say, “I am a full service whatever. I can do it all,” and stand there. You know, maybe you want to start there, but I don’t even recommend it because after a couple weeks of that, they are just going to tune you out. Instead, you just want to hone down and get really specific.

If you really want to do a good job, say, “This week, I am going to talk about this topic. Next week, I am going to be talking about that topic.” So you will have people prepared. They are going to know what your next topic is and they are going to be prepared for it.

Over the course of a year, if you have done a whole series of LCDs, you have taught them so much about your business and given them such a clear idea of how well you know your industry that it makes it much, much easier for people to give you referrals.

Does that make sense?

Priscilla:
Yeah. There is a plumber in our chapter that does that so well. Each week, he comes in with a story about toilets, faucets or hot water heaters. One time, he did a ten minute presentation and brought in all the different little pieces of plumbing attachments that he works with. He just specifies one thing each week. It is very fun.

Ivan:
Yeah. He is doing it the right way because first of all, it shows his breadth of knowledge and secondly, it is almost like training a dales force, which you have heard me say many times. You know, your weekly presentation is about training a referral team, training a sales force. It is not about closing sales. You want to teach people how to refer you. The way to teach people to refer you is just get laser-specific and talk about one element of your business. That is what the LCD is really, truly all about.

Here is what I would love from those of you listening to the podcast today. I would love to hear your story. If you have used your LCD, you have really broken your business down into one little piece and someone then came up to you and said, “Wow. That was helpful. I didn’t know that you did that. That makes it easy for me to give a referral to you.” I would love to hear stories from people who used the LCD process and got a referral as a result of it, because I have seen it happen many, many times. I would love to have that posted here on our podcast.

Priscilla:
Great. Well, thank you, Dr. Misner. Do you have anything else to add?

Ivan:
No, I think that covers it for today. Remember LCD. You want to do this every single week. Break it down. You have to prepare your presentation. Don’t wing it. You know, you have to spend like 10 minutes preparing for your weekly presentations. Not much time, but that preparation rather than winging it will make all the difference in the world in the amount of referrals that you are going to get.

Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, you are so welcome. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you can break your business down to its smallest components and focus on just one aspect of your business in your weekly presentations, it works much better than providing a laundry list of things you do, or a vague and meaningless term like “full-service.”

title.rendered: Episode 564: What’s in the Way Becomes the Way

content.rendered:
Synopsis

When Dr. Misner was a child, his teachers all had the same complaint: “Ivan talks too much.” What his teachers saw as a problem ended up being an advantage. Dr. Misner’s job is to talk to people, and he gets paid well to do it.

The secret here is to take the thing that’s in the way and channel your efforts in a manner that makes that problem part of the solution.

In 1985, Dr. Misner had a massive thing in his way. He’d lost a client and could hardly pay the mortgage, so he started a referral group to help himself and his friends generate more referrals in a structured way. That group became BNI, bringing success not just to Dr. Misner, but to thousands of business owners around the world.

Successful people know how to focus on a roadblock and turn it into an overpass.

What’s been in your way that you’ve turned into an advantage? If something is in your way now, how do you plan to channel it? Share your experiences in the comments.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 564

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I am in Austin, Texas. I have decided not to travel this week. I have spent so many years traveling during my birthday that I decided this year, I am going to stay in town. My birthday is in just a few days. It’s June 30, so I am in Austin, Texas this week.

I’ve got, I think, an interesting topic: what gets in the way becomes the at. You have no idea what I am about to talk about.

Priscilla:
No, I don’t. But first, can I say Happy Birthday?

Ivan:
Yeah. Absolutely. Thank you very much I appreciate it.

When I was in elementary school, I generally received pretty good reports from my teachers. However, one thing came up time and time again, and it was a comment by almost every teacher I had: Ivan talks too much in class. That’s what they all said. Now, my mother had numerous conversations with me about this, but really, it was to no avail. I figured that she thought my grades were pretty good and she generally liked to pick and choose her battles on issues. So she didn’t really push the matter, and so I talked. And I talked and I talked and I talked in class. It showed up on almost all of my report cards.

My teachers felt that it was a problem for me in school. My mother, on the other hand, didn’t give me too much grief on the subject. While the teachers generally thought it was a roadblock to my learning, I think they may have been wrong on that. What my teachers saw as a problem ended up cutting an incredible passion: I talk.

I talk a lot. I talk to individuals, small groups, middle-sized groups, large groups, and massive groups. Any way that you cut it, I am a talker. My job today is to talk to people. In fact, I get paid a crazy number to talk to companies, associations, and organizations. I love to share ideas, I love to coach people, and most of all, I love to inspire people. To do that, I talk. Over the years, I have learned that oftentimes, what is in the way becomes the way.

My talking too much in class was thought of as a roadblock by my teachers. In fact, they almost had me convinced that it was a problem. My mother, not so much. She didn’t see my talking as a big issue and that gave me the freedom to really be myself. Sure, I have probably toned it down a bit, but It wasn’t drummed out of me. I am grateful for that because, despite the fact that people thought that talking was blocking my way, the truth is it would eventually become the way in my life.

I think the secret here for anyone is to take the thing that is in the way – lots of times, it is what people say might be in the way. Sometimes you recognize that it is something in the way. Take the thing that you or others perceive is in the way and channel your efforts in a way that makes that problem part of the solution.

In 1985, I had a massive thing in the way. I had lost a client. I could hardly pay the mortgage on my new home. That was a pretty doggone big thing to have in my way, and I desperately needed more business. So I started a small referral group to help my friends, to help myself, generate referrals in a structured way. And the solution for the problem that was in my way ended up becoming a global enterprise with thousands of groups in dozens of countries around the world called BNI.

I think that successful people sometimes understand that many times, what is in the way can become the way and the key to that is to channel your efforts to focus on the roadblock and making it an overpass. Focus on the roadblock and make it an overpass. Some of us do this unconsciously. However, imagine how impactful this paradigm – if you are listening to this, I want you to sit down and imagine how impactful this paradigm could be if we were just more conscious of it at work in our lives.

I would encourage you to take a moment to think about something that you were told was in the way as part of your life. Has it become the way for you? If so, how? For me, one of the first things in my life that was really in the way was that I talked too much in class. Looking back, I would have to say that it worked out pretty well. What do you think, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think you are right. Can I interject one thing here?

Ivan:
Sure.

Priscilla:
That happened to me. I talked too much in class.

Ivan:
And now you are in studios interviewing people, talking to people. It has become your career.

Priscilla:
Exactly. That’s right. It’s amazing. It’s very valuable what you are saying because it makes you think. Oftentimes, you know, we are discouraged from something that might be our greatest strength.

Ivan:
Right. And it is drummed out of us for one reason or another. What I suggest is if you are listening to this and you find that something has been drummed out of you, let that go and focus on turning that, channeling your efforts to make it work for you.

You know, I would like to tell you that I had this conscious decision that I was going to be a speaker and that I was going to be training people. It evolved over time, but I think it helped me because it hadn’t been drummed out of me. But that doesn’t mean that you can’t bring that thing back. What I would love for people listening to this podcast to do is to really think about what was in their way in the past and are they doing something now today that is a response to what was in their way in the past, or are they doing something today that was in their way? And what can you do to channel your efforts and help take that thing that thing that is a problem and help turn it into a solution, to take a lemon and turn it into lemonade?

So that is my podcast for today, Priscilla. What do you think about people posting? What can we ask people to post – the thing that was in their way and has now become their way? Do you think that’s a good idea?

Priscilla:
Yeah, I think that’s a great idea.

Ivan:
Alright. So if you are listening to this podcast, we would love to hear from you. Tell everyone one simple thing that maybe was in your way in life and how it has become your way in life. And if you are thinking of something that is currently in your way, tell us what you are doing to help that become your way. In fact, those kinds of comments are, I think, valuable for other people to see and I provide you, I read every single post, every single comment that goes up on the podcast. I read them all, for the last how many years, 12 or 13 years that we have been doing this.

So thank you so much. I guess it has been 12 years, 11 years. But thanks so much, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
You are welcome, Ivan. I appreciate this one. It is really valuable. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Successful people know how to focus on a roadblock and turn it into an overpass. The secret is making the problem part of the solution.

title.rendered: Episode 563: The Referral Part of the Meeting

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week we’re revisiting a topic we covered in Episode 551, “Announcing One-to-Ones During Referrals”. Today’s guest, Kip Kint, Executive Director of BNI Utah South, joins Dr. Misner to talk some more about the referral part of the meeting.

In his comment on Episode 551, Kip asked whether it would make sense to include the TYFCB (Thank you for closed business) in this part of the meeting. The problem, however, is that it’s too easy to turn “Thank you for closed business” into “Thank you for giving ME business,” and there isn’t really time to give someone a proper thank-you.

The referral part of the meeting–this 30 to 60 seconds per member–is the whole reason that Dr. Misner created BNI. This part of the meeting is what makes BNI a referral marketing organization. This is why it’s so important to maintain the integrity of this part of the meeting and not allow feature creep.

This is not the “slips” part of the meeting. It’s not the “I have” part of the meeting. It’s the referral part of the meeting. If you don’t have a referral, give a specific, heartfelt and meaningful testimonial to another member. When you give the referral, include some information about how and why you gave this person this referral.

When you’re very focused, you’re more likely to get results. Keep the referral part of the meeting focused on referrals.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 563 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla! Today we’re revisiting a topic that we did some time ago. It was Episode 551, Announcing One-to-Ones During Referrals; I did that podcast. And our guest on today is Kip Kint. Kip and I were talking about this podcast and we came up with some ideas to add to that podcast- because this is such an important part of the meeting. So, I invited Kip to be on this podcast. Kip is the Executive Director of BNI Utah South. He’s been a member of BNI for 13 years. He is a trainer, speaker, and business coach for nearly 20 years. He’s the author of, “You Can If You Will – How to Succeed Through Commitment and Accountability.” He lives in St. George, Utah, with his wife and family.

Kip, this is your first time on BNIpodcast.com, welcome!

Kip:
I’m excited! Thank you for the warm welcome and I’m really excited to talk about this topic today!

Ivan:
Well, we had an interesting conversation about the referral part of the meeting and this particular podcast. Do you want to talk a little bit about that conversation and where it led us in terms of what we should be doing?

Kip:
I don’t know if you recall, Ivan, but there’s always comments that people make on your podcast after you’ve posted them. I was one of those commenters from Podcast 551 because in my mind I’m thinking, “Okay, I think Ivan might have left something out here. What about announcing thank you for closed business during the referral part of the meeting?” Because I was mislabeling it, I was calling it the wrong thing. I was calling it the “I have” part of the meeting- which of course, it’s not. It’s the referral part of the meeting. But you had made this interesting point on Episode 551 about this part of the meeting is about what we are giving to our fellow member. It’s not about what we are asking for. So, I’m thinking – and I even put this on my post on the podcast, “Well, aren’t we giving our gratitude when we say, ‘I have thanked you for closed business?’” You made an interesting point to me when we had this conversation at the national conference, that it kind of ends up being, “Thank you for giving me business!” And it almost tends to comes out that way. In the small amount of time that we do have, there’s not much time for a really great thank you at that point anyway. It really shifted my paradigm about what this part of the meeting is really all about.

Ivan:
I think this dialog is so important, Kip, because I think back to 33 years ago when I started BNI, this one minute- or sometimes if it’s big chapters it’s 30 seconds- this small amount of time was the very reason I created the entire organization. So, this part right here, is why I started BNI. So that we could give referrals to people we trust, and so that we could get referrals from people that we trust. So, this is the referral part of the meeting. And what I was trying to communicate- and I probably could’ve done a better job which is why we are doing this follow up, is that Feature Creep is taking over this part of the meeting. The very reason for our existence, we call ourselves a referral marketing organization, and if we don’t have one portion of the meeting that is sacrosanct for our referrals, then we’re hardly a referral marketing organization.

Kip:
Exactly! You’ll have to forgive me because my kids joke about my analogies, but if this 90-minute meeting is a sandwich that really has one primary meat on it, that meat has got to be the referrals! And everything leading up to it is a big piece bread and anything that comes after that is a piece of bread. We can’t mess around with the primary contents of that sandwich.

Ivan:
Agreed! And without it being maintained well, you basically have an air sandwich. There is no meat! This is why it’s so important to not allow Feature Creep to take over and to really focus on this being the referral part of the meeting. It is not the ‘slips’ part of meeting, or the ‘I Have’ part of the meeting. We do say and use the words, “I have.” The reason we do that is because people used to apologize for not having anything and we’d say, “No, don’t apologize for not having a referral. Give a testimonial, that’s okay!” If you don’t have a referral, give a testimonial. So we started using that phrase, but it’s not the ‘I Have’ part of the meeting or the ‘slips’ part of the meeting. This is the referral part of the meeting. Again, if you don’t have a referral, give a testimonial.

Kip:
You know, Ivan, you said something that really resonated with me, and I think you and I chatted a little bit about this before.  I think I have a sense of why we experience this Feature Creep. It doesn’t mean it should be allowed or continued, but I think I have a sense of why. And it’s because when we stand up in the referral part of the meeting, and we say, “I have a referral.” And we put a referral slip in the basket. Well, we typically put in these other slips too, for One-to-Ones, CEUs, and all of these other things that we report on. I think because the slips are in our hand as we are speaking it, like this tangible thing, it sort of Feature Creeped into, “I have a referral, and I have a One-to-One that I held with so and so, and I have these CEUs that I did.” I think it’s why it happens, but we need to fix it. We need to get back to the original intent of this part of the meeting. “Givers Gain” is not only our motto, but our number one core value in BNI. And accountability is a topic that is so near and dear to my heart, that I wrote a book on it, that is another core value. We need to stand up and be accountable for giving referrals. The average number of referrals worldwide is one per week.

Ivan:
That’s right. But listen, you can’t always have a referral. I agree with you on that! When I was a member I didn’t have a referral every single week. So what do you do then?

Kip:
On the rare occasion that you don’t have a referral, that is when you give that heartfelt and meaningful testimonial. Just one to a fellow member. That is the only other way to actually appropriately give to them in this part of the meeting.

Ivan:
So a lot of people don’t know this, but when I started BNI, I was a management consultant. One of the programs that I used was The One Minute Manager by Kenneth Blanchard and I trained on that. One of the things that Ken said, and this is in the context of the employees; when an employee does a good job, you give them one minute of praising. I remember his words clearly, “It’s got to be specific. If it’s not specific, it has no value.” So our whole idea of the brief testimonial in the 30 to 60 seconds that you have, is to be crazy specific. Give one, and in a future time give another one, but give one and make it really specific. That is One Minute Manager 101. That’s what was going through my mind when I created it in BNI.

Kip:
Ivan, what would you say to those who like to give a referral and a testimonial? What do you think about that?

Ivan:
Look, every now and again there may be an exception. Somebody might say, “Hey, look I was somebody in the audience today. Would you mind giving a testimonial that you were going to give?” So that might be an exception. Otherwise, don’t do it. Pick one or the other. The reason for that is when you give a referral, I recommend you say, “ I have a referral for so and so. I just met this person.” Give a little bit of information so that others can go to school on you. “Here’s how I met them. Here’s how I was able to give you the referral. Here it is.” Even if you have given the referral to the person already, announce it and describe it briefly. There’s power in that. If you have no referral, then do a testimonial. I wouldn’t recommend both, unless there’s some rare circumstance.

Kip:
Yeah, I wouldn’t think that there’s really much time for both if you’re going to give a little information about that referral – which of course, in most cases has been given in advance unless it’s a tier-one referral you’re giving on the spot. Otherwise, you’ve probably already contacted your fellow member and gave the referral, but now you’re telling the rest of the chapter, “Here’s a little info about this referral.”

Ivan:
Absolutely! Listen, I hope this is of value. I think you and I having this discussion certainly has helped me clarify how to describe it to other people, and I hope that those other members and directors that are listening take this information back to the chapter and share it with their chapter. I think this should be an educational minute/few minutes or even a presentation for the education coordinators for the chapter because we must maintain the integrity of the most important part of the meeting, which is the referral part of the meeting.

Kip:
I would call it the most strategic part of the meeting. I’m not sure if you’re the originator of this quote, but I think I first heard it from you, “Culture eats strategy for breakfast.” So the one piece of advice that I want to give to our listeners is, if your culture has become strongly steeped in this Feature Creep of “I have more than just a referral, I have all of these other things”, let strategy lead the way again. Get back to the point of this being about the referrals or a quality testimonial.

Ivan:
Amen, Kip. I couldn’t have said it better myself. I’d love to have our listeners chime in here and talk about the power and the value of this, and maintaining it as the referral part of the meeting.

I really appreciate your time, Kip. Is there anything you would like to add before we wrap up?

Kip:
Just that I’m grateful for the opportunity to talk about this important topic. To some it may seem like a small thing, but when you do all of the small things right, it adds up to some pretty big results. I’m confident that your chapters will see great results as you get back to the original intent of this part of the meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah, that’s a good point about it seeming like a small thing. Sometimes those small differences and changes make a huge difference. When I’m speaking I often talk about the top ten professional golfers in the world and how much they make. On average the top ten professional golfers make about 10 million dollars a year each. Then I’ll ask the audience, what about the top 10 amateur golfers? How much do they make? And the answer is nothing, they’re amateur. But the difference in strokes between the top 10 professional golfers and the top 10 amateur golfers in one game, the difference is only 2 strokes. Two strokes difference between the top 10 professional golfers in the world and the top 10 amateur golfers in the world. To me, what we are talking about here in the referral part of the meeting is knocking a couple of strokes off your game. It doesn’t feel like a big deal, but that small thing will get you very focused. When you are very focused, you are more likely to get results. That’s why I’m a zealot on keeping the referral part of the meeting sacrosanct and not changing it to be other things.

Kip, thanks for being my guest on the podcast! I think this is one of the most important podcasts I’ve done in years.

Kip:
Thank you so much, Ivan.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great! Thank you so much! I would like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening, this is Priscilla Rice. We look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The referral part of a BNI meeting is the reason BNI was created. It’s not the “slips” part of the meeting. It’s not the “I have” part of the meeting. It’s the referral part of the meeting. If you don’t have a referral, give a heartfelt and meaningful testimonial to another member.

title.rendered: Episode 562: How to Get Referrals

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tiffanie Kellog, who holds the record for most frequent BNI Podcast guest, joins Dr. Misner on the show today to share a specific, proactive referral-generation strategy. Here are the steps you need to follow.

  1. Schedule a one-to-one with someone in your chapter that you’re at high credibility or even profitability with, someone you’re willing to share your database with. (If you’re new in the chapter, schedule one-to-ones with everyone in your chapter to establish credibility.)
  2. Go through every person in your database, describe them briefly, and ask whether that contact would be a good referral for the person you are meeting with (e.g. “This is a dentist with two offices in this town,” or “This is a mother with two children under 5”). Select a small number of top choices to introduce to this person. Then switch places and go through your referral partner’s database.
  3. Strategize on the best way to make the introductions. Do you want to send a letter? An email? Hold an open house? Invite the person to your BNI meeting? If there are a lot of potential referrals, spread the introductions out over several weeks.
  4. Start making introductions and check in with each other on how well the process is working.

If this referral-generation strategy works for you, let us know in the comments.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 562 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CAlifornia. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner.

Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Los Angeles at the founding region of BNI this week. I’m going to be doing an Ask A Founder session in the Claremont area, which is in San Gabriel Valley -the founding region of the organization.

Priscilla:
Oh, that sounds great!

Ivan:
Yeah! It’s good to be in my old stomping ground.

Priscilla:
Who is our guest today?

Ivan:
Well we have a guest today, who has been on a number of times; Tiffanie Kellog. She has been a BNI member since 2005. She’s a reoccurring guest on the BNI podcast. This is her seventh guest appearance on the BNI podcast. I believe she now officially holds the record for the most requested guest back on the BNI podcast.

Tiffanie, congratulations! You are also the author of Four and a Half Networking Mistakes and Knock the Socks Off Your Audience. She is one of the top assentive facilitators in the world and she has her own podcast, Brunette Business. It’s found online at TiffanieKellog.com.

Tiffanie, welcome to BNI podcast for the seventh time!

Tiffanie:
Thanks for having me back!

Ivan:
You bet! We are going to talk here about how to get referrals, and particularly a proactive process that you’re recommending.

Now, if you listen to this podcast, you’re listening to somebody who has not only been a member for more than a decade, she’s also a trainer on networking. She knows what she’s talking about here.

So tell us a little bit about how people can get more referrals in BNI.

Tiffanie:
So I want to share a specific proactive referral generation strategy. I know that we’ve got lots of opportunities in BNI to kind of let the referrals come to us, but I think if we had the opportunity to focus and create those referrals, we’ll see amazing results. So the first step in this proactive referral generation strategy is actually scheduling a 1-2-1 with the right members within your chapter. You’re going to want to find somebody that has high credibility or high profitability wise. Somebody that you’d even be willing to open your database to.

Ivan:
How do you do that, Tiffanie if you’re a fairly new member and still want to do 1-2-1’s, what if you don’t have that credibility yet with people?

Tiffanie:
So if you haven’t made it to the high level credibility with the people in your chapter, then you’re still going to want to do the 1-2-1 preferably using the DANCE card work sheets to gain your bio, the Contact Here sheet, the Lasting Customer sheet- that’s part of the Members With Us program, to help move those relationships from visibility into credibility.

Ivan:
Yeah, so let’s say you’ve done that, you’ve got some relationships, and you’re at a point – and this is a key point for BNI members because I’ve had people say, “I’ve already done 1-2-1’s with everyone. What do I do now?” So this really fits there. It’s like you’ve done the 1-2-1’s, you have a relationship, you have credibility, now what you’re talking about is really going to take it to the next level. Am I correct?

Tiffanie:
That’s correct!

Ivan:
Please proceed, I’m sorry to interrupt you.

Tiffanie:
Oh no, that was great clarification! Yeah, this is not going to work for your first 1-2-1 and maybe even your second or third. Once you’ve hit that level of trust with your members, you’re actually going to come to the 1-2-1’s with some form of your database. Whether this is your client roster, everybody that you know in your CRM system, or the chamber directory -if you’re a very active chamber member and the people in there too. What you’re going to do is go through entry by entry, every single person in your database, and describe them to the person sitting across from you. Very quickly you need to say, “Is this a good person for me to connect you with or not?”

Now sometimes when I mention this, there are people that have privacy issues that have attorneys and things of that nature will say, “Oh I can’t share.” We’re not asking your to divulge client information, so you don’t necessarily have to say the name of the person or the company’s name. Just describe, for example, “This is a dentist with two offices and this pound.” Or, “This single mom that lives in this neighborhood that has two kinds under the age of 5.” You’re just kind of painting a picture of what the person looks like to see if that would be a good client or a good referral for.

Ivan:
That’s the way to do it. Also, it doesn’t have to be a client. You could be naming some people that are business associates, neighbors, and even friends. So we’re not talking about just clients here, but I like what you said to just describe the person, not necessarily give any names and then you can take it from there. That was a good suggestion!

Tiffanie:
Right! So all you’re doing is trying to take this large list of, “Here are all the people that I know.” And narrow it down to, “Here’s some people I know that you should know, too.”

Ivan:
Great! What’s next?

Tiffanie:
The next step, you want to strategize on how do you creat the introduction. How do you see if the person has a need of a potential prospect, or they facilitate you getting to know that person that’s on their list. There’s so many different ways to go about doing this. I know that I’m familiar with your Ten Shades of Referrals from many years ago, Ivan. And the incentive programs in, I believe it’s World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. I believe recently you’ve kind of simplified that down a little bit.

Ivan:
I have, yes. I have Five Levels of Referrals, and Ten Shades of Referrals in business by referral in World’s Best Known Marketing Secret. But now we’ve got it down to five, so that it would match the BNI referral slip. But no matter which form you’re using, you’re talking about different levels of a referral. Whether it’s five or ten.

Tiffanie:
Yeah, so it could be, “Let me send a letter on you’re behalf to the people in my network.” If a letter seems a little old school, I do think there’s value in putting it in writing, but you can also send out an email on your behalf. Connect people on social media. Sometimes you can do what we call a Tiered Referral, where maybe I send out a letter, followed by a phone call, and follow that up with a face to face introduction. So, we’re getting multiple touches in creating the introduction.

Ivan:
And we’re not talking about a spam that goes out to a bunch of people, we’re talking about selective people that there is a real legitimate connection with, not a bulk mailing.

Tiffany:
Yes! This isn’t, “Let me send this out to a hundred people in my network.” It could be four that I want to do a layered referral with. Or perhaps you say, “I’m going to have an open house.” And invite everybody on the list to this open house that you’ll be at, which then combines, “Let me build with my network, as well as face to face introduction.”

Ivan:
I love that one! Because then you can make a personal introduction at that event, in particular that would work with people where there’s confidentiality issues like medical and legal.

Great suggestion!

Tiffanie:
And one of the strategies could be, “Hey, let me invite this person to BNI and I’ll create a face to face introduction while you’re right there at BNI!”

Ivan:
I like that one a lot!

Tiffanie:
So the goal is once you come up with a list, you’ve identified who you want to invite, then you want to strategize on, “How do I create the introduction?” Whether it’s electronically or over the phone, face to face is probably the best. So you come up with a plan for each and every person on how to introduce them. And sometimes people may have identified 20-40 names and it can be overwhelming to try to do that much that quickly. So one of the things I like about the strategy is I may come up with those 40 names and 40 strategies on what to do with these people, but it doesn’t mean I have to do them all this week. It could be, “Let me attack 4 or 5 introductions a week, for the next 10 weeks.”

Ivan:
Right. And the key here is that this is a reciprocal process. So while I’m doing it for you, you’re doing it for me . And we’re helping support one another with our contacts and connections.

Tiffanie:
Definitely! And as we’re out there doing this for each other, it is very important that we check in to see, “Hey, what’s going well? Or where are there opportunities for improvement on this referring process?” You really get to know your fellow chapter member when you’re doing this with them and their business, and makes you an amazing referral source. Hopefully they become an amazing referral source for you.

Ivan:
You know, I share this and this is such an effective and positive way to carry the relationship forward, while maintaining that Givers Gain philosophy. “Let’s get together. Let me give you my connections and you give me your connections, and it’s not a sprint or a marathon, let’s spread it out over time so that they can be meaningful connections.” I love the strategy that you’re sharing here!

I hope if you’re listening to this, that you’re taking notes and education coordinators. I urge you to share this at a BNI meeting.

Any closing thoughts before we wrap up?

Tiffanie:
It’s a very simple process. Identify who you want, strategize on how to make it happen, and go out there and execute those referrals.

Ivan:
I think that’s a great strategy! Again, if you’re listening to this, I would urge you to sit down and share it at a BNI meeting. And when you sit down, start thinking about who you’d like to do a 1-2-1 with, where you have built a relationship, and then do this strategy that we’re talking about here. You know you can print out a BNI podcast transcript as well as listening to it. Print it out and create an outline of the things that Tiffanie has talked about here.

Tiffanie, you always have great ideas that you share and it’s one of the reasons I keep bringing you back. You always have something really good. Any final thoughts before I rap up or did we cover everything?

Tiffanie:
I want to ask you guys – the members that are out there and doing that, I’d love to see in the comments how many referrals you’re generating from you’re 1-2-1’s that you’re working on. Just to see it in action and what you’re able to create.

Ivan:
That’s a great suggestion! So post something up here, even if it’s a few months from now, post something up here on this particular podcast and let us know how it’s worked out for you. If something worked out really well, let us know. If there was some kind of challenge, tell us about it!

If you want more information about Tiffanie, you can go to her website: TiffanieKellog.com. Tiffanie, thank you so much for being on my podcast again!

Tiffanie:
Thanks for having me!

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Ok, great! I think that’s it for this week. Thank you both so much for the great information.
I would like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening, this is Priscilla Rice. We look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Tiffanie Kellog shares a specific, proactive referral-generation strategy. Once you’ve established credibility with the other members of your BNI chapter, you can accelerate the referral process by sharing your databases during a one-to-one and developing a strategy for introducing each other.

title.rendered: Episode 561: Is a Facebook Endorsement Really a Referral? (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 467.

Synopsis

This post is part of the Ask Ivan series. If you have a question for Ivan Misner, email AskIvan@bni.com.

Michelle asked this question after discussions with fellow BNI directors and members.

If I stand up as a member and give my weekly presentation and say that my referral request is that you “Like” my business Facebook page, can my fellow members “Like” my business and submit a referral?

To answer this question, we have to return to the definition of a referral. A referral is the opportunity to do business with someone who is in the market to buy your product or service.

As a BNI member, you can redefine “referral” as it applies to you only. For some people, a referral to an unpaid speaking engagement can lead to a lot of business. So is it a good idea to count a Facebook “Like” as a referral?

  • Is it really an opportunity to do business?
  • How would the VP perform the referral reality test?
  • Is it going to generate a “Thank you for close of business”?

Dr. Misner believes a Facebook “Like” is an endorsement, but not a referral. Your group can ask members to “Like” each other’s pages and recognize them for doing so without counting them as referrals.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 467

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am on the road still. I am in northern California at the Transformational Leadership Council meeting. I have mentioned it a number of times here on the podcast. I am a member of TLC, which is the organization which was founded by Jack Canfield, who wrote this book series, Chicken Soup for the Soul. We do these events twice a year. I really learn a lot and come back to BNI with a lot of great concepts from it.

Priscilla:
Great. Well, what is this topic that you have about Facebook?

Ivan:
There is a broader topic in here. That is what constitutes a real referral. The question, as it was asked- it was asked by Michelle. Michelle is a BNI Director. The question is pretty straightforward. It also has an addendum. I will read the whole thing.

Michelle says she has been having some interesting discussions with a few Directors and members regarding referral requests for Facebook page “Likes”. A member is standing up in a chapter and requesting a “Like” for their Facebook page and saying it would be a referral.

“So if I stand up as a member and give my weekly presentation and say my referral request today is that you ‘Like’ my company’s Facebook page, can my fellow members that go to Facebook, ‘Like’ my business, and submit a referral?”

That is the question. I am going to give you the pros and cons to this answer. Many of these were suggested by Michelle and I am going to give you some of those.

Let’s first start with talking about the definition of a referral. A referral is the opportunity to do business with someone in the market to buy your product or service. I will repeat that. A referral is the opportunity to work with someone, to do business with someone who is in the market to buy your product or service.

Here is the caveat that many people have heard me talk about that makes this topic come up. That is you, as an individual member, may redefine that as it applies to you only. So, Priscilla, I can’t redefine that as it applies to you, but I can redefine that as it applies to me.

I will tell you where this stems from. This stems from chapters- and I did this myself when I was a business consultant and I was a member of BNI. I took a referral for a potential speaking engagement as a referral. I said, “Look, if you could refer me to an organization that would like to bring me in” – and at the time, I did free talks to service clubs. “If you give me a referral to a service club to go speak, I will count that as a bonafide referral.”

Why? Because at the speaking engagements, I got a ton of business as a result of doing these free speaking engagements.

So the short answer of this is you can redefine it as it applies to yourself. But let’s talk about whether it is a good idea or not. We will begin with some of the reasons why it might be a good idea. The first is that the member’s redefining the definition of a referral for themselves only. They can never apply it to someone else. That sometimes gets a little tricky, but that is one.

Next is referrals really are in the eye of the beholder. You know, some professions will ask for a referral- like, for me, a non-paid speaking engagement back then was great.

Facebook pages need a specific number of “Likes” to get the analytics for postings, and sometimes people judge a Facebook page, a business page, by the number of “Likes” they have. For example, Michelle said that if you are looking for an auto detailing company in the area and it has 10 “Likes”, if there is another one with 900 “Likes” in the area, you might go with the one with 900 “Likes”. Frankly, I try to pick the one that somebody I know personally refers them, but I get it why the likes are important. I really do get it.

So those are some reasons why you might say yes. Let me give you the reasons for no and then I will give you my opinion. This was an “Ask Ivan” question, so I am going to give you my opinion.

The reasons for no is, is it really an opportunity to do business? I think it is clear. No, it is not, but there is that caveat that you can redefine that for yourself.

How would the VP do the referral reality check? That would be tough.

And the “Like” isn’t going to generate any thank you for closed busness. That is true.

Here is my opinion on this. My recommendation on this is two fold. First and foremost, if this is really an issue in your chapter, the membership committee should get engaged with it and have a discussion about whether this is a legitimate referral or not. Get the membership committee involved. My personal recommendation is no, this is not a referral.

It is an endorsement, in the same way that I for many years have said if you do business with somebody, ask them to write up a letter, add their name to it, or edit it. You know, ask them if they will write it up themselves. That is even better, but busy people sometimes don’t have time to write it up.

I have asked people, “What did you like most about working with me?” They gave me three or four phrases. Then I wrote up something with those three or four phrases and gave it to them.

To me, that is an endorsement. That is a thank you. I don’t think it’s a legitimate referral, but I leave that up to the individual member and the chapter membership committee. My recommendation is don’t use it for that. It, I think, devalues what a referral is.

The speaking engagement led to bona fide thank you for closed business that ended up being trackable. I could track how many referrals I got for a free speaking engagement and how many clients I got. I always came back and thanked the member that gave me that free referral, which was my marketing technique for generating business, and how much business I generated from it.

You can’t generate that from this. So my advice to all members is make a “Like” an endorsement and a thank you, not a referral.

Your thoughts, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
I think it’s more of a branding kind of activity. You are helping that person in terms of how the public sees them. It is not going to lead directly to a financial gain, so I think you’re right. It’s not really a referral. It’s just an endoresement.

Ivan:
I think it is, and I think as BNI members, we should support each other. This is an easy, easy, easy thing to do. I have seen chapters where they have had a “Like” month where they ask everybody this month. “Here is a list of all of our websites and this month, a little at a time- you don’t have to do it all at once- can you go ‘Like’ everybody’s page?”

I have seen that. What a great team building thing where you get everybody to support one another and help one another. You know, I saw one chapter that said when someone has done everyone, drop an email to the vice president so the vice president can say, “So-and-so and so-and-so have ‘Liked’ all of your pages. Let’s give them a big round of applause.” And you recognize people who have completed the process.

It is a real team building exercise more than a referral. That would be my advice, Michelle. I hope that answers your question.

Priscilla:
Perfect. I think that is great. Thank you so much, Dr. Misner. Well, I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: A Facebook “Like” is an endorsement, but not a referral. It’s okay to ask members to “like” each other’s pages and thank them for it.

title.rendered: Episode 560: I Hit HIDWAL

content.rendered:
Synopsis

In the book Good to Great, Jim Collins opened up a whole new paradigm by explaining that bad is not the enemy of great: good is the enemy of great. People who want to be more successful but aren’t committed to making a change in their circumstances become victims of metastatic mediocrity.

This is “I Hit HIDWAL” syndrome: I’m Happy in this Hole, and I Don’t Want A Ladder.

If you offer someone a referral to a person who could help them or an idea about how they could grow their business, and they start telling you all the reasons that couldn’t work, envision a great big sticker on that person’s forehead that reads “I Hit HIDWAL.”

You can’t help people who don’t want help–or who don’t know they need help. But you–and BNI–can help the people who are ready and willing to be helped. When you meet a propsect, find out whether they want that ladder before you try to offer it to them.

If you’re in a hole and want to get out, find a mentor, even if that mentor is a book or a course. Don’t accept mediocrity when excellence is an option. Keep looking for ladders.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 560 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. I bet you are very confused about the title of this topic.

Priscilla:
I have no idea what HIDWAL is.

Ivan:
I promise everybody it is not violent. It is absolutely not violent. It is an acronym, so let me explain a little bit. In the book Good to Great, Jim Collins opened a whole more paradigm for many people. He showed us that bad is not the enemy of great. It is good that is the enemy of great. Everyone can recognize when something is really horrible. It is in fact good that is the enemy of great performance. You’ve all heard the expression, “It’s good enough. Things are okay. Hey, it’s not bad, doing alright.” I call this metastatic mediocrity at work.

Priscilla, I love what I do. I am passionate about helping people improve their business, networking efforts, and be successful. While doing this, I sometimes run across people who want to be successful but they aren’t really committed to making a change in their circumstance. They have what I call a success disconnect. They want to be more successful but for some incomprehensible reason, they don’t see a connection between their desire for success and the behavior they are embracing.

On one hand they say they would like to be making more money and then a few months later, they will say things which will indicate that they are uncomfortable making the necessary changes to make that happen. This is my absolute favorite success disconnect statement, Priscilla: “You don’t understand. This won’t work here because,” and then fill in the excuse de jour.

Over the years I have found that good enough eventually leads to metastatic mediocrity. So I call this condition “I hit HIDWAL” syndrome or “I am happy in this hole and I don’t want a ladder.” Like that, PRisiclla?

Priscilla:
Yeah.

Ivan:
I’m (happy in this hole) and I don’t want a ladder. Now, you may be listening to this podcast and you may be thinking this is a crazy statement. Give it some time. I promise you, you will be out talking to someone in the future and they will be complaining about their circumstance. You may offer them a referral to someone with ideas that will help them or you may give them some ideas of your own that may help them, and they will tell you all the reasons those ideas won’t work for them.
