At that moment when, Priscilla, this happens to you, at that moment, I want you to stop and think about this podcast and envision a great big sticker on that person’s forehead that reads, “I hit HIDWAL.” Yes, I am happy in this hole and I don’t want a ladder.

Now, we all know that they may really need a ladder. They just don’t want a ladder. My entire professional career has been dedicated to those who want a ladder. I want to work with people who recognize they are in a hole and they want out. I was in a hole when I started BNI. I wanted out. I wanted referrals. I have also learned over time that when it comes to taking advice, some will, some won’t, so what. Not everyone is in a place where they recognize they even need the ladder. Before they can want it they need to recognize that they need it. If they don’t recognize that they need it, then offering them help or a ladder will be of no use whatsoever.

I have also learned that there are ones that think the hole is the natural state of things. They feel comfortable with there they are. They have become so accustomed to the great big hole they reside in that they think it is just part of the landscape.

I can, however, help the ones who recognize their condition and know they want out. More importantly, they not only want out of the hole they are currently in, but they will do just about whatever it takes to get themselves out of that hole. We can only help those who are ready and willing to be helped.

As an entrepreneur in your profession, if you are listening to this podcast, you will meet people who need your help all the time. My advice to you is figure out if they are ready for the ladder. If not, let them know that you are ready for them when they are ready for you. And move on to somebody who desperately wants that ladder that you are going to send down to them.

Good is the enemy of great. I want you, if you are listening to this podcast, to think about the people you know. Think about your life. Are you in a hole? DO you know people in a hole? There is a way out. I promise you. Find someone who can be a mentor for you. Find someone who can be a coach for you. Even a virtual mentor or virtual coach in books and videos can be a great start.

When I started BNI, they didn’t have podcasts. Everyone has podcasts and YouTube videos with so much content. Use that as a virtual mentor and find people who can personally help you. Find someone with the ladder that is needed to get out of that hole, and start climbing out to success.

I have said it many times, Priscilla, and I really mean it. Why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option? Excellence is an option. If you are in that hole, find someone with a ladder. If you have that ladder, find people who need it. Help other people achieve success. For those people who are happy in that hole, there is not much you can do for them until they are ready.

What do you think? Do you like I hit HIDWAL better now?

Priscilla:
Yeah, now I totally understand it. Sometimes people just want to complain. They don’t want a solution.

Ivan:
Yeah. It amazes me that sometime people complain as tough it were an Olympic event. It is like a competition and it doesn’t do much good to complain – especially people. I have complained about stuff, but generally speaking, with an end in mind. I have this problem; how am I going to fix this problem? So I am looking for a solution. Those people I am okay with because they then listen, even if they don’t necessarily agree, they hear you out. They want advice.

I think back – this is really off subject, but I think back to my health issue. I was in a hold I needed help. I went to so many people and asked their advice. Many of them who were giving me advice on how to address the issue, I was like, okay, that is really good to know. I am never going to do what they did. That’s not the ladder I want. I found someone – multiple people – with ladders who would help me out in a way that worked for me.

So it is okay to find one that works for you, but keep looking and get out of that hole. If you really want to be successful, I think you are constantly looking for ladders. I have been doing this a long time. Even though I don’t think I am necessarily in a hole, I am always looking for ladders to take me up to a higher level and be a really successful person.

So look for those stickers on people’s heads, Priscilla, and I want to you repeat it in your mind, “I hit HIDWAL” when you see those stickers.

Priscilla:
Okay, that’s great. Did you invent that?

Ivan:
I did. Well, I heard variations of the phrase. I wanted some acronym to kind of sum it all up so I just placed it with a little parenthetical “and” in the middle. I write an article for Entrepreneur that published a while back on the topic. I though it would make a great podcast.

Here is how it is great for BNI members. If you are listening to this podcast, think about how you can get out of whatever hole you might be in. If you are an Education Coordinator, share it with members. Share it with the intent of solutions. It is not just about recognizing that you are not where you want to be. It is about finding the solution to get out of that hole and get to the place. It’s about solutions and not just about problems, but first you have to recognize the problem. Then find the solution.

That is it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay. That was great, Ivan. I appreciate it. Well, I would just like to remind the listeners, that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: You can only help people who are ready and willing to be helped. When you meet a prospect, find out whether they want a ladder before you offer to help them out of the hole they’re in. And never stop looking for ladders to take you to the next level. There’s no reason to accept mediocrity when excellence is an option.

title.rendered: Episode 559: Don’t Thank the Speaker (Instead of Making a Referral)

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Frank De Raffele, Executive Director of BNI Hudson Valley and one of the co-authors of Business Networking and Sex, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast this week to talk about who should thank the speaker who gives the feature presentation, and when.

In the BNI Agenda, it says that the president of the chapter thanks the speaker at the end of the feature presentation. Some chapter presidents go one step further and highlight something they learned during the presentation when thanking the speaker, which is a great thing to do.

Thanking the speaker is a good thing to do. The problem, Frank explains, is when thanking the speaker creeps into the referral part of the meeting. As Dr. Misner said in Episode 552, the referral and testimonial part of the meeting is for passing referrals. This is the time during the meeting when we celebrate our members’ achievement in passing referrals.

If you’ve referred the speaker or used her services and want to give a testimonial, that’s fine. But don’t miss the chance to tell your chapter that you’ve been passing referrals, even if you’ve done them on BNI Connect.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 559 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in Austin, Texas this week, Priscilla. I am not traveling for a change. I have a guest today who is a really good friend, Frank De Raffele. He is a New York Times bestselling author, video talk show host, professional speaker and he is an Executive Director of BNI Mid-Hudson Valley. He has been a member and Director of BNI for over 25 years. Frank and I co-wrote along with Hazel Walker, that title called Business Networking and Sex: Not What You Think, about the difference between men and women in how they network. We are working on a new book together, which I am sure we will be talking about as time goes on. Frank, welcome to the BNI Podcast.

Frank:
Well, thank you. It is great to be here. You know, it is so funny. My favorite number is 559, and look, I am on the 559th podcast. Go figure.

Ivan:
Perfect. We have a controversial sounding topic. It might actually be controversial, but it’s “Don’t thank the speaker.” Let’s first begin with who should be thanking the speaker because we do believe the speaker should be thanked after they do their featured presentation. In the agenda, it says that the President, when the speaker or speakers are done, should thank the speakers for speaking that day. We even take a step further, which I don’t think is done enough, where they pick something that the speaker said that they didn’t know and they highlight that. They may say maybe you knew that about the the speaker. Think about something that you didn’t know so that you could refer them. It should be the President.

Now, just a few weeks ago, I did podcast 552, about not reporting thank you for closed business during the referral part of the meeting. Frank, you want to talk about something else that has creeped into the most important part of the meeting – the referral part of the meeting and that is thanking the speaker. Do you want to talk about that?

Frank:
Yeah. I will tell you how this came about. I was at a chapter probably about a year and a half or two years ago. It was a chapter with probably about 50 members in it. At the end, they were going around and doing the referral testimonial portion of the meeting. I go around and am counting the number of people who stood up and said they had a referral for somebody. It was five. At the end, I was saying there are five referrals crossing the table today out of almost s 50 member chapter? That doesn’t make any sense.

Everybody stood up to say, “I want to thank John. That was a great presentation. Fantastic.” Everybody was thanking the speaker for what he was doing, but nobody was giving referrals. I was going, wow. This chapter needs help.

I went to the Vice President and said, “How many referrals were passed today?”

He said, “Uhhhh, 27.”

I said, “27 referrals you got today?”

He said, “Yeah.”

I went to the membership committee after and said, “We have a problem. Five people said they had passed referrals but 27 people passed referrals today- but they didn’t talk about it. They thanked the speaker. No more. We are not going to do that anymore.”

It is almost becoming the easy thing to stand up and talk about versus standing up and either giving the referral or testimonial for somebody. So we put that in place and said no more thanking the speaker in terms of this part of the meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah, again, for those people who are apoplectic on us right now, we have the President thank the speaker. But do you really need 40 other people thanking the speaker? No.

Frank, I think back. In 1985, I started BNI for referrals and I discovered that testimonials were as valuable or more valuable. A great testimonial can lead to multiple referrals.

Frank:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
This part of the agenda is for giving referrals or a testimonial for a member. If you don’t have a testimonial for a member, give a testimonial for BNI. That is what this part of the meeting is. For those of you listening to us, go back and listen to 552 because Feature Creep has taken place in BNI, where we are starting to add things in.

Before recording, Frank, we were talking about one of the reasons that we think it is happening, and that is BNI Connect, which is fantastic but it has evolved into something because everything is online. Do you want to talk about that?

Frank:
Yeah, I think the technology we have is great in that we are passing referrals and doing that online makes it more efficient, makes it easier. We are tracking everything, and that’s fantastic. But what is happening is we are not seeing the visual part of that during the meeting, the part standing up. When I first started in BNI, it was all about you stood up, walked across the room and handed it to somebody. There was a great visualization dynamic about that. So now that it is not happening, it feels like people are forgetting about the referral part.

When I talked to this leadership committee – and eventually, I talked to all the leadership teams – I said, “Look, this is the celebratory part of the meeting. This is why we are in BNI. We are here, we work very hard. It is not easy to get a referral for somebody. We go through a lot of energy to try to get one for somebody. Then we give one to somebody and we don’t even acknowledge it. That’s the big part of the meeting. Let’s celebrate the things that we are doing here, which is giving referrals or giving testimonials to people. But number one is with the referral.You have to talk about the referral, how you got it, tell the story. It’s an educational process. It’s a celebratory process.” We seem to have been losing that. I just felt like wow, I couldn’t believe this when I saw it, and you know what, Ivan, it reminded me of? When somebody stands up and they thank somebody for a referral. I said don’t thank somebody for a referral. Give a testimonial to that person. It is much more valuable. So it is the same thing- let’s give a referral or let’s give a testimonial for a member in the chapter, a single member and if that member happens to be the speaker that day, then great. You are giving them a testimonial about who they are, what they do, how they deal with their clients, something that they have done for you – not about their presentation but about who they are as a professional.

Ivan:
Right. And maybe they spoke and now is a great opportunity for you to give a testimonial about the quality of their work that they did that you experienced. That’s fine. But oh my goodness, hearing somebody stand up and person after person thanking the speaker completely misses the celebration that you talk about. I never thought about it in that term, but that is what it is. It’s time to stand up and go, “I have a referral or a testimonial about a great member in the chapter.”

You are right- with BNI Connect, because we are doing it online – there is this concept in management theory called the unintended consequences of a seemingly good idea. Well, it is a very good idea to use BNI Connect to give all of these referrals, but one of the unintended consequences is that we have chapters not celebrating the referral part. So we need to get back to that. That means print out that referral you have given. That may mean go old school and actually fill out a referral slip. Or it may mean, as Priscilla mentioned before we went on air, Priscilla, in your chapter, they put in a business card. They say, “I have a referral for so-and-so. This is what it is.” They describe it and then they put in a business card. Is that right?

Priscilla:
That is right. At the end of the meeting, they take back their business card and it saves paper, which is why we do it that way.

Ivan:
Okay, well, keep it in, take it out, I don’t care, but taking it is out is part of what you say, Frank, the celebration recognizing the referrals. Without that, what happens is there is empty space. With empty space, people fill it in. And what are they filling it in with? Thank you for closed business, thank you to the speaker – or it is a full moon on a Tuesday, or it is going to rain. They just start putting stuff in. That is what is called Feature Creep.

Frank:
What is also happening is that referral part of the meeting- as we all know, the referral part of the meeting is as much about education as anything else. If I stand up and give that referral and how I got the referral, that is part of the educational process. If I am giving a testimonial, that is part of the educational process.But we also have to understand people learn differently, right? Some people are visual learners, some are auditory learners, some are kinesthetic learners. So if we can actually still give out the referral slip- even though we have done it online and done that – now, you have a visual for people in the room identifying with people are giving referrals. You have that part with that, just like the tactile part, where, ok, somebody is giving a referral. For every room, it creates more interest because you are using a little more stimulation and by using different mediums of communication about the referral and the testimonial.

Ivan:
Especially the visitors. Those visitors are sitting there and they see five referrals given, when in fact, almost 30 were given, the power of standing up for the visitors there and seeing one person after another -“I have a referral.” “I have a referral.” “I have a referral.” “I have a referral.” “I have a testimonial for [so-and-so].” “I have a referral.” There is power to this, everyone. This is a must-listen podcast for every member of BNI.

Education Coordinators, take this podcast to your chapters. Talk to them about it. Talk to them about this and episode 552. We have to get back to the process that is, as you say, Frank, a celebration of the referral portion of the meeting, the most important part of the meeting, the reason I created BNI.

Frank, I am so glad that you brought this up? Any closing comment before I wrap up?

Frank:
No, I would just say that I agree with you. I think this is so important to Education Coordinators to bring to a chapter and have this an an ongoing subject matter. If we don’t do that, we are going to end up changing what the meeting is all about. We have to make sure that we are doing that celebration and we are recognizing people – and we are making sure that the chapter is as successful at it can be.

Ivan:
Great suggestion. I appreciate you bringing this topic. I look forward to having you again on the podcast. We are working on a new book together, and I am sure we will do a podcast as soon as that comes out. Thanks, Frank.

Frank:
Thank you so much. I appreciate being here. I really do. Thank you.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Thank you so much, both of you. That was very interesting. I am sure my chapter is going to be interested in hearing the topic. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: The referral part of a BNI meeting is meant for referrals and testimonials. This is your chance to tell your chapter you’ve been passing business to other BNI members. Let the chapter president thank the speaker at the end of the feature presentation, and keep this time for referrals and testimonials.

title.rendered: Episode 558: BNI—The Ultimate Referral Source

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Vickie Wacek, Executive Director of BNI Vermont, joins Dr. Misner on the podcast today to talk about how everyone can use BNI, even those who aren’t members. Why trust an algorithm to refer you to a service provider when BNI already vets its members and holds them accountable to BNI’s core values? BNI is the relationship version of Angie’s List, the ultimate referral source for everyone.

When you tell people in your community about BNI and your BNI chapter, don’t just talk about the benefits of BNI membership. Talk about how they can find qualified professionals for any job by going to the regional BNI website.

Not everyone you meet would make a good member of BNI, but everyone you meet can benefit from knowing about BNI. Take the opportunity to be the connector. Thanks to BNI Connect, you can refer people to BNI members anywhere in the world.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 558 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, thank you. I have a guest on the podcast today, Vickie Wacek. Vickie has been involved in BNI since she was 24 years old. She is a charter member of one of the first chapters in Vermont. Since 2012, she has been the Executive Director of BNI Vermont, and she has taken the average membership from under $8,000 per seat to over $36,000 per seat in Vermont. Vickie is a professional opera singer.

Priscilla, I saw Vickie sing at the US Conference less than a month ago. Vickie, you are amazing! You are amazing. When I heard them announce your name and you went up there and started to sing, and you sang the Star Spangled Banner, I had goosebumps. I looked to my right and said to somebody, “Is that Vickie? Vickie Wacek? Seriously, is that her?” You are an amazing singer. You did a better job than almost any professional singer I have seen do the national anthem. I am in awe of your singing ability and I just know that has nothing to do with what we are going to be talking about today, but I just wanted to tell you that publicly, that you are an amazing singer.

Vickie:
Thank you very much!

Ivan:
My pleasure, I did have goosebumps. And you are also a great BNI Executive Director, and you have a topic that you recommended. It is a little different than other topics that we have done on the podcast, and that is using BNI as a referral service and recommending it not just for members to give referrals, but telling the public to use BNI to generate referrals.

I think it is important to teach our communities that BNI is not just to join but you can use BNI to find referrals for your business. Talk a little bit about that, what you had in mind for this.

Vickie:
Well, I figured this out when I was in my 20s and had a membership. I remember a fellow BNI person saying to me, “BNI is for everybody. Everybody can have a membership.” I remember thinking no, that’s not right. Over the years, what I developed both through my membership and being an executive, is that treating everybody the same way when they talk about BNI is doing a disservice to the organization because not everybody is going to use it for a membership.

What I have learned over the years is when somebody asks me about BNI, I have learned to discover whether or not they are somebody who would be interested in membership or whether or not they would be interested in the connections the group could provide. It made me think of what a lot of us know about Angie’s List or Home Advisor. We think of all the tradespeople, and we go online and we can use these algorithms to find the right person to fix our roof or paint our house.

But you don’t need algorithms because BNI does it for you. We already know BNI vets the applicants for you. We hold them accountable to their professionalism year after year after year. We hold them to our core values that are even beyond painting a house well but being a good professional and being passionate for it.

So it has been about teaching people to stop constantly discussing the membership opportunity and getting people to say, “Hey, we are a vetted group of people in the community that you can rely on. Just hop onto our website. We are Angie’s List on steroids.

Ivan:
I love what you said about it is not just about the algorithms, and I couldn’t agree more. It’s not about the algorithms. It’s really about the relationships and that is he big difference between an online referral service, which is ironic because it is not really a referral but a form of advertising in a way – an online service versus BNI, which is all about relationships that you can do online using BNI Connect, but it is really wall about relationships. it works best almost just asking a BNI member if you know one, asking them about the relationships.

So you say that it is important to BNI members to communicate with no members about the beneficial use of BNI in their personal and professional needs? Do you want to talk about that a little bit? You mentioned clients, friends, families as part of that spoke.

Vickie:
Yeah. You know, Ivan, you and I are in the same  role. It is about getting members as many benefits as possible without spending more time or without spending more money. How do we help our members make more money? Well, we teach the community to come to the regional website to use their services. If we get the communities to be knowledgable about us like they are with Angie’s List and Home Advisor, and those kinds of highly marketed things, and people start to get referrals not just from their referral partners, these people they spend week after week with, but someone in the community starts reaching out to them and says, “Well, you know, I heard that you guys are vetted, and I need an IT professional. I need a tire crafter. I need a candle maker.” Whatever it is.

Suddenly, BNI members are going, “Wow, I get referral introductions, I get referred to clients, and then I get these out of the blue referrals because people know that I am in BNI and they just go to the website, and they use it that way.” What an amazing experience for an individual involved in BNI when they get almost no paid experience, people calling on them because they are part of BNI!

Ivan:
You know, let me get real old school on you, Vickie. WhenI started BNI in 1985, at that point I had not really thought about it being a global organization or even having multiple groups. One of the things that I did was actually sent out a letter. This is real old school. I sent out a letter that said I really believe in a referral based business and if you are looking for any of the professions listed on the attached page or enclosed page, please give me a call and I will put you in touch with them. Then I had a list of all he members who were in my one BNI chapter, only one. And I just listed all of the professions. What was amazing about that was that I wanted people to call me so I could make the introduction. I didn’t just list everybody’s name and phone number. I just listed the professions. That’s it, so they would have to call me and I would be the connector. I ended up generating a lot of referrals for people not because I knew so many people. I just knew a few people well and I told a lot of people about those individuals.

Today, in BNI, with BNI Connect, the technology we have, the increased size of the chapter, the ability to make those kinds of connections is far greater, and I think we should still take the opportunity to be the connector and tell people about he opportunity the way you described and the way I did years ago but we haven’t focused this much on. Would you agree?

Vickie:
Absolutely. There is one other thing I would like to mention about this. I bet you have run into this in your years. Members say, “BNI just wants us to invite more visitors and have more people join.” By teaching our members don’t always invite for membership because not everyone is a good fit- teach our members to have that double dialogue in their head. One way to invite is for membership. Another way to invite is to say, “I have this group of reputable, vetted professionals. We are on the website anytime you need us.” You no longer have to pitch the membership every time you talk about BNI.

If you are talking to somebody who is chief of police, you can say, “If you have a bad back, we have a massage therapist, but there are groups all over the area.” It’s teaching people, our members, not to just sell a membership. We don’t want them doing that anyway, but give them another avenue to have a really wonderful dialogue with somebody that doesn’t result in a membership, and not all situations result in that dialogue, membership.

Ivan:
You are absolutely right. I had a BNI member in Malta who did just that with her family. She told her family, “Whenever you need a referral, whenever you need a service, look it up on BNI Connect or give me a call.”

One day, her mother gave her a call and said, “You keep talking about this BNI thing. I need a landscape designer to help landscape my yard. Can you recommend somebody?” She got on BNI Connect, referred her to somebody in London. The person did such a great job that her mother called her like three weeks later and said, “I need a handyman. Do you have one of those handymen people in BNI at all?” Sure enough, she gave her another referral. Here, a member in Malta was giving referrals to BNI members in London and the reason that happened is she was telling people to use BNI as a way of getting referrals for your business, qualified referrals that have been screened for membership and then have to continue to do a good job in order to stay in a BNI chapter.

I love your suggestion with this. We are the relationship version of Angie’s List.

Vickie:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
Any closing thoughts, Vickie, before we wrap up?

Vickie:
Not that I can think of. This is a perspective I have been talking about for years, especially as an executive. I get to speak in front of lots of different groups and not all of them are a good fit for BNI so this perspective has helped me not come off as salesy and provide a benefit to the many, many groups that I speak to across the state of Vermont. I hope that members, consultants, and executives all over the world who hear this use it as a new tool to communicate openly and honestly with everyone in their life.

Ivan:
Vickie, this is a great topic, and it is actually different than a lot of the stuff that I have done before. I appreciate you bringing it to my attention and being a guest on the BNI Podcast. Thank you so much for being on it. Thanks for the great work you do as the Executive Director in Vermont.

Vickie:
Thank you, Ivan. It has been a pleasure.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, that was great. Thank you so much to Vickie and to Dr. Misner. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thanks for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Vickie Wacek joins Dr. Misner on the podcast today to talk about how everyone can use BNI, even those who aren’t members. Why trust an algorithm to refer you to a service provider? BNI is the relationship version of Angie’s List, the ultimate referral source for everyone.

title.rendered: Episode 557: Making a Good Living While Serving the Greater Good (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 400.

Synopsis

In honor of the 400th episode of The Official BNI Podcast, Dr. Misner chose to discuss BNI’s core values.

We’re all in business to make a profit, but business should serve a greater need. Business can be honorable and noble and make a difference in communities and individual lives.

The true foundation for success rests in an organization’s culture. Culture eats strategy for breakfast. BNI’s core values help to establish its culture. It’s never too late to be thinking about your core values—or too early.

There are 5 established core values in BNI:

  1. Givers Gain
  2. Building Meaningful Relationships
  3. Lifelong Learning
  4. Traditions + Innovation
  5. Positive Attitude

There are two additional values that BNI is considering adding:

  1. Accountability
  2. Recognition

BNI serves a greater good by helping businesses around the world to create a culture of collaboration. Dr. Misner’s Big Hairy Audacious Goal for BNI is to change the way the world does business by incorporating these core values.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 400 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, coming to you from from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you, and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am doing great and I am working at the office this week. This week’s topic is- I wanted to make something that was kind of related to the fact that we are at episode 400. Can you believe that? 400. I think you have done 350 of those 400 with me?

Priscilla:
Something. At least that much.

Ivan:
It’s really a huge number. I wanted something that is really kind of focused on sort of the big picture for BNI because this is our episode number 400, so the topic is make a good living while serving a greater good. It is really about, I think, in part the values that BNI has as an organization.

You know, all of us are in business to make a profit. That is what we are in business for. That is what entrepreneurism is about. But if it is the primary driving force in a business, we really become, I think, mercenaries to that process and I believe that I as a business professional should serve a greater need than simply making a profit. I think that business can be honorable. I think that business can be noble.

It can make a difference in individual lives as well as in communities. Small business is really an engine that drives many of the economies around the world. Small business doesn’t have the resources of large corporations. However, as small businesses network together, I think the sum of the whole becomes greater than those individual parts. Well designed collaboration based on an effective system and strategy can lead to, really, small business success. In the final analysis, the true foundation for success rests in an organization’s culture, whether you are talking about an individual small business or whether you are talking about an organization like BNI.

You have heard me say this before, Priscilla. I really believe that culture eats strategy for business. An organization needs a sound strategy to succeed, but it needs a great culture to excel. For me, that approach has been about creating core values around a corporate collaboration in BNI. Our core values help establish, as it does with any organization, our culture. It’s never too late or too early to think about your core values in business or in life.

So if you are hearing me talk about this and you think, I haven’t thought about that. I have been in business a long time. It is really never too late to be thinking about your core values. On the other hand, you may be new in business and you are thinking, this isn’t relevant to me. Well, it is relevant. The more you think about your core values as an organization, as a business, early in the process, the easier it is to pass that on, I think, as you grow.

In BNI, we have really five established core values, but there are two additional ones that we are considering adding to our core values. So let me give you the five, Priscilla, and then I will give you the two add ons and I will be happy to talk about any of these.

The first is one that shouldn’t be a surprise to anyone. That is the philosophy for givers gain. That is one of the core values of the organization. It began in BNI almost from the very beginning, this idea that what goes around comes around and if I help you, you will help me. We will all do better, and it is absolutely a core value to the success of BNI.

The second is really about meaningful relationships, about building meaningful relationships. BNI is a relationship organization. The more we can build those relationships with other business professionals, the more successful we are going to be. It’s really about trust. People do business with people they know and trust. So if you have those relationships, you build that trust.

The third is about lifelong learning. We used to call it education and training, but it is more than education and training. That is sort of the mechanism. The value is lifelong learning. Oftentimes, Priscilla, I really think that business people pay lip service to education. They say it is important, but they don’t spend much time on it. I have found that the businesses that are constantly sharpening their ax, that are improving what they do everyday are the ones that are most successful.

So lifelong learning is important to me personally. It is important, I think, to the organization. That is the third core value in BNI.

The fourth is an unusual one because they sound completely different. There are two points and they sound completely different. They are traditions plus innovation. They sound almost mutually exclusive. I don’t think they are.

If you want to have a successful organization, tradition is key. Your traditions tell you who you are as a company and the things that are important to you. But if your entire organizational existence is based on the past, you are going to fail because things change, so you have to have innovation. So it is really about that balance between balance and innovation.

The fifth core value in BNI is a positive attitude. People hate doing business with grumpy people. That is a technical term, by the way, Priscilla. Grumpy people.

We have done a number of surveys for Masters of Success of what it takes for a master networker. A positive attitude comes up over and over again. It is in our code of ethics. Have a positive and supportive attitude with others. So it is part of our code of ethics. I think it is one of our core values.

If you have somebody- I know I said this in a podcast some time ago. We had somebody in a chapter who the woman said, “The best thing this guy ever does is call in sick because when he is not there, we have a great meeting. He is a fun sucker. He sucks the fun right out of the room.”

So if you have somebody like that in your group with a really negative attitude, you have the wrong person. Go back and listen to the podcast we did. We have a podcast on Who Is in Your Room, right?

Priscilla:
Right.

Ivan:
Go back and listen to that podcast, Who Is in Your Room? You want to pick people who are professional and good at what they do, but I think a positive attitude is really, really important. It is a core value.

There are two others that I am pretty sure we will be adding. The sixth is accountability. You know, one of the things about BNI or any network, if you have a network, one of the strengths is that all of the members are friends. One of the weaknesses of a network is all of the members are friends. It is both a strength and a weakness.

But groups like BNI are not friendship groups. They are referral groups. If you want to have a powerful personal network, you have to have accountability. Otherwise, it becomes a coffee clatch, a social group. So accountability is a key to success in business and in life and absolutely in a network like BNI. Accountability is, I think, critical.

The seventh, and I am really kind of shocked that the sixth and seventh were never put in our original core values. I am certain we are going to be adding these. The seventh is recognition. When I started BNI, one of the most important things was to recognition those who contribute to the group. We have a door prize at the end of the meeting for people who bring in the highest number visitors or most referrals. That is why we have the notable networker badges or notable networker certificates, the recognition each month for people coming in. These are all important aspects of the organization. We want to recognize the people who are contributing.

These are our core values in BNI. These are what I think has helped BNI achieve success. I think that by applying these core values, I think in many ways, we are serving a greater good. We are helping business people collaborate and be successful around the world.

What are your comments on that, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
As you said each value, I thought about it and accountability is one that I haven’t thought about before, but requiring people to come to every meeting unless you have a substitute or sick or something like that. There are a couple you can miss, but basically, having to be there every week and your leadership team keeps you accountable to that standard. You know, so that is just one little aspect of accountability, but I think it is a great value to have. I am glad you brought it up.

Ivan:
Why settle for mediocrity when excellence is an option? Excellence is an option and accountability is the key to that. It is possible to make a good living while serving a greater good. The core values that I have tried to apply in my life and in my business have helped to create a culture in a context of building a business through networking. I think this approach is really a great way to get business. I think it is an even better way to do business.

Business, as I said in the beginning of this podcast, can be honorable, can be noble. It can be something that improves peoples’ lives, as well as communities. It can do so by not only generating more business for one another but by giving back to the community, by mentoring others, by immersing in a culture of shared learning and by collaborating with others.

I have a BHAG. Priscilla, I don’t know if you have ever heard that expression. BHAG. A big, hairy, audacious goal. I have a BHAG for businesses around the world. I believe we, BNI, can change the way the world does business. We can do that by incorporating these core values into our business and support collaboration and positive, meaningful relationships.

We are celebrating our 30th anniversary for BNI, and I believe that our focus on these core values, our philosophy, our vision, is really responsible for our 30 years of consecutive growth. There aren’t too many companies that have 30 years of consecutive growth. I think the reason we have that is that we are, in fact, serving businesses all around the world and helping them build their business through word of mouth.

It has been fantastic to do these podcasts with you, Priscilla. Number 400. I appreciate you and I appreciate your efforts in making these podcasts the success they have been.

Priscilla:
Thank you, Ivan, it has been a real pleasure to be on this podcast with you every week. It is incredible. You are my personal coach and I love talking to you and hearing all of your wonderful and interesting ideas, so thank you.

Ivan:
Thank you. I appreciate that a lot and I look forward to our podcast again next week.

Priscilla:
Okay, excellent. Thank you so much. I think that is it for this week. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: In honor of the 400th episode of The Official BNI Podcast, Dr. Misner chose to discuss BNI’s 5 core values and 2 additions: Accountability & Recognition. They’re still relevant now that we’re at Episode 557.

title.rendered: Episode 556: Don’t Show Off, Show Interest

content.rendered:
Synopsis

When Dr. Misner goes to networking events, he frequently observes people engaging in dueling monologues, even though no one really wants to listen to anyone else’s sales monlogue at a networking event. How do you avoid dueling monologue syndrome? Don’t show off, show interest.

A good networker, like a good talk show interviewer, asks questions and lets people talk. Here are some questions you can start with. Rather than ask all of them in sequence, follow the thread of the conversation to ask more.

  1. What do you like best about what you do? Why? What are some examples?
  2. What got you started in your industry?
  3. What’s your target market? What’s an example of a great client?
  4. What are some of the challenges you have in your business?
  5. Where else do you network?

If the questions sound familiar, it’s because Dr. Misner has mentioned them in previous podcasts (144, 167, 183, 544). But even if you’ve heard them before, they will be new to someone you’ve just met.

If you hear this new contact describe a problem or a need of any kind, have someone in your personal network that you can refer them to. Nothing expedites a relationship faster than helping someone you’ve met by referring another person.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 556 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I have kind of a fun topic today: Don’t show off, show interest. Sometimes when I go to networking events, I observe people engaging in what I like to call dueling monologues. Now Dueling Banjos, that’s a great song. Dueling pianos, that’s a great music venue. We have a fantastic one here in Austin. But dueling monologues, which are basically prolonged dueling talks by one person at a time, well, that’s just not so exciting. Frankly, I don’t think most people if any, if you really ask them, are interested in participating in a dueling monologue.

The problem is there are a lot of people winging it when it comes to networking, and they tend to go straight into sales mode. They do this with someone who is also winging it. It leads to a dueling monologue syndrome. This whole scenario would be pretty funny if it werent actually true. I have seen it many times. Unfortunately, it is true, so the question is how do we avoid the dueling monologue syndrome?

The answer, I believe, is don’t show off, show interest. What is the goal for our networking? It is to build our business. So if that is the case, then it is about building a relationship with people. When you go to networking events, keep your eeye on the ball. Don’t try to dazzle people with your brilliance. You can do that anytime, you can certainly do that later. Stand out from the crowd and impress them with your genuine interest- just for the record, your interest in them, not your interest in selling to them but your genuine interest in them.

I understand that feels counterintuitive to people but it is a bit of a paradox. The truth is though that the best way to build a relationship with people is to show interest in them. A good networker is like a good talk show interviewer. A good interviewer asks the guests questions and gives them time to elaborate and talk. I think a good networker, Priscilla, is very similar to that. They ask questions and let people talk.

You have to follow the thread of the conversation to the point where you can ask more questions. Here are some examples of questions that you can start with. Don’t ask one question after another. Do one or two of these questions and follow the thread of the conversation to ask more. Remember it is not an interrogation. It is a discussion. 

So the questions should be open-ended probing questions. These are some of the questions that allow the respondent or someone you are networking with to open up and discuss wha you ask. Here are some examples, but don’t do them all. Just do a couple, and like I said, just do the thread. Here we go:

One: what do you like best about what you do? If they give you a short answer, ask them why or to give some examples.

Next: what got you started in your industry? When they talk about this, throw in a couple of additional clarification questions as they are talking.

Next: what is your target market? Here,  you can drill down on this and ask more questions to be clear about who their best clients are. 

Next: give an example of a great client. “Tell me an example of a fantastic client for you.”Again, drill down and see if you can get them to be specific.

Here is another one: what are some of the challenges that you have in your business? This is a great chance for you to find out how you can help them. Don’t sell to them. Help them. Find out what their challenge is. Maybe you can refer somebody to them who can help them out.

Here is always a good one, and I haven’t talked about this one much: where else do you network? It’s some great information to know where else you can go that you can see them again. If you want to build a relationship with people, then seeing them on a regular basis, having those touch points, is very valuable. What other networking groups do you go to? Where else do you network?

Remember that this should be more like an interview, not an interrogation. Make it conversational and follow the thread of the discussion that you find interesting, or better yet, what they find interesting.  During that process, if you hear them describe a need or a problem of any kind, have someone in your personal network whom you trust that you can refer to. Make sure to let them know that you would be happy to refer them to this person and that you respect and highly recommend the person that you are referring to them. 

Nothing expedites a relationship faster than helping someone you have met by referring another person – or even sometimes a book, website or article that can help them with the challenge. I have done that for years. I have referred people to articles that either I have written or other people have written.  I just did a Facebook Live today and I referred people to an article that I have been recommending for more than 30 years on motivating people. It is very powerful when you can refer people to other content that helps them succeed. 

Don’t be like most of the people out there who think that they know how to network when they are really just doing face to face cold-calling. I recommend that you be different. Don’t show off. Show interest. Next time you are a witness of dueling monologues syndrome, if you are listening to this podcast, I want you to if nothing else in your mind, start humming to yourself the theme song from the movie Deliverance. For you youngsters out there, that is Dueling Banjos – because if you ever needed to be liberated from a conversation, it would be right then at that time. 

Priscilla, that is everything I have for today. Any thoughts on my dueling monologues?

Priscilla:
You know, the one thought that came to me was I have discovered myself making a mistake in a situation like that when somebody else will be talking about themselves and they will come up with a situation. Then I will interject my story because it is similar in some way to what they have just described. I realize in a way what a mistake that is because then I draw the attention back to myself and I kind of do exactly what I shouldn’t be doing as far as building the relationship. 

Ivan:
Yeah, you know, there are some times where interjecting with your experience is helpful. It can edify somebody. If it becomes about you, then that is different. 

There is a talk show host now in the US, I am not going to say who it is. In many ways he is good, but everything always comes back to him. Every single person he interviews comes back to him and his experience. I sit there and I think – now, I am going to really date myself, Priscilla, and for everyone outside the United States, you will probably not know who I am talking about and I apologize for that. I keep thinking I miss Johnny Carson.

Priscilla:
Oh. Right.

Ivan:
Johnny Carson was always about his guests. I remember Johnny Carson once – I saw this live many years ago – in the same week, he had two guests, both of which said the same joke in the same week.

Priscilla:
That’s funny.

Ivan:
Carson laughed just as hard the second time as he did the first time. I remember thinking this guy is a great, great interviewer because he just heard somebody else do the same joke that somebody else did earlier this week and he laughed just as hard.

Priscilla:
That’s great.

Ivan:
He was fully engaged, listening and asking questions. It wasn’t about him. It was about his guests. I really think that is what makes a great networker. It is not about the individual. It  is about the person they are talking to.

Priscilla:
I think you are right.

Ivan:
That is all I have for this week, Priscilla, unless you have anything to add.

Priscilla:
No, I think that is it, Dr. Misner. Thank you so much for that. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking For Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening, this is Priscilla Rice. We look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: How do you avoid dueling monologue syndrome? Don’t show off, show interest. Nothing expedites a relationship faster than helping someone you’ve met by referring another person.

title.rendered: Episode 555: The Go-Giver Influencer

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Bob Burg joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about his new book, The Go-Giver Influencer. The concepts “Go-Giver” and “Givers Gain” are perfectly aligned in terms of the way we network and do business.

An “influencer” is someone who can move a person or persons to a desired action. The essence of influence is “pull”–as opposed to “push.” Influence is an attraction. Great influencers attract people to their ideas through their focus on bringing value to others.

In this book, Bob and his co-author John David Mann share five secrets of genuine influence. Here are three of them.

  1. Master your emotions. It’s the key to turning a negative situation into a win for everyone involved.
  2. Set the frame. Set a productive frame when you go into any situation, and learn to re-frame situations to turn adversaries into allies.
  3. Communicate with tact and empathy. When we speak tactfully, it opens the other person up to our ideas.

The premise of the Go-Giver philosophy is that shifting from getting to giving is the most profitable way to do business.

If you go to thegogiver.com, you can get the first two chapters of the book for free.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 555 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. Thank you so much. I am in Las Vegas this week for the US BNI Conference. We have been doing these conferences since 1990, so many, many years. And we are in Las Vegas, so second time back around after a couple of decades. It is great to be meeting the BNI members here in Las Vegas again. 

Priscilla:
That sounds great.

Ivan:
I am really excited to tell everyone that I have a great guest on today’s podcast. He has been on a couple of our podcasts. His name is Bob Burg. Bob has written a number of books including Endless Referrals and The Go Giver. Today, we are going to talk about his latest book, The Go Giver Influencer. You can’t miss it. It is a beautiful purple covered book. It is a great color.

Let me say something about Bob. There are literally only about two or three other people that I have ever recommend if I can’t do a speaking engagement. If I am not available, there are only two or three other people I ever suggest as a possibility to speak on networking, and Bob is one of them. Bob is one of them. Bob is incredibly knowledgable but more importantly, I truly believe that Bob walks the talk. Bob is a fantastic networker in that he is all about building relationships. I would like to think I have a great relationship with Bob. Hopefully, Bob, you think I have a great relationship with you. I really respect you a lot. It is a pleasure to have you on the BNI Podcast today. Thanks.

Bob:
Well, you know, Dr. Ivan, hearing one of your mentors and one of your heroes speak that way about you the way you just spoke about me- you already made my day. As far as I am concerned, the interview is over. I am done. This is it. Thank you so much.

Ivan:
My pleasure. People that listen to my podcast – I have been doing it for more than 10 years. It is rare that I say something like that, but I really believe that about you, Bob. I have seen it multiple times. You walk the talk. You have great content. I recommend your books. And it is a real pleasure to have you on the podcast today. Let’s get going. This is a great little book. I endorsed it. It is a quick read. It’s a very informative book, but let’s start with how do you find an influencer?

Bob:
That’s a great question. I think you go back to the word “influence” and there is both that surface level and then that deeper level because on a very, very basic level, influence is simply the ability to move a person or persons to a desired action, typically within the course of a very specific goal. Now, that is the definition but it is not its substance or its essence. The essence of influence is pull – pull as opposed to push. You never hear someone say, “Wow. That Dave or that Donna, she is very influential. She has a lot of push with people.” No, she has a lot of pull. 

That’s what influence is. It is an attraction. Great influencers attract people to themselves and then to their ideas and they do this through their focus on bringing value to others. That is exactly, of course, what you teach with Givers Gain.

Ivan:
Right. You know, your Go Giver and Givers Gain are absolutely perfectly aligned in terms of the way that we network and do business, so I love that expression. You have five steps or secrets that you talk about in the book. The first one is master your emotions. I want to talk about at least three of those five if you can. Do you want to talk about that first one? Master your emotions. 

Bob:
Sure. Well, you know, the sages of old asked who is a mighty person? And they answered that person who is strong enough to control their own emotions and make of an enemy or a potential enemy a friend. This is really where it all begins because it is only when we are in control of ourselves, of our own emotions, that we are even in a position to take a potentially negative situation or person or prospect or potential network partner and turn that into a win for everyone involved. That seems simple, but the fact is we are human beings, and as human beings, we are emotional creatures.

We would like to think we are logical, but we are really pretty emotion driven. We make major decisions based on emotion and then we back up those emotional decisions with logic or we rationalize – and if you take the word “rationalize”, it simply means we tell ourselves rational lies. We do that to justify those decisions we know we shouldn’t have made. 

So the key is not to try to deny our emotions or forego our emotions but to be sure we are the master of our emotions. We are controlling them as opposed to them controlling us. Or as one of my friends Dondi Scumaci, a fantastic leadership speaker puts it, “By all means, take your emotions along for the ride, but make sure you are driving the car.”

Ivan:
That is really important, I think, when you are talking about a networking environment building relationships with people. It is very easy for your emotions to sometimes get in the way of progress in the relationship. Have you seen that as well?

Bob:
Oh, absolutely because if you think about it, ego is just such a big part of the human makeup, but it’s a big part of human nature. A lot of times, when someone is acting in a way that is really not productive – for themselves, either- often, that ego has risen up, and that is a big part of emotion. Here is the thing: when we can control our own emotions and help the other person to work effectively within theirs, now our influence is ready to go to a whole new level.

Ivan:
Yeah, if we can put our emotions aside and drill down to the issue and solution, oftentimes, we are able to bypass all of the emotional energy that is around and deal with the challenge so that we can get past it and have some success. I have seen that over and over with people who are really good at networking and really good at being leaders.

Bob:
Oh, I agree. Absolutely. 

Ivan:
So you have two more. Set the frame. Talk about that. That is one of your five. Set the frame. What does that mean?

Bob:
Oh, this is so key because when we set the frame, we are really 80% of the way there. Here is what I mean. What is a frame? A frame is the foundation from which everything else evolves. Let me give you a vey quick example. Something that happened a few years ago, probably one of my favorite frame stories. I was in a Dunkin Donuts having coffee and reading, which I am always doing. There was a little boy about probably two and a half years old, a little toddler. He was running around the restaurant when his parents called him to the table. He starts to walk back and he falls. He slips on the floor and he hurt himself, but you could tell he was shocked. He did not expect that to happen. So what was the first thing that he did? He immediately looked at his parents, the two people he trusts most in the world in order to get their response, their interpretation of the event. 

Now, I truly believe that had they gotten panicky or upset, he would have started crying. But they handled it beautifully. They walked over calmly. They smiled. They began to applaud, “Oh what a good trip! That looks like so much fun!” And he immediately began to laugh. What the parents did is they set a productive frame from which he could operate.

We can do that with anyone we are about to meet. It can be as simple as an inside out smile. It can be that when you are in a group and you see someone maybe new coming in during the meeting, before the meeting, you kind of open up body language, to give the frame of “Welcome. You are a part of us.”

But we also have to be able to reset someone else’s negative frame because when they come somewhere in a negative frame, we need to reframe. Let’s say that you are doing a presentation for someone.  You can tell that this person is kind of defensive. Maybe they had a bad experience with a salesman before. Perish the thought. But we want to make sure that they feel very comfortable. Before going to the presentation, we say something like, “Mary, we have been able to help a number of people with this product. Whether or not it is the right answer for you, we simply can’t know without exploring deeper and determining whether it meets your needs. So please know our conversation is really about both of us discovering that. It if does, great. If not, that’s okay, too. “

So we have now turned this from two adversaries into two allies who are now simply looking for a solution that is best for her, the prospect. 

Ivan:
You know, I am hearing this information as you share it. To me, the Go Giver Influencer is really about being a giving leader.

Bob:
Oh I love that.

Ivan:
Would you not agree? It is really about leading a direction, all of the things that you talk about – masting your emotions, stepping into the other person’s shoes, which we haven’t talked about and setting the frame, and we are going to talk about communicating with tact and empathy. These are all about really being a good leader, I think.

Bob:
It is. It is. Sales is leadership. Life is leadership. 

Ivan:
Yeah, no question about it. Let’s do one more before we wrap up. Communicate with tact and empathy. 

Bob:
Yeah. Well, you know, this is all what really brings it home in a sense. My dad has always defined “tact” as the language of strength. To me, that just makes so much sense because when you think about it, tact is a way of being able to communicate with someone in such a way that not only are they not defensive to us and resistant to our ideas, but they are open to us and open to our ideas. When we speak tactfully and with empathy, we are able to sort of do it is such a way that it really opens this person up to ideas. That allows us to sort of take emotion out of it, and ego out of it and really allows for two people to collaborate. When it comes to the Go Giver Influencer, rather than looking to compromise, we are looking to collaborate where we can make one plus one [equal] three.

Ivan:
These are all part of the five secrets of genuine influence, but in the bigger context, it is all part of the Go Giver philosophy. So, just to wrap up real quick, can you talk just a little bit about the Go Giver philosophy because this hits home with BNI members and our philosophy of Givers Gain.

Bob:
Well, you know, it does. This is why I tell people you are one of my greatest mentors. We have probably met in person only a few times, and you have had such a huge impact on my life and on my business. You know, what we say basically is that the premise of the Go Giver philosophy is that shifting one’s focus  from getting to giving – and giving in this context simply means constantly and consistently providing value to others – understanding that doing so is not only a nice way to conduct business, a pleasant way to conduct business, it happens to be the most financially profitable way as well. 

Ivan:
Yeah, I tell people about BNI and the philosophy of Givers Gain that it’s not only a great way to get business, it’s an even better way to do business. That sounds very similar to what you just said there.

Bob:
Right.

Ivan:
Listen, Bob, it is always a pleasure to talk to you and you are welcome on my podcast anytime you would like.  Your books are fantastic. I highly recommend everyone who is listening to this, go pick up a copy of The Go Giver Influencer, a little story about a most persuasive  idea. Bob is there a website you want people to go to, or would you just like them to go to Amazon to get a copy? Where would you like them to go?

Bob:
If they go thegogiver.com and click on the blue graphic for the new book, they can actually get the first two chapters of the new book. They can read those and see if they like where the story is heading and they can always click through and purchase it.

Ivan:
Okay, so maybe I am color blind. It looks kind of purple to me. 

Bob:
Did I not say that?

Ivan:
You just said blue. 

Bob:
Oh, excuse me, I am sorry. No, it is purple. I’m sorry about that.

Ivan:
It’s a royal purple. I love the color.

Bob:
Royal purple. Yep.

Ivan:
Listen, Bob, thanks so much for being on my podcast. I hope to have you back again. You have great content. If you are listening to this podcast, go pick up that book. I don’t recommend a whole lot of books.  I do recommend Bob’s books, so go check it out.

Priscilla, back to you.

Priscilla:
That’s great. Thank you both very much. I think that is some great information. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking For Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening, this is Priscilla Rice. We look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Great influencers attract people to their ideas through their focus on bringing value to others. In this episode, Bob Burg shares 3 of the 5 secrets of genuine influence from his new book, The Go-Giver Influencer.

title.rendered: Episode 554: The Introvert’s Edge

content.rendered:
Synopsis

This week Dr. Misner interviews Matthew Pollard about his new book, The Introvert’s Edge. Dr. Misner is an introvert himself, and thinks it’s fantastic to talk about how introverts actually have a little bit of an edge in the way they do business.

In BNI’s survey of about 3400 business people last year, introverts really shone because networking is all about building relationships. Introverts who are successful in business and in networking develop a system for themselves. That system, combined with the ability to listen and empathize, increases their ability to deliver what the customer really wants.

The Introvert’s Edge outlines a natural step-by-step process for making a sale without aggressive techniques. Three key elements of the process are being able to utilize story, asking questions, and the trial close.

Get Matthew’s book for yourself at theintrovertsedge.com.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 554 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast which is brought to you by The Networking For Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla. And by the way, I have just had somebody maybe a week ago say to me, “Hi Ivan. How are you and where are you today?” They said they listen to all of the podcasts and always listen for Priscilla to ask where I am today and how I am doing. I am doing great. This particular week, I happen to be in Austin, Texas at home. I am interviewing a new friend of mine, Matthew Pollard, who has written a book called The Introvert’s Edge.

I really love the content that he has got and the whole spin he has. I think introverts have gotten a bad rap for many years, and I think it is fantastic to talk about how introverts actually have a little bit of an edge in the way they do business. They have an advantage as Matthew says in the way they do business. Matthew Pollard is really known as the Rapid Growth Guy. The Introvert’s Edge is a bestselling book. If you want to get information on the book, I will have that at the end of the podcast.

He is also the Executive Director of the Small Business Festival: smallbusinessfestival.org. Matthew had me on one of his videos. He does a whole series of videos on The Introvert’s Edge. He got me coming up on another one really soon, and I am really excited to have him here on this particular podcast. Matthew, welcome to the bnipodcast.com site.

Matthew:
Thank you very much, mate. I am very happy to be here. I have to say, episode 554, I think you said. That is amazing, how many episodes you have managed to get out in 8 years. (Editor’s note: it’s actually 11 years.)

Ivan:
Yeah, if nothing else, I am very consistent, Matthew. I tell people I may not be as smartest guy in the room. I may not be the most talented guy in the room, but I am almost always the most persistent guy in the room. We have over 500 episodes of the BNI Podcast. It’s fantastic to have you on this show. I appreciate it.

You know, I enjoyed my interview with you because as many of my listeners probably know, I am a situational extrovert actually, where in some cases I come across as an extrovert, but when left to my own devices, I am pretty much an introvert, which a lot of people are very surprised about. I did a survey of about 3400 business people past year, asking them questions about what it takes to be a great networker, and we found that introverts really kind of shined in that survey because it was all about building relationships. I think that gives them a distinct advantage. Talk about that a little bit. How does an introvert have a distinct advantage in the sales process?

Matthew:
You know, that is an interesting question. It was absolutely a blast having you on The Introvert’s Edge podcast. I am surprised continuously to discover so many people that I have projected upon them that they are obviously extroverts because they are perceived as natural speakers, natural networkers, natural salespeople and then discover later that they are actually introverted. I loved reading your article about the fact that you discovered that you are an introvert in the later years and looked back at how you started BNI and discovered why you created these systems around networking.

I think that introverts for the longest time put barriers up against ourselves that say we can’t network, we can’t talk, we can’t present. We have to have the gift of the gab for that. It’s just not true. What I have discovered after interviewing so many introverts that are successful in business or really successful in sales or networking is that they have developed this system to be successful.

So they have gone against the grain and said, “If I am going to be successful, I need to create a system, not so much a personal activity, and then I can go ahead and run the program.” Then once they have that program, they have so many advantages. They have such high levels of empathy and the ability to actively listen and be interested in a client or a prospect–such as what you found in your survey–to actively listen and be interested and not trying to process what to say next, gives them a huge advantage in the selling process. Because they listen, the cancellation ratios are less and their customer happiness levels are so much higher–because they listened to what the customer said, and they delivered on what they told them that they would.

Ivan:
You made a comment there about being interested. You know, I just wrote an article on this topic, and I thought about the fact that all too often, people are trying to be interesting when they are networking. They are trying to be interesting rather than interested. This is a great example of where I think the introvert has an edge because they are more likely to be interested, whereas the extrovert wants to be interesting. Of course, what do they want to talk about? Not you. What is their favorite topic – the extroverts’?

Matthew:
Oh myself! I love to talk about me. Let’s make all the attention about me.

Ivan:
That’s right. That’s absolutely true. Look, those extroverts out there, don’t take it personal. It’s okay. This is just a skill set that you need to work on in the same way that the introverts need to learn how to connect better with people. Extroverts need to learn how to be more interested in people and not just interesting to people.

So in your book The Introvert’s Edge, you outline a seven stage sales process. I would love to cover a couple of them if we can. You could talk about those seven steps in the sales process for introverts. Talk about those more.

Matthew:
Absolutely. I think one of the biggest differences between introverts and extroverts, which is why we can be a little bit more pro-introvert in this session is that both introverts and extroverts have advantages and disadvantages. The difference is the extrovert will look at the disadvantages they have and say, “ I will go and learn actively. I will go and learn how to be more empathetic, while an introvert will say, “This isn’t for me. I can’t do that in my natural ability.”

That’s why we needed a book that talked about introverted sales – because we can’t be those bulldog techniques. We can’t do those hard closings. It doesn’t feel congruently us. So we needed to find another way, and I think the reason my book has been endorsed by wonderful people like yourself, Ivan, and Harvard and Princeton, Neil Powers and Marshall Goldsmith is because if that natural step by step process leads to a sale, without any of those bulldog techniques and those hard closings. One of the key elements of the process is being able to utilize story.

This is one of the key advantages that introverts have because they are great people at preparing before we go into sales. Preparation is one of the keys to any introvert’s activity. What we do is we look at how to prepare great stories before you go into any selling, before you go in to any networking events. That really gives us a bit of a superpower because we are not actually in our heads trying to think about what we say, like an extrovert would be. Also, in our heads trying to process the problem, we have these great stories lined up ready to say.

A lot of times, a person when they see this will say, “Oh, I don’t want to sound scripted.” Here is the thing- Ivan, I saw that you had posted just the other day an anniversary with your wife. You shared that on LinkedIn. I am sure that years and years. Ago, when you first told the story about how you and your wife met, you would have probably had a bulkier story and you would have noticed that there were certain things that triggered people’s interest more and other things that kind of bored people. What you did is you learned to gloss over the boring parts and embellish a little bit more in the exciting parts, and that story.

Ivan:
Embellish? Really, Matthew? Embellish? Me?

Matthew:
[laughing] I think everybody embellishes. They polish their “how they met their partner” stories as much as they possibly can, right?

Ivan:
True.

Matthew:
That’s the thing as an introvert – we have this ability to get these stories set up before we go in, really practice them and then make sure that we can deliver those. But we have them in our pockets, so what we are doing when we are in the sales activity is–of course we want to ask great questions beforehand and listen to the answers, which introverts have an unbelievable power to do, to have that active listening and then to empathize with the client and then to have a story of a client just like them that they worked with that had a similar problem and they gave this outcome. These are things that introverts can deliver better than anyone.

An introvert delivering a story- introverts have got these really amazing qualities. Firstly, for us introverts who struggle to create rapport. There is a study out of Princeton that found that telling a story creates a natural resonance between the teller of the story and a listening. It creates an artificial rapport. That’s one of the huge advantages in utilizing stories.

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree more. We have only got a couple minutes. I want hear a couple more. For those of you listening, if you feel like you are an extrovert, you can learn from this. If you feel like you are an introvert, this is edifying your position as an introvert, your reality and behavioral style as an introvert. So this is good for both introverts and extroverts to hear. We have a couple more minutes. Give me a couple more steps in your book about the seven step sales process.

Matthew:
