title.rendered: Episode 538: The Collaborative Economy of BNI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Laura Hurren joins Dr. Misner to share a story that illustrates the difference between a scarcity mentality and an abundance mentality.

A new BNI chapter was launching in a small town in Italy. They held an enormous launch event and Visitors Day, attended by more than 100 visitors. The members of the new chapter went to extraordinary lengths to make it a great event.

But to Laura’s horror, when the visitors started to leave after the event, the leadership team stayed on stage taking selfies.When she tried to persuade them to go outside and speak to the visitors and invite them to join, they assured her that all was well.

When she went out to see what was happening, she found the members of another BNI chapter, who had come 28 kilometers to welcome the visitors, answer their questions, and get them signed up to the new chapter.

Laura had never seen anything like it, but BNI members in Italy are very focused on the community. The most recently-formed chapter helps the next chapter get started. The members of this chapter described it as a collaborative economy.

The BNI chapters in this region (Piemonte Sud) recognize that if they work together, they can build their chapters more quickly and more strongly than if they work in silos. This is a perfect illustration of the abundance mentality: there’s a lot of business out there, and if you work with chapters nearby, all of you are going to be more and more successful.

Chapters that support their fellow chapters and support each other’s members build the BNI brand, build business, and build BNI globally. These chapters also participated in the Business Voices program from their desire to support the community.

If you hear of a chapter opening up near you, volunteer to help them.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 538 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic. Thank you, Priscilla. I have a guest who has never been on the podcast, but I have known her for many years. Laura has been an Executive Director and a Co-National Director. For over 20 years, she has been involved in BNI. Laura started when she was only 16 years old and she has been in BNI forever. She has been in the Founders Circle of BNI. She has been on the Franchise Advisory Board, which is sort of like a membership committee but for executive directors. I consider Laura a great friend, a long-time BNI Director, and she is also the CO-National Director besides coming from London and was also the Co-National Director in Italy. Welcome to your first interview on the BNI Podcast, Laura.

Laura:
Thank you, Ivan. Thank you for such a warm welcome.

Ivan:
It is awesome to have you on and I don’t know if I gave your last name. Laura Hurren. Laura has a fantastic story. She told it to me at the BNI Convention recently, and I asked her if she would share it in a podcast because it is an amazing story that I think shows the difference between a scarcity mentality and an abundance mentality. So why don’t you tell the story, Laura? Then let’s talk about that and why we named it what we did for this podcast. Go ahead.

Laura:
Okay. Thank you. So last year, I was privileged to attend the launch of a chapter in Italy in a town called Versano, which has just 20,000 inhabitants. It is south of Turin in the Piedmontese Sur region. Over 100 visitors attended the launch and we held it in the cinema, which is the only building that could accommodate that amount of people.

Incredibly proud of what they were doing, the members really did go to extraordinary lengths. They branded the cinema BNI. They were giving away popcorn in BNI cups. The leadership team was displayed on posters around the cinema as the stars of the show. It was a true event. I was blown away, and everybody was excited and engaged.

So we went through the launch which went incredibly well. We ended the formal part. the visitors started to leave the auditorium, and to my disappointment – almost horror, shock horror – the members stayed on stage, congratulating themselves, taking photos and celebrating. After all of their hard work, they appeared to miss one vital ingredient- that is taking the opportunity to talk to the visitors and to ask them if they wanted to join.

So I ran up on stage and I pleaded for them to go to their visitors, but they assured me that all was well. I really did beg them, beg them to go outside. In the end, I saw that I was getting nowhere so I went out myself to see what I could do. I was absolutely amazed to see the members from Sylipso, a chapter 28 kilometers away, which had been the previous chapter to launch in the region. The were there, in full, taking care of the new chapter’s guests, answering their questions and signing them up. All the tables were set. The visitors were sitting in front of the members from this other chapter, and it was the perfect visitor hosting. I had never seen anything like it.

I asked them why they were doing it. They said this is how it works. The last chapter to launch helps the next chapter to launch and so on and so forth. Why? because it builds the region more quickly and more strongly because they are able to tell stories of how it is working for them.

I have never in my 20 years in BNI seen anything like it in my life. It was absolutely fantastic and heartwarming to see. You see, there, they really focused on the economy- I’m sorry, on the conveniences. They call it a collaborative economy.

Ivan:
When you first told me this story, I had goosebumps. I was like, really? Seriously? You know, I see chapters and these chapters are reasonably close, but there, it’s a very rural area, so there is not much in between, right, in terms of population?

Laura:
Yes. Absolutely.

Ivan:
Their chapters are kind of side by side, even though they may be a slight distance apart. There is not a lot in between. I see a lot of chapters that have a scarcity mentality, like, oh, no there is a chapter that is going to open up in another town next to us? We are not going to help them.

But to have a chapter go to this length to support a fellow chapter opening up in the next town, it just made me so proud to hear what that group did. And you had to be on top of the world to see the collaboration and cooperation between the two groups.

Laura:
Yeah, I absolutely was, and I think it was their mentality as well. You know, this chapter that was just launching. They are ready to help the next chapter.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Chapters that embrace an abundance mentality, support their fellow chapters and support each other’s members build business and build the BNI brand.

title.rendered: Episode 537: Specific Is Terrific for Referrals (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 416.

Synopsis

If you’re engaged in referral marketing, it’s very important to teach others how to refer you. In this episode, Dr. Misner leads listeners in a live exercise.

  1. For the next 15 seconds, write down as many silly things that people have done in the world as you can.
  2. In 15 seconds, write down as many silly things that your children have done (or children you know if you don’t have any) as you can think of.
  3. In 15 seconds, write down as many things that are white in color as you can think of.
  4. In 15 seconds, write down as many things as you can think of that are white in color that you might find in a refrigerator.

Did you get more answers for 2 and 4 than for 1 and 3? Most people do, because it’s easier to respond to a specific request. Referral marketing works just the same way: the more specific you are about what you do and who you do it for, the more referrals you’ll get.

Brought to you by Networking Now.

Complete Transcript of Episode 537

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey, Priscilla. I am in Mexico this week. You ask that every week and I am sure people wonder why. I like to remind people from time to time. Years ago, I had people saying, “Oh, I van is on the beach, just hanging out, not doing anything these days.” I just want people to know that I am out visiting regions a lot, or out on business.

This week, I am in Mexico for a Transformational Leadership Council meeting. I am a member of TLC, which is an organization started by Jack Canfield. It is a lot of fun. Very interesting. I get some great content that I end up using in BNI. The whole phrase, “changing the way the world does business” came from TLC, from a brainstorming session I did at TLC.

Priscilla:
That is great. You may end up on the beach. But you just wouldn’t tell us, right?

Ivan:
This one is not going to be on the- we are nowhere near the ocean on this one.

Priscilla:
Okay. Well, tell me what this topic is about- specific is terrific.

Ivan:
Specific is terrific. This is so important. If you are a BNI member, this is so important because if you are engaged in referral marketing, it’s very important to teach others how to refer you. So we are going to do an exercise. We are going to do something that I don’t think we have ever done before. Priscilla, I am going to do a live exercie. We are going to have dead air on a podcast for 15 seconds at a time.

Priscilla:
Okay.

Ivan:
Alright? Are you ready for that?

Priscilla:
I am.

Ivan:
If you are listening to this podcast, I want you to do this live if possible. If you are in a car, don’t do it, but if you are not, take out a piece of paper. I want you to be prepared to write some things down in these two very brief exercises, okay? Do you have a piece of paper, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
Yep, I do.

Ivan:
Alright. For the next 15 seconds, think of as many silly things that people have done in the world as you can.

[15 seconds] Okay, stop. How many did you get?

Priscilla:
I only got two, Ivan.

Ivan:
Okay. That’s fine. Alright. Now for the next part. Everyone, play along, In 15 seconds, think of as many silly things, if you have children, that your children have done. If you don’t have children, that other children have done or your grandchildren have done. So in 15 seconds, as many silly things as you can that children have done. Ready?
[03:49]

Priscilla:
Yeah, I am ready.

Ivan:
Begin.

[15 seconds]Stop. Okay, how many did you get?

Priscilla:
I got four things.

Ivan:
Okay. Next piece of the exercise. in 15 seconds, write down as many things that are white in color that you can think of. Begin.

[15 seconds] Stop. Okay, in 15 seonds, if you are playing along as you are listening to this, on that sheet of paper, I want you to write down as many things as you can think of that are white in color that you might find in a refrigerator. 15 seconds. Write down as many things as you can think of that are white in color that you might find in a regrigerator. Begin.

[15 seconds]On the first, how many things that were white in color could you think of?

Priscilla:
I got six things that were white in general.

Ivan:
And in the refrigerator?

Priscilla:
In the refrigerator, I got eight things.

Ivan:
Eight things. So in both exercises, you got more things when I was specific than when I was general. Find silly things that people have done in the world, you got two. Find silly things that your children have done, and you got four.

Then as many things as possible that are white, you got how many? Six?

Priscilla:
I think I got six, yeah.

Ivan:
Things that are white in color in your refrigerator you got eight. In both cases, you got more.

Now, most of you who are doing this exercise, you probably have a similar result, where the second piece of question you most likely got more responses than the first piece. The reason for that is that sort of honing down a question, lasering in on a question makes people think more specifically and it is easier to draw backwards.

So when you say think of silly things people have done, it is like where do I begin? It is too broad to start. If I ask you to find silly things that your kids have done, boom, that is easy. you think of several or a number of things your kids have done that are silly. In 15 seconds, how many things that are bright is pretty broad. But in your refrigerator, you are honing in your thought process.

Referrals are very much like that. You see that?

Priscilla:
Yeah. I totally see that and experienced that. It is true. Because the first question you asked me, I couldn’t think of very many at all of what is silly in the world. I just went blank, but you know, the more honed in you got, the better it was. That is interesting.

Ivan:
You know, you got the result that most people get. It is a lot easier to get the second half of the exercise and the reason is that you were asked to be even more specific each time. You had less trouble actually envisioning the things that you were asked to think of when they were more specific than when they were broad.

The principle transfers powerfully into referral marketing efforts. When you are actively developing a partnership with another business person, particularly in a group like BNI, to get referrals, you are much more likely to get referrals from them when you are laser-specific.

Have you ever tried to hit a target when you don’t know where it is or what it looks like? Can you imagine a bow and arrow target and covering the target and saying, “Take a shot and see where you hit?” It is pretty tough to do, but when you have a very specific target, it is much easier to hit that.

It is the same with referrals. If you say to someone, “I want to be referred to anyone who needs my service,” that is so generic it doesn’t stick in anybody’s brain. It is like a target that you can’t find. But if you say you want to be referred to Lewis Brown, the superintendent of the local school district, wow, you remember that. Lewis Brown, Dr. Brown. It is a very specific referral request, and even if you don’t know Dr. Brown, you are going to be thinking about that. You are going to be remembering that much easier than, “anyone who can use my service.”

Priscilla:
I just had an experience like this in my meeting this week. The organizer in our group wants to be referred to a home decorator. I immediately thought of somebody that she could call and I made that referral.

Ivan:
Whereas if she had just said, “anybody who is decorating” you might not have thought of that person.

Priscilla:
Or a decorator at all. I wouldn’t have thought of a decorator.

Ivan:
Right. Right. It is the same principle. I see decorators do it a lot and you know, they will talk about “anyone with a spine is who I am looking for” and it’s funny but it is not effective. I used to love it. I thought it was really cute. But it is just not effective.

Whereas, a chiropractor coming in and saying, ” I can help you build a strong immune system through chiropractic care,”- well now, immune system, that is really interesting. You know, people who are trying to enhance their immune system for some reason- It might be to stay away from cold or flu. It might be that they have some more serious health issue.

For example, when I had my really serious health issue, I was going to a chiropractor very regularly to help keep my body in alignment physically.

So specific is, in fact, terrific if you want to get referrals. It is counter-intuitive but here are three tips to help get the most out of your referral request:

When possible, ask to be referred to a specific person by name. If you want to be connected to a certain company, find out the name of a person there that you can begin connecting with.

Two, when explaining what you do to a referral partner, share one or two examples only. Avoid the temptation of giving a laundry list of your services. Be specific.

Three, give an example of a client whom you solved a problem with.

These are perfect for your weekly presentation, each week doing something like this. Give it a try. I promise you it will work. If you are listening to this podcast and you have had had an experience just like Priscilla did, tell us your experience right here on this podcast. I want to read them.

That is it for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Oh perfect, okay. Thank you so much, Ivan. That was great. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by NetworkingNow.com, which is the leading site on the net for networking downloadables. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Dr. Misner leads an exercise to demonstrate how important it is to be specific in referral marketing. You need to tell people precisely how to refer you.

title.rendered: Episode 536: What My Children Taught Me about Networking

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Today’s podcast topic comes from the second edition of Dr. Misner’s book Networking Like a Pro, which is now available on Amazon.

If you want to build a powerful personal network, it’s really important to connect and engage with your network via the social media platforms that they use rather than the ones that you use.

Dr. Misner learned this lesson when his children moved out. His oldest daughter only responds to text messages, not email or telephone. His second daughter only wanted to communicate via WhatsApp. His son didn’t respond to any of these channels, but spent a lot of time on Steam. In order to talk to his son, Dr. Misner downloaded Steam and purchased a game so he could talk to his son.

This applies to face-to-face networking opportunities as well as to online networking opportunities. You have to go where your connections are instead of expecting them to come to where you are.

If your children–or your clients–have taught you about how to communicate more effectively and what platforms to do it on, share your story in the comments.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 536 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic, Priscilla, and I have kind of a fun topic. You have actually heard this when I saw you recently in the Bay areas, I think I told this story. I really enjoy this topic. We chatted before we went on here on the podcast and you said you enjoyed it. I said you’ve got to laugh in all the same places.

Priscilla:
That’s easy to do, Ivan, because you are very entertaining.

Ivan:
Well, thanks. I appreciate that. This is actually some material from a new book that I have – well, a second edition of a book. Networking Like a Pro is now out in its second edition, and this is some of the new material that is in Networking Like a Pro, Second Edition.

So, you want to build a powerful professional network. It is really important to connect and engage with your network via the social media platforms that they use more than the ones you use. Now, this is particularly important if you need to develop a relationship with someone and you want to get to know them professionally. I am here to tell you that you have to go where they are, not where you are.

I learned this lesson in a really unusual way. A few years ago, my children all moved out and were on their own. One at a time, they eventually moved out over a few years. And it soon struck me that I was communicating regularly with them in completely different ways.

Now, my eldest, would not respond to emails ever. She would not even answer the phone when I called her. I discovered, however, that when I texted her, she responded immediately. So I even tested this once by calling her. No answer. Then I texted her. Immediate response. I mean, I shook my head. I was like, okay. Rather than try to get her to move over to my preferred platform, which is email, I figured that in order to keep a good line of communication open with her, I would mostly text her. It was great. We started talking a lot more by text.

Now, my second daughter wouldn’t use email, didn’t use the phone to actually talk – which I am pretty confident it was invented for. She wouldn’t consider texting. It was so old school. No, she communicated by What’s App. What’s App. I had no idea what What’s App was. I barely know how it works now.

But my wife, she pointed out that if I wanted to keep a line of communication open with her, I needed to download What’s App. So I did. I still have no idea really how it works other than to send texts to my daughter, whereby she almost immediately responds.

Now, my son- he was really the most interesting. He didn’t use email. He didn’t use the phone to talk. Wouldn’t consider texting and did not like What’s App. So, Priscilla, I’m thinking what am I to do? How am I going to connect with him? Then I realized that he is a big online gamer. And he was using a platform- I don’t know if you have ever heard of it. He was using a platform called Steam, which is available in the app store. You heard me talk about this in the Bay Area. Have you ever heard of Steam, Priscilla?

Priscilla:
No, I never have.

Ivan:
I had no idea what it was. It’s a gaming platform. So I discovered that Steam had an instant messaging feature on the program. So I actually downloaded Steam and purchased a game so that I could instant message my son. That’s right. I bought a game so I could talk to my son. As crazy as it sounds, it worked. If I called him, nothing. If I emailed him, nothing. If I messaged him on a social media platform, nothing. I would get no response. However, if I saw him online and messaged him, boom! I would get an instantaneous response. Success.

The bottom line here is if I wanted to communicate with my children, I needed to use their platforms, not mine. If it were up to me, I would pick up the phone and call or I would email them. But not them.

This has taught me a lesson in networking. If I wanted to stay connected to the people I meet, I need to go where they are, not stay where I am. This is a powerful message for BNI members here on BNIPodcast. It’s another lesson in networking, that it’s not about me. It is about them. This applies to face to face networking as well as online opportunities.

If building a powerful network is important to you, you have to go where your connections are and don’t expect them to always come to you. Priscilla, this is how my children thought me a lesson in networking.

Priscilla:
That’s great, Ivan. I have heard this before, but it is so true. I even know it in my own life because I have a daughter and she is probably similar in age to your kids and I have to text her. She doesn’t answer the phone or answer emails, either, so that’s kind of interesting.

Ivan:
You know, it’s frustrating. As frustrating as it is, it really applies generally in life but also in that as much as we would like people to be where we are, it doesn’t really matter, does it? If we are trying to building our business, we are trying to increase our sales, we want to build relationships. As I have always said, networking is more about farming than hunting. Then, we need to be in their territory, not our territory but where they are. That is where we have the opportunity to do business.

So the very fact that I realized that I need to communicate with my children in the way my children want to communicate- and they were all different.

Priscilla:
Yeah. That’s interesting.

Ivan:
It applies to networking. Don’t you agree?

Priscilla:
Yeah. I think it is really important to ask you clients how they want to be communicated with also because they have different needs and they may prefer texting over phoning over email or whatever. It’s important.

Ivan:
Good point, Priscilla. What I recommend you do is write that down in their contact information. You know, “prefer to be called directly.” “Prefer to email.” Whatever it is that they prefer, put that in there. And you remind me, I tell people when I give [them] my card and they want to reach out to me – I say, “You can call me but it is not easy to get through to me. So the best way to get to me is through email.”

So that is my preferred way. It may not be your preferred way. It may not be the person’s preferred way that I am talking to, but that’s my preferred way, and they are more likely to get a response than if they post something on my social media or they try to give me a call.

Well, look, I hope the lesson that my children taught me in networking can help you listening to this podcast and I would love to hear your story. If your kids or your clients have taught you about how to communicate more effectively and what platforms to be on, please share it right here on BNIPodcast.com. I really enjoy doing these podcasts. I love reading your messages. I read all the posts that go up there and will continue to do so.

Priscilla, thanks for everything that you do. We have a guy who never gets any credit. I don’t know if I have even- I think I have named him once or twice live – and that is James, who does all of the recording here on BNIPodcast. James, thank you for all the years of work that you have done on this podcast show as well. Thanks, everyone.

Priscilla:
His name is James Ward and he is a fabulous engineer.

Ivan:
Excellent. Thanks, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great, Ivan. Thanks you so much. Well, I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: If you want a powerful personal network, you have to go where your connections are instead of expecting them to come to where you are.

title.rendered: Episode 535: Stepping on Your Toes in the Margins

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Josh Perry, winner of the BNI Foundation video contest, joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about how it’s not always in your best interest to protect “your” industry spot too aggressively.

The people who “step on your toes” because they overlap with some areas of what you do can be your best referral partners and let you focus on what you love, for two reasons:

  1. Abundance. Givers Gain tells us there’s enough work for everyone. The more people who are doing similar things to what we’re doing, who are meeting similar people to those we work with, the better chance there’s going to be someone they meet who needs what we do.
  2. Focus. By having great referral partners you can turn to, you can help your clients solve problems on the fringes of what you do while still concentrating on the part of you work that you love most. Having these people in your chapter also helps you focus your weekly presentation and ask for the best possible referrals.

Don’t be a classification cowboy. Clear, open communication turns the members who “step on your toes” into your best referral partners.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 535 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hi Priscilla. I am back in Austin. I took part of the week off last week. I went to Galveston and had some vacation time last week. It was Thanksgiving here in the United States. It is good to be back home.

Priscilla:
Great.

Ivan:
I have a guest on today. His name is Josh Perry. Josh and I met about a month or so ago. Josh was the winner of the BNI Foundation video contest and his punishment for that was to be required to spend a day with my wife and me with his lovely wife, Kay. I joked a lot about the fact that we jammed so much into one day. It’s like I wouldn’t normally do all that in a week, but I wanted to make sure that we had lots of things to do. By the end of the day, Josh, I think you were probably pretty exhausted, but I will let you comment in just a second.

Let me introduce you to everybody. Josh is a consultant with Fusion Forge. He is a disciple of continuous improvement, an advocate for small business. He is a BNI member of just under two years. He is a native of Indiana. He has been a BNI member, a great BNI member. When we met, Josh told me about a couple of things that I thought was really interesting and would make a great podcast. So I asked Josh to be on.

Josh, before I throw your first talking point at you, how bad was it hanging out with me for a day?

Josh:
Good afternoon, Ivan. It was a great day. My wife and I thoroughly enjoyed it. If anybody out there is listening and has the chance to come and visit Austin, it is a beautiful city. Your hospitality couldn’t have been better. Thank you and the [BNI] Foundation for sponsoring the contest and allowing us to come and enjoy a week with you in a day.

Ivan:
A week in a day. I like the way you put that. It’s good. For anyone interested in the foundation, go to bnifoundation.org. The BNI Foundation does some amazing things.

We have an interesting topic. Josh, tell me about the problem and why we titled it what we did.

Josh:
Sure. Well, what I see now- I am serving on the membership committee as the Vice President of our chapter. I see a lot of members who very, very aggressively attempt to protect their “industry spot” in BNI. We really like to keep our territory safe and we will stake those fence posts out as far as we can to try to protect our best interest, which isn’t always in our best interest. Frankly, I have been as guilty when I was a new member as anybody else in my chapter that I have met.

Ivan:
You know, I was in Miami last month and I was talking to a chapter and there were three attorneys in the chapter. I said, “Wow. I am really impressed that there are multiple attorneys. That is a fantastic thing to do. I visited a region and they have seven attorneys in a chapter.”

They looked at me and were like, yeah, so? Seven attorneys in one chapter. “We have seven attorneys in our chapter.” No. Seriously? And I met all seven of the attorneys from a Miami chapter. So I think this is what we are talking about. So what is your opinion on this? What is your expert position, having been in this situation?

Josh:
Having been in the situation, I will be the first to admit it is scary when someone walks in your chapter who does something very similar to what you do or even steps on your toes a little bit and does some of the exact same activities that you do.

When I was just starting out my consulting business, my background is pretty heavy in accounting. The first couple of client were folks that I did accounting-type work for. And one day, the same person that brought me to the chapter brought a bookkeeper as a visitor. That was intimidating. What impact was that going to have on my business if I let somebody come in who represented the same profession that many, 30-35%, of my current workload came from?

The same situation happened a little bit down the road when somebody came in that did some social media and web design work – a portion of what I do on the fringes. Just like any other member would be, I was a little bit nervous about it, but I just remembered the philosophy of Givers Gain. I remembered the philosophy that BNI is all about. I had a chance to meet with those folks, do a dance card, have a great 1-2-1, learn about who they are, what they do. They both obviously applied to be in BNI because why would you not? They both became members of our chapter and are some of the best referral partners that I have.

Ivan:
I think people who haven’t experienced it are shaking their heads going, yeah right. But you have to see it operate. For both the chapters, the one in Manhattan years ago, and the one I was just at in Miami, the attorneys all said, “This is awesome because do I all areas of the law? Yeah. Do I hate doing or am I not excited about doing some areas of the law? Yeah. I am excited about ____(whatever).”

There is the family law attorney who is doing divorces and I’m going how could you love that? But they do. They love it. That is what they want to do. They don’t want to do the business law and the contracts. So if you can get the focus on what you love and let other people do what you are doing just to make a living. It is just a win-win-win. I think that is what you are describing. Yeah?

Josh:
Yeah. Absolutely. There are two points that really speak out to me about why it works. One, of course, is the concept of Givers Gain. There is the concept of abundance. There is not a limited amount of work out there that we can get. There are tremendous amounts of work that any individual in the industry can try to capture in any of the markets that we are doing business. The more people we can have who are doing similar things to what we are doing, that are meeting similar people to the type that we need to work with, the better chance that there is going to be someone that they meet who is going to need specifically what you do.

The second point comes back to what you mentioned on focus. Those were areas of my business, both bookkeeping work and web design work, where I was able to make a little bit of margin. But by having great referral partners that I could turn to, both of them numerous times at this point just since we have been members of the group for almost two years- that allows me to focus on places where I can add greater value to my clients but still have a constant referral source, a constant person where if I have a client that may need to work with this person, I help them solve a problem and I have helped a person in my group also.

Ivan:
So one of the things that you said to me was, “Don’t be nervous to allow visitors and prospective members to step on your toes.” Tell me a little bit about that because that is the theme for this podcast. Tell me a little bit more about that.

Josh:
Absolutely. I just think about dancing when I think about that. You are going to go out and you are going to dance. Then once in a while, your partner is going to step on your feet. If it’s your significant other, or somebody you like, you are okay with that, right? You are not going to kick them out of the room because they get a little bit close to what you do.

It’s the same way with our best referral partners. There are going to be times where somebody in that group is going to be real close to what you do, but I just can’t highlight enough that for every one referral I may get from the Realtor in our group, or one referral I may get from the mortgage originator in our group, that other person who is in that B-to-B world that is working the same size, same type of customers that I work with, I will do business with one of them ten times over.

You get your toes stepped on every once in a while and you deal with it.

Ivan:
Yeah, and clear, open and honest communication can deal with that. I heard the exact same words from the attorneys that I recently met in Miami. So why do you think this works, Josh?

Josh:
One of the big reasons I think it works is it makes you better at doing your [weekly presentation] commercial on the day of your meeting. You have to focus in on what you do. You have to focus in on what you do that is different and what you do that is similar. By having that open and clear communication with your referral partner, knowing what their purview is and what your area of focus is, it helps you be a better presenter to give a better commercial and helps you be more focused so that the other members of your chapter know who to refer to you.

Instead of having, like with the attorneys, “Someone who needs an attorney should contact me,” that may not stick out. I had the pleasure of meeting a business man two weeks ago that his focus is on small business law. I have never referred any work to an attorney in my chapter before, but I had a 1-2-1 with this gentleman who focuses on small business law, which is exactly who my clients are. I have already referred two or three people to him. If I can get as pointed in who I am looking for as that attorney was, that is going to pay the dividends for me and my business.

Ivan:
It really is a matter of abundant thinking versus scarcity. Yeah?

Josh:
You got it. Absolutely.

Ivan:
Listen, anything that you want to add, Josh, before we wrap up?

Josh:
I would just say that the people that I have had the chance to meet through BNI – new members coming in, leadership team members, executive directors, the team at the Foundation, you Ivan, and everyone else that I have had the chance to meet with are the type of people that I have enjoyed doing business with. It’s folks who are setting about doing their work the right way, serving the people that they look at as clients and partners and are well beyond the transactional business focus that you see in the news or you read about in the newspapers. I feel honored and blessed to have a chance to be in the BNI chapter for two years now, and I am looking to several more years of success with my very close referral partners and friends.

Ivan:
Well, Josh, it is a real pleasure to have members like you, and I am always excited to bring on a BNI member on to the BNI podcast. My wife, Beth, and I really enjoyed spending the afternoon, the day, with you and your wife, Kay. I hope our paths cross again many times, Josh. Thank you so much.

For everyone listening to this podcast, there are some other podcasts that are related that you might want to look at. Do a search on BNI Podcast for “classification cowboys”. It is not exactly the same but related to what Josh has been talking about here. But do a search on “classification cowboys” and I think that will help provide some more context to what we are talking about.

Josh, please say hi to your wife, Kay, and thanks so much for being on my podcast today.

Josh:
I will do that. Thank you so much for having me.

Ivan:
Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, thank you both. That was some wonderful information. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

excerpt.rendered: Josh Perry joins Dr. Misner to talk about how the people in your BNI chapter who “step on your toes” can be your best referral sources.

title.rendered: Episode 534: I’m Too Busy for BNI

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tiffanie Kellog joins Dr. Misner this week to talk about why you’re never too busy for BNI. Here are three ways you can maximize your membership and get better results from the time you spend networking.

  1. Get better referrals. When your referrals are ready to do business right away, it saves a lot of time.
  2. Prepare your weekly presentations in advance to help your fellow members give you better referrals.
  3. Schedule your one-to-ones efficiently. Make appointments either right after or right before your BNI meeting, or stack them at a single location to save travel time.
  4. Learn while driving. Listen to training CDs or the Networking for Success videos.

You can find more ways to maximize your BNI membership on Tiffanie’s website.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 534 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
Hey, Priscilla. I just got back recently from the International Convention in Long Beach. It was the last convention in the US for a few years because we are now- 60% of the organization is global. We are going to hold these international conventions all around the world.

Priscilla:
That is a great idea.

Ivan:
It was awesome. There were over 1000 people. It is like a United Nations meeting. If you ever hear of a conference or a convention for BNI in your area, you have to go. I promise you, you have to go. First, I would love to meet you. Second, you are going to be blown away.

So we should jump into the topic. I have here a guest. Her name is Tiffanie. That name may sound familiar. Tiffanie Kellog. Tiffanie has been a member since 2005. She is a top Asentiv trainer. Priscilla, you mentioned Networking for Success, and I think the phrase you used was “top networking experts”. Well the truth is there are about three or four other people who I have said, “Yeah, you are a top networking expert. You get to go on the site.” One of them is our very own Tiffanie Kellog. I think, Tiffanie, you have been on what, 25 of these podcasts now?

Tiffanie:
I don’t think that many yet. I think it is only six or so.

Ivan:
You have been on more than any other person. It is great to have you back. The topic of your podcast is , oh my goodness, did you say, “I Don’t Have Time for BNI”? Talk to me about that.

Tiffanie:
It is something I hear BNI members say all of the time. How about doing a 1-2-1? “I am so busy.” How about doing this training? “I am so busy.” I just remember all the time talking about “I Am Too Busy for BNI.”

Really, if we are too busy for BNI, does that mean we are too busy for referrals?

Ivan:
Yeah. That is exactly right. It is crazy when I hear people say, “I am so busy that I need to step back.” It’s called hiring people. Have you ever heard this commercial? I know we have a lot of material to cover. Have you heard this commercial? I think it was for the telephone. I will really date myself. It was for the Yellow Pages. It was called Tom’s Desk. Tom had one desk in his retail shop. He sold desks. He wouldn’t sell his desk because if he sold his desk, he wouldn’t have any more.

It was all about failing your business. You have to have an inventory. You do something crazy hire people. You bring in more resources. That is the way that you build your business, right?

Tiffanie:
Definitely,. My intention is I wanted to share a couple of tips that I found useful in my seven years being a BNI member to kind of maximize my membership because I think that we get so much out of BNI as far as out word of mouth marketing within our company that we are going to have to spend and invest our time in it in we are going to get more from it.

Ivan:
First of all are different shades of a referral. Do you want to talk a moment about those?

Tiffanie:
Yes, so one of the ways that I think you can maximize your time in BNI is if you can get better referrals. In Asentiv, it might be business or certified network referral program depending on where you are in the world. We actually talk about that there are ten shades of a referral. Shade one is like you get a name and a number and a shade ten is a closed deal.

Most BNI members are familiar with a six, which is hey, give me permission to call. But if somebody can set an appointment on your behalf or even connect you face to face or via email, then you would actually spend less time working the process and be able to close the deal sooner. So if you can get a better referral, it will actually save you time.

Ivan:
No question about it and my guess is the next couple of tip that you have will help you increase the quality of the shades of the referral. The next one you have is preparing your weekly presentations in advance. Do you want to talk about that?

Tiffanie:
Yeah. So a lot of these BNI members worry, “What am I going to say this week? What am I going to say this week?” It is always this kind of last minute thing so it is never as well prepared as it could be. I actually recommend that you create your weekly presentations in chunks, be it four or ten. You just sit down and you create them all at once. I also will say do a theme. It makes it easier for us to determine what are we going to talk about at that time. Plus it helps create some continuity with your members and they are able to keep that idea in mind. So i recommend write them in advance and then you have them to practice befor your meting. So that’s it.

Ivan:
You know, writing them in advance I have been talking about for a long time, but doing them all at once- I have always thought about it, write one at a time. but doing three or four, doing a theme or a series that ties together- brilliant. I love that idea.

If you are listening to this, write this on a rock. This is a really great idea. Do a theme. You know, maybe take whatever business you are in- some particular product or service has a series of elements to that product or service. So write out a whole series and then each week cover it over the next several weeks. Great, great suggestion, Tiffanie.

So I think your next one is 1-2-1. Do you want to talk about that?

Tiffanie:
Yes. The 1-2-1 meetings are, to me, so important within BNI because it is where you really get to know your fellow members and you can get into what is my action plan and what am I going to do for you. However, oftentimes, people will spend a lot of time traveling to or from the 1-2-1s and waiting. So my recommendation is maximize your time.

First off, there is no reason that you can’t meet after your BNI meeting or if you are an early bird like me, maybe even before your BNI meeting. You could do a 1-2-1. You are going to be there in the location. Meet at the coffee shop around the corner.

Ivan:
Great idea. You are a glutton for punishment to do it before a BNI meeting, but I am proud of you. That is awesome.

Tiffanie:
Sometimes, I will hear people say, “I really need to be out right after the meeting.” But I am up and with the guys, so 6:00 is no big deal. But not everybody is up as early.

Ivan:
No, but you have to take these ideas that we give, and Tiffanie has given some great ideas here. You have to takes these ideas and apply them to your schedule, your process in doing business. So whether it is before or after, the point is still the same. Do your 1-2-1s sometime around your meetings. IT is a great idea.

Tiffanie:
And then the other idea I have for 1-2-1s is I like to do what I call stack 1-2-1s. I will pick a location and I will be there for two, three, four back to back 1-2-1s. I am only driving there and back and I don’t have a lot of time waiting. I am able to do several 1-2-1s at one time in one spot. one location.

Ivan:
You know, that is a fantastic idea, and it keeps the 1-2-1a pretty tight. If you have somebody lining up behind the nextperson, you are not going to get really sidetracked. What a great idea. You have some really great ideas that I haven’t heard before, Tiffanie. I love them

Priscilla:
Can I ask a question?

Ivan:
YEah, sure, Priscilla. Go ahead.

Priscilla:
Have you ever done 1-2-1s on the phone?

Tiffanie:
I typically, if I am in the same general area, I always want to meet face to face. But if it is a 1-2-1 with somebody across the counrty or across the globe- for example maybe a Director Consultant or Asentiv Facilitator, then I will do it via Skype so I can see them face to face. But if I can do it in a local location, I almost always try to make sure that we are face to face.

Ivan:
It is funny that you mention that Priscilla, because I just had somebody ask me yesterday does a Skype 1-2-1 count? First of all, I said talk to your chapter, talk to your Executive Director because that sounds like a loaded question that you have already been given an answer to and you are coming to me. But in my opinion, whether it counts or not is secondary to whether it is as good or not. And I think a Skype 1-2-1 is not as good as a 1-2-1 in person. And certainly, over the phone is even less effective. So I am inclined to agree with you.

Alright, we have time for one more and that is learning while driving. Do you want to talk about that?

Tiffanie:
Sometimes I spend a little bit of time in my car and I know fellow BNI members do, and I think it is a great opportunity for us to learn more toearn more while we are sitting in our vehicles. Whether it is a BNI podcast to gt the infromation- I have figured out that I can put things on YourTube to listen to them but not watch them while I am driving so I can listen to the Networking for Success channel while driving. Or even some of the CDs produced by Del Fuego. I have those in that car ready to go and I can learn while I am in the vehichle. So I am maximizing my time and I can get my CEU’s.

Ivan:
Awesome. Great idea. Del Fuego now has all of their CDs on digital audio so you can actually download them. So just connect with Del Fuego for those audios. I was just looking at their site today. You can download their things. It is fantastic content.

Tiffanie, we have to wrap up. We are out of time. “I don’t have enough time for BNI.” People who say that aren’t focusing on having higher quality referrals so they spend less time. They are not doing a good job of nailing their weekly presentation. They are not doing 1-2-1s as often, and they are not learning while they are driving and walking. I think those are great suggestions. Anything you want to add to wrap up?

Tiffanie:
I think there are some good, solid, easily implementable tips.

Ivan:
Well, fantastic. If people want to learn more about you, what is your website?

Tiffanie:
So if you go to TiffanieKellog.com/more, there is a lot of information there they can take a look at.

Ivan:
Fantastic. And you can also go to YoutTube and look for the YouTube channel Networking for Success and you will see Tiffanie on a bunch of videos. There are only a handful of people who I have accepted to do videos with me on that channel, and Tiffanie is one of them. Tiffanie, thank you so much again for such fantastic information. You are an amazing trainer and a fantastic member for BNI. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. Thank you both. I think that was some great information. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of the Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

excerpt.rendered: Think you’re too busy for BNI? Tiffanie Kellog shares four ways you can maximimze your BNI membership.

title.rendered: Episode 533: There’s No Substitute for a Good Substitute (Classic Podcast)

content.rendered:
This is a rebroadcast of Episode 386.

Synopsis

Steve Tannuzzo joins Dr. Misner this week for what they expect will be one of the most unpopular podcasts in BNI history.

It’s important to understand the appropriate use of the substitute program in BNI. Massachusetts BNI piloted a substitute program in 4 chapters, two very successful, two not so successful. All four chapters and their revenue grew. One chapter went from 18 members to 50 and $200,000 to more than $1 million in revenue.

The core of the program is that substitutes do not speak about their own businesses.It was never Dr. Misner’s intent for substitutes to be able to give their own 60-second presentations.

When the same substitute attends frequently and talks about his or her own business, it’s good for the individual, but not for the chapter.

Good substitutes are valuable to the chapter by giving great presentations about the person they’re representing. Substitutes are not there for themselves: they’re there for you.

Don’t accept mediocrity when excellence is an option. Try this in your own chapter.

Brought to you by The Global Networking Show.

Complete Transcript of BNI Podcast Episode 386 –

Priscilla:
Hello everyone and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast brought to you by the GlobalNetworkingShow.com, which is hosted by Andy Lopata and Dr. Ivan Misner. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, CA. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and Chairman of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello Ivan. How are you today?

Ivan:
I am doing great and I have a guest here on the show, Steve Tannuzzo, who is a freelance copywriter with a focus on website content, newsletters and advertising. He joined BNI in 2002, so Steve has been a member for 12 years and over the course of his membership was able to fulfill his dream of starting his own business in copywriting. He has been on the BNI Massachusetts team since 2003 and currently serves as an Area Director for over 1,000 members in more than 37 chapters.

Steve, thank you so much for being on the podcast today. We really appreciate you being on.

Steve:
Thank you, Ivan. My pleasure.

Ivan:
Listen, we are about to do what I think would be one of the more unpopular podcasts in BNI podcast history. How do you feel about that?

Steve:
I am very excited about it because while it is unpopular, I think by the time this podcast is over, we will change some minds.

Ivan:
You know, I couldn’t agree with you more. I think it might end up being a little bit unpopular at first, but the truth is what we are going to talk about will have a more significant impact on chapters than the overwhelming of the 380 podcasts that I have done so far because I think this is really, really important.

I created the substitute program because I thought it was really important to have an opportunity for people to be able to bring someone else in when they have a problem or some meeting that they absolutely have to do. I will be the first person to say that it has been extraordinarily abused in BNI. I think that as important as substitutes are to a membership, I think it is also important to understand the effective use of the substitute.

And there are some real positive uses of the substitutes, one of which is bringing in fresh faces rather than the same old person coming in again. So talk to us a little bit about some of the appropriate use of the substitute program in BNI.:

Steve:
Sure. We believe that when you choose the right substitute, you have a potential member but not always. In fact, most of the subs that we get don’t turn into members. Once in a while, they do, but we do find that when they come in they are potential clients for our members. So it is important to carefully choose who you are going to use as your substitute and to train them about the meeting, what to expect.

One of the things that you are not going to say is, “You are going to talk about your business.” By definition of substitute, you are substituting for the member that you are going for. But there are a lot of benefits to attending the meeting anyway- participating in open networking, getting to know everybody, watching the meeting in progress and seeing all of these great professionals. Again, taking part in post-meeting networking. It kind of adds a lot of value to a member who comes in- not a member, a substitute who comes in and sees the meeting and really has the opportunity to take part in a really great meeting.

Ivan:
Yeah, I think that is really important. So you are from the Massachusetts area, and you have been having a very specific substitute program in Massachusetts which I think has worked out really well. Do you want to talk a little bit about what you are doing there and how that has worked?

Steve:
Absolutely. We started four years ago. I brought this idea to Patti Salvucci, which is our Executive Director.

Ivan:
She has also been on the podcast a number of times.

Steve:
Absolutely. She is excellent. She said why not pilot this idea. The idea being simply an idea that has been around BNI for a while, which is you are wearing one of three hats. You are either a member, a substitute or a visitor. If you happen to be a substitute, you are not going to give your weekly presentation because you are not a member.

We piloted it in four chapters. We tried my own chapter, which was about 40 or so members and we were doing about 1.4 million dollars at the time. Pretty successful chapter. We also did a couple of chapters that were not in such great shape. Under 20 members, under $300,000 in revenue for the year. Not very good.

What we found was that all the chapters that participated, all four chapters grew and revenue grew. Some grew dramatically. One chapter was down to about 18 members and was doing about $200,000. Now, it is over 50 members and more than $1 million. And they have been the Founder’s Award winner. It has been fantastic.

You know, of course, it is not just the subs for the reason that this chapter grew, but what it did was it put a standard in place that the other chapters weren’t doing. I think the other chapters rose around it, and these chapters did really well.

But it is true that some of the substitutes came in and said hey, they want to be a part of that. By holding back and not allowing them to do their weekly presentations as a visitor, they filled out an application and came in.

Ivan:
Let’s talk about that a little bit because one of the things that I see is that a lot of the chapters have substitutes who basically come in and they are visitors. They do their 60 second presentation and I have to tell you that was never my intent with the substitute program ever. The idea was that there would be s sub who was representing your profession. So if I am there for you, what a crazy idea- I am there to actually talk about you.

Chapters ball. They just give in on this issue and they say hey, you know what, they really need to market themselves and talk about themselves instead of talking about themselves when they are there to represent you.

Talk to me a little bit about what you are doing with the sub program there.

Steve:
What we found is those people who were coming in and essentially getting a free membership because some people were abusing this practice so much that they had the same people coming in every week and they essentially were members and newer members had no idea that these people were just substitutes. They weren’t actual members. They weren’t paying any dues. They weren’t following any rules. They didn’t have to- they were just getting this, you know, free ride.

Ivan:
Hang on to that thought for just a second. Don’t leave it. Let’s talk about this for a second because we are basically saying that somebody comes in and it is good for the individual and works for them but it doesn’t work for the chapter because they are not building up the chapter. They are not supporting or helping the chapter. So it is good for one person, but not particularly good for everyone.

I think chapters really forget about that. They think, oh my goodness, I can’t make it next week so I am going to bring in somebody and let them talk about themselves. It’s helpful for them but it is really not good for the whole. Would you agree with that?

Steve:
Absolutely. Especially since what we find is we will have this person who comes in and speaks about his business even though he is not a member and that seat never gets filled because they think they filled the seat.

There are so many negatives to it. If your chapter has been doing- I know there are a lot of chapters that unfortunately do this, but if you give them an opportunity- that is what we said to a lot of our members who had resistance to this. We said just give it a shot. Try it out and see how it goes.

Now we have at least 80% of our chapters following this substitute practice, and it is working beautifully.

Ivan:
Well, I couldn’t agree more that you have to have the sub actually represent the person who they are supposed to be substituting for. That was what the program was created for. It was not created as a way to sort of establish another visitor. I think that oftentimes, people like water, tend to seek the path of least resistance. They do what is easiest and not necessarily what is best for the chapter or even for themselves. They assume that having someone to represent them solves the immediate problem, but it really doesn’t focus on the long term issue.

The long term issue is the health of the group. To have the health of the group, you want the person to really represent the individual.

And let me tell you, we talked about the potential problem with subs, but there have been some great subs that I have seen in people who have come in. I actually went to a chapter where the sub came in and did a better 60 second presentation than the individual. Here is why: they came in and said here is why you want to do business with my friend- with my associate, with the person who has provided my service. They are so good at what they do. It was a third party talking about the member instead of the member talking about the member.

It’s just like what we do with referrals. With referrals, it’s about somebody else talking about you. When you have a sub there who really hits the nail on the head and talks about you and how good you are, that is almost better than you talking about you. Have you experienced that at all with your groups?

Steve:
Yeah, absolutely. We had a member, my friend Debbie who has been in the chapter for almost 20 years, a real estate agent who never misses a meeting. She had her boss who runs the real estate company come in as a substitute one week and she was blown away by the meeting. People, you know, great professionals. She was so impressed that after the meeting, she wrote an email to Debbie that said [she] loved the meeting so much and [she] want[s] to do business with the following people. She named some people in the real estate sphere and would up passing about $15,000 worth of business. Just by being a substitute, which wouldn’t have happened had she not asked her.

Ivan:
Yeah.

Steve:
Choosing your substitutes wisely is very important and creating your own personal substitute list so that when the time comes, if have a vacation or something in advance that you can plan and send that substitute in for- that is where the value comes in.

Ivan:
Sometimes chapters do this sort of chapter sub, which becomes almost sort of a professional sub.

Steve:
Oh yeah. A sub to the group. That has got to go.

Ivan:
Is that a problem in real life?

Steve:
It is a big problem because we see that a lot of times, where a list is passed around the group that says that these are the people who are willing to substitute for you. But those are not the people that you want because those are the people who have been through BNI and are really there for themselves and not for you.

Ivan:
They are there for themselves. They are not there to promote you. The whole concept from the very beginning- it is so important for members to understand this, that the whole concept from the very beginning was that the sub was there to represent you, not just to fill a void. So if you are just trying to fill a void you are missing the point of the whole substitute program. It has to be somebody who is there to represent you and support you. That tends to work well. Would you not agree, Steve?

Steve:
Absolutely. If you are choosing the right substitute who is going to give some value to the meeting, they are not going to care about talking about their own business because- and this is an interesting thing: members care more about substitute speaking than the substitutes do. If you remember back to your first time that you visited a chapter, especially a larger chapter with all that buzz going on and you are taking in all this stuff, they are happy not to talk about their business. They are there just to substitute for you but the members sometimes feel like we have got to let them speak, but you know what, it’s fine. They will happily sub for someone and get some value out of the meeting anyway.

Ivan:
You know, if you expect the best, you tend to get it. If you expect less than the best, you tend to get it. I couldn’t agree more. You have to have high expectations and invite people in to support you. That is what makes the most successful chapters, and that is certainly what you have seen in Massachusetts. You have great groups there in Massachusetts.

Any last thought before we wrap up today, Steve?

Steve:
Well, to chapters that want to try this- that are using that substitute list that has gone around and see the same old faces all the time, where members from other chapters sub for them- and trying to implement this so that your chapter is going to grow and make more money, I think it just starts with a few people trying it out.

I know the resistance that you get initially comes from members saying that it is going to be really hard to get substitutes. But you know what, if you put a little effort into it, it is not so bad and the return on the investment of your time can be worth so much money to the people of your chapter.

Ivan:
You are absolutely right. I think that one of the things that Patti, your Executive Director there, has said, loved to talk about and loved to share is this idea that why accept mediocrity when excellence is an option? Excellence is an option with a BNI chapter with the substitute program, but you have to use substitutes in the proper way and not use them as a crutch. Instead, use them as a benefit to what you have to offer. Bringing in people to come in and talk about you effectively when you are not able to be there really makes a difference, I think, in the program.

I believe it is all about creating excellence, and that is how you get chapters who double or triple their number of referrals that they pass in a year just like you described today.

Steve:
Absolutely.

Ivan:
We are out of time today, Steve. Thank you so much for talking about his subject. I think this will be one of those interesting podcasts where people will be very frustrated or very happy that we had this conversation because the truth is that what we just talked about will make a really, really positive difference in a lot of chapters who want to make their group more successful.

I appreciate you being on the podcast.

Steve:
Thank you. I had a great time.

Ivan:
Thanks, Steve. Back to you, Priscilla.

Priscilla:
Okay, great. I think that is just about it for this week. I would just like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by the GlobalNetworkingShow.com, which is hosted by Andy Lopata and Dr. Ivan Misner. Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we hope you will join us next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

ShareTweetLinkedinWhatsappSaveSendFlipboard

title.rendered: Episode 532: Why You Might Not Be Getting Referrals

content.rendered:
Synopsis

Tiffanie Kellog joins Dr. Misner this week about why you might not be getting referrals in BNI. One of the big reasons you’re not getting referrals is because you aren’t doing good weekly and featured presentations. If you’re doing any of the following things, it’s time to adopt better techniques.

Weekly Presentation Mistakes

  • Reading your presentation. It’s better to practice than to read, and better to have a few bullet points to remind you what you want to say than to read your whole presentation.
  • Being vague. Remember, “specific is terrific,” especially if you only have 30 seconds. Focus on one product, or service. Never say “anyone” or “everyone.”
  • Giving up your weekly presentation when you do a featured presentation. Your weekly presentation can be a great introduction to your featured presentation.

Featured Presentation Mistakes

  • Focusing on your history instead of why you do what you do and how you help your clients. Remember the goal is to drive referrals.
  • Not making an emotionally charged connection. People won’t do business with you if they don’t know, like, and trust you.
  • Staring at the screen. Reading your slides will send your audience to sleep. Use images and a few bullet points in your slides.
  • Opening up for Q&A. This can take your presentation far off track. Instead, invite people who have questions to schedule a one-to-one with you or ask them to write it on their business card so you can answer later.
  • Saying “I haven’t had time to prepare.” You’ve had six weeks. Saying this makes you look unreliable.
  • Spewing tons of details about your business (verbal vomit). People won’t remember it all anyway. Share a couple of points people can hang onto.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 532 –

Priscilla:
Hello everybody and welcome back to the Official BNI Podcast, brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube, which features Dr. Ivan Miser and many other networking experts. I am Priscilla Rice, and I am coming to you from Live Oak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I am joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan, how are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am doing fantastic and I am at the BNI International Convention in Long Beach. It is the last time that we are going to hold it in Long Beach for many, many years. It is always awesome. I feel like I am at a United Nations meeting because there are flags from all over the world. It is awesome. Talking about awesome, I have an awesome guest this week: Tiffanie Kellog.

If that name sounds at all familiar, Tiffanie and I figured out that she has been on the BNI Podcast more than any other guest that I have brought on to BNIPodcast.com, so if you are listening to this, that should tell you something about the quality of her content. She has been a BNI member since 2005. She is one of the top trainers in the world for Asentiv. It is awesome to have Teffany on today’s podcast. Welcome, Tiffanie.

Tiffanie:
Thank you, Ivan. I am glad to be back.

Ivan:
You have a great title for this podcast. Repeat the title for us.

Tiffanie:
Why you might not be getting referrals in BNI.

Ivan:
Okay. Now, I want you to hear that title because what we are going to actually talk about are your weekly presentation and your featured presentation. When we just about started this recording, I said, “Let’s talk about weekly and feature presentations,” but you had a much better title, Why You Are Not Getting Referrals. And one of the reasons you don’t get referrals is that you don’t do good weekly presentations and your featured presentation. So let’s jump into that if we can.

One of the first things that you have on doing the weekly presentation is reading off your phone. Talk to me about that.

Tiffanie:
Yeah. It could be an age thing and it may be the younger people are better off the phone, but there is just something distracting to me seeing people scrolling up and down on their phone when they are talking. Really, if you think about it, most in most regions in BNI, you only have 30-60 seconds to talk about your business as it is. If we can’t speak passionately and powerfully about our business for 30-60 seconds and we have to read instead, I think that a can be detrimental to our referrability if people don’t think that we can talk about ourselves.

Ivan:
I couldn’t agree more, and listen, at the same time, Tiffanie, I plan my spontaneity. I write almost everything down. I tend not to read it verbatim, but if you are one of those people like me, make a few bullet points so you are not reading your presentation- like me, but you can speak extemporaneously.

I get it when you want to prepare, but Tiffanie’s point is really important. Don’t read it, have some bullet points.

Priscilla, you saw me a few weeks ago do a presentation in your region. My presentation was bullet points on the back of my business card. That was my presentation. I just glanced down at my business card and I was ready to go.

Tiffanie:
Ivan, you talk quite frequently about practicing. I think if we practice our presentation four to six times, then we should be able to deliver it with notes but without reading it.

Ivan:
Exactly. Practice, practice, practice. It’s very, very important. And here is another one that I think is awesome. It is being vague. If you want to start getting more referrals, don’t be vague. Talk about that one.

Tiffanie:
So we talked at BNIWCS in central Florida about being specific to be terrific. I think weekly presentations are an interesting thing because some people try to be too vague and some people try to cram too much in. If you think about the 30-60 seconds you have, it is much more powerful to talk about one single product or one service. Share one success story of how you helped.

You know the old saying, how do you eat an elephant? One bite at a time. I think the weekly presentations are an awesome opportunity for us to teach our fellow members one thing about our business one week at a time.

Ivan:
I agree, and you have words that you should never, ever use. Do you want to name those?

Tiffanie:
Included in my words not to use in BNI are anyone, someone, everyone. It doesn’t put into mind people.

Ivan:
That is exactly it. Specific is terrific when you are talking about your weekly presentation. The more laser specific you can be- and this is totally counterintuitive, complete counterintuitive for most people because they think they are being general. “I could use anyone who needs this service. Refer me to everyone who does this.” It just doesn’t work as well. If you get laser specific, people are much more likely to refer you.

Okay, so that is some advice one. Doing the weekly presentation.

Tiffanie:
Can I do one more on the weekly presentation?

Ivan:
Yes, absolutely.

Tiffanie:
Make sure that you don’t give up your weekly presentation just because you are doing your featured presentation.

Ivan:
Oh, good point. Do you see people do that?

Tiffanie:
I had seen less and less of it, but I saw it this last month. Apparently, it is still happening.

Ivan:
Yeah. I think having 30 seconds or a minute is a great lay up. “You know, later, I am going to be doing the featured presentation. I am going to be talking about this.” Or whatever. It is a no-brainier.

Okay, back to featured presentation. I only have a few minutes, so give two or three suggestions you have on doing a great feature presentation.

Tiffanie:
I think it is really important to remember that the goal of your featured presentation is to be able to help create referrals. Too often I will see members who will, you know, spend five minutes of their presentation with their 28 year history or their whole life story. Really, our goal and our intention is how do we create referrals? I think it is really important to focus on why you do what you do – what drives you- and how you help your clients versus a lot of personal. You can save that for the one to one.

Ivan:
Yeah, I have seen people do their why in a couple of minutes and it is why they do what they do, and there might be a little. Historical element to that. You are not talking about that. You are really talking about somebody going the our their whole history. “I went to high school at this place and college-”

Tiffanie:
Essentially, talk about what drives you. You can call that your emotionally charged connection. To me, that is what drives you and that is different than the paths that you took to get there. You got fired from this job. Or you got hired here. Things like that.

Ivan:
Yeah. So what is the number one thing that you think people should do in their featured presentation?

Tiffanie:
Anytime you are at a speaking engagement, a presentation, a 1-2-1, especially at a BNI chapter, I think your number one goal is to compel people to like you because they are not going to refer you if they don’t know they can trust you. An emotionally charged connection is certainly one of the keys to help people connect with who you are and what you do.

Ivan:
And I think it is not, you know, a friendship kind of contest. It is the second part of that which I think is important: know, like and trust. So I think what you may be saying here, Tiffanie, is that by getting people to that point where they like you, that is what leads to trusting you.

And sometimes they spend too much time on what, Tiffanie?

Tiffanie:
On the personal stuff?

Ivan:
You said they spend too much time on other-

Teffanie:
On other stuff. It’s that background stuff, yeah. My dogs, my cats…

Ivan:
There should be a technical term of other stuff. I just want to bring up an important one. Don’t be staring at the screen the whole time. Talk about that.

Tiffanie:
Yeah, so the screen, if you use PowerPoint, which you do not have to in BNI – if you are using it, it should go along with your words, not reading from it. Just like the weekly presentation, you don’t want to be reading your entire feature presentation. Notes are fine but don’t read it off there.

Ivan:
If you are going to use PowerPoint, and that is completely up to you- I agree with you, Tiffanie, it is not mandatory. If you are going to use it, I recommend a really thin book. It is an easy read. It is called Presentation Zen. Awesome book. I will give you the Reader’s Digest version: don’t put a lot of written verbiage. Instead, use images. I do my PowerPoint as a character in my presentation. It’s not the content. It is a character.

For example, when I did the presentation on Santa Claus, Easter Bunny and Six Degrees of Separation, instead of having that typed out, what do these all have in common, I had the images of Santa Claus, images of the Easter Bunny and images of six degrees of connection.

It has to be a character in your presentation so graphic images are what you mostly want.

Any other last thoughts, Tiffanie?

Tiffanie:
Quickly, on the feature presentation, I recommend never opening it with with Q and A because that has too much of a tendency to go off point. So I might in my presentation say if you have any questions, please see me afterwards to schedule a 1-2-1.
